name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads dynamic content for families, surfaces key actions (scheduling, finding therapists and clinics), and links into services, groups, resources, pricing, planner, and donation pages.

core_features: Load homepage content via getHomePageContent on page initialization, including primary text, primary actions, and featured services, groups, resources, and donation campaigns.
Present primary action buttons based on heroCtas from getHomePageContent, routing users to scheduling, therapist search, and clinic search pages using URL navigation.
Display a list of featured services from featuredServices, with each item linking to its service detail page using a serviceId URL parameter and an additional link to the overall services listing.
Display a list of featured group programs from featuredGroups, with each item linking to the groups listing page filtered via a query URL parameter.
Display a list of featured resources from featuredResources, with each item linking to the resources listing page filtered via a query URL parameter.
Display a list of featured donation campaigns from featuredDonationCampaigns, with each item linking to the donation page and passing the selected fundId and suggestedAmount as URL parameters.

Provide global navigation from the header to core sections: services.html, therapists.html, resources.html, groups.html, locations.html, pricing_insurance.html, planner.html, donate.html, and about.html.
user_workflows: Schedule an initial evaluation from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home. 2) Page calls getHomePageContent and renders heroCtas. 3) User clicks a primary action whose actionKey corresponds to an evaluation flow (for example, an actionKey that maps to a speech evaluation). 4) The click handler maps the actionKey to appointment_booking.html and constructs a URL with appropriate query parameters (e.g., serviceId and appointmentType). 5) Browser navigates to appointment_booking.html with those parameters in the query string.

Find a therapist from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home and content loads via getHomePageContent. 2) User clicks a primary action with an actionKey mapped to therapist search (for example, 'find_therapist') or uses the 'Our Team' link in the header. 3) Click handler navigates directly to therapists.html without additional URL parameters. 4) Therapists page handles any further filtering or search.

Explore a specific service from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home and featuredServices is populated via getHomePageContent. 2) User clicks on a featured service card. 3) Click handler reads the card’s serviceId. 4) Browser navigates to service_detail.html with a query parameter such as service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}. 5) The service detail page uses the serviceId to load the full service information.

Browse all services from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home. 2) User clicks a link or button labeled for viewing all services within the services section. 3) Browser navigates to services.html without additional parameters. 4) Services page lists all available services and provides its own filters or navigation.

Discover and register for groups from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home and featuredGroups is populated via getHomePageContent. 2) User clicks on a featured group program card. 3) Click handler reads the group’s title and navigates to groups.html with a query parameter such as groups.html?query={encodedTitle}. 4) Groups page uses the query parameter to pre-filter or highlight matching group programs, where the user can proceed to registration.

Read parent resources from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home and featuredResources is populated via getHomePageContent. 2) User clicks on a featured resource card. 3) Click handler reads the card’s primaryTopic and navigates to resources.html with a query parameter such as resources.html?query={encodedPrimaryTopic}. 4) Resources page loads a list filtered or highlighted by that query.

Find a clinic location from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home and heroCtas is rendered. 2) User clicks a primary action mapped to locating clinics (for example, 'find_clinic_near_me'). 3) Click handler navigates to locations.html without extra URL parameters. 4) Locations page manages its own search or geolocation features.

Access pricing and cost-related information from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home and may see a text link or button within a pricing teaser area. 2) User clicks the pricing/insurance link. 3) Browser navigates to pricing_insurance.html. 4) Pricing and insurance page uses its own interfaces to display pricing details and cost estimators.

Access the therapy planner tool from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home. 2) User clicks a 'My Planner' link or button in the header or planner promo block. 3) Browser navigates to planner.html. 4) Planner page uses its own interfaces to manage weekly plans.

Donate from a featured campaign on the homepage: 1) User lands on Home and featuredDonationCampaigns is populated via getHomePageContent. 2) User clicks the donate CTA on a specific campaign card. 3) Click handler reads fundId and suggestedAmount from that card. 4) Browser navigates to donate.html with query parameters such as donate.html?fundId={fundId}&amount={suggestedAmount}. 5) Donate page uses these parameters to pre-select the fund and prefill the amount.

Learn more about the organization from the homepage: 1) User lands on Home. 2) User clicks a link associated with organization overview (e.g., a 'Learn more about us' text link). 3) Browser navigates to about.html without parameters. 4) About page displays organization background information.
interactions: Page load: On initial load, an onInit or onPageLoad handler calls getHomePageContent with no parameters. On success, the returned object is stored in page state and bound to the primary actions panel, featured services, featured groups, featured resources, and featured donation campaigns. If the call fails, an error state is set and the page can show a generic error message or fallback navigation links.

Primary action button click: Each heroCtas item is rendered as a clickable button. When clicked, an onClick handler reads the actionKey and uses a routing function that maps actionKey values to target URLs and URL parameters (e.g., routing a scheduling actionKey to appointment_booking.html with appropriate serviceId and appointmentType query parameters).

Featured service card click: Each card built from featuredServices is clickable. On click, an onClick handler reads the associated serviceId and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}.
View all services click: A link in the services section navigates to services.html when clicked. The link does not depend on API data and is always available.
Featured group program card click: Each card built from featuredGroups is clickable. On click, an onClick handler reads the title of the group and navigates to groups.html?query={encodedTitle}.
Featured resource card click: Each card built from featuredResources is clickable. On click, an onClick handler reads primaryTopic and navigates to resources.html?query={encodedPrimaryTopic}.

Featured donation campaign CTA click: Each campaign in featuredDonationCampaigns includes a donate button. On click, an onClick handler reads fundId and suggestedAmount and navigates to donate.html?fundId={fundId}&amount={suggestedAmount}.

Header navigation link clicks: The header includes clickable items for Home, Services, Our Team, Resources, Groups, Locations, Pricing & Insurance, My Planner, and Donate. Each onClick simply navigates to its corresponding page (e.g., services.html, therapists.html, resources.html) without adding extra query parameters from this page.
Organization intro link click: A text link associated with organization overview triggers navigation to about.html on click.
Error state retry: If getHomePageContent fails, a retry button can be shown. On click, an onClick handler calls getHomePageContent again and updates the state on success.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not require incoming URL parameters; it always calls getHomePageContent without parameters on load and renders whatever content it receives. Internal page state consists of: (1) loading state before getHomePageContent resolves, (2) content state holding the exact structure returned by getHomePageContent, and (3) error state if the interface call fails. The page itself does not read or persist any state via localStorage. Instead, user intent is carried forward by encoding state in URL parameters when navigating to other pages. For example, clicking a featured service card constructs a URL service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}, where serviceId is read directly from the featuredServices entry. Clicking a featured resource card constructs resources.html?query={encodedPrimaryTopic}, so the resource listing page can pre-filter by that topic. Clicking a featured group card constructs groups.html?query={encodedTitle}, allowing the groups page to focus on that group context. Clicking a donate campaign constructs donate.html?fundId={fundId}&amount={suggestedAmount}, enabling the donation page to pre-select the fund and amount. For scheduling actions based on heroCtas, the click handler uses the actionKey to determine the appointment_booking.html URL and query parameters (such as serviceId and appointmentType); these parameters define the initial state of the appointment booking page. All cross-page context is therefore encoded into query parameters on the target URLs rather than stored on the client.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading for the Home page, manages loading and error states, stores the getHomePageContent response, and exposes it to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the page is first loaded; invokes getHomePageContent(), sets loading state true before the call and false after it completes, and on success stores the result in a shared content state object.
onLoadSuccess: Triggered when getHomePageContent returns successfully; clears any error state and passes the data to child components via props or equivalent bindings.
onLoadError: Triggered if getHomePageContent fails; records the error in state and exposes a flag so child components can show an error message or a retry option.
onRetryLoad: Triggered when the user clicks a retry button in an error view; calls getHomePageContent again and re-runs the success/error logic.

components.functionality: Provides top-level navigation links including a Home logo/link and links to Services, Our Team, Resources, Groups, Locations, Pricing & Insurance, My Planner, and Donate pages.
event_handlers: onClickHome: Navigates to index.html (Home) without parameters.

components.functionality: Displays the main introductory text and a set of primary action buttons derived from heroTitle, heroSubtitle, heroBody, and heroCtas, enabling users to quickly start key tasks such as scheduling, finding therapists, or finding clinics.
event_handlers: onClickHeroCta: Receives the clicked CTA item (including its actionKey) and routes the user accordingly. For example, if actionKey is mapped to an evaluation flow, construct a URL to appointment_booking.html with appropriate query parameters (e.g., ?serviceId={mappedServiceId}&appointmentType={mappedAppointmentType}). If actionKey corresponds to therapist search, navigate to therapists.html. If it corresponds to location search, navigate to locations.html.
onRender: Reads the heroTitle, heroSubtitle, and heroBody from the controller’s content state and renders them along with one button for each heroCtas entry.

components.functionality: Renders a collection of service cards based on featuredServices, each showing core information such as name, a short description, age group labels, and a primary action label, and allows users to navigate to detailed information or to the full services listing.
event_handlers: onClickServiceCard: Triggered when a service card is clicked; reads serviceId from the clicked item and navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}.
onClickServicePrimaryCta: Optionally distinct from clicking the card body; also navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId} or to another services-related page if configured.
onClickViewAllServices: Triggered by a 'View all services' link or button in this section; navigates to services.html.

components.functionality: Displays group programs sourced from featuredGroups, showing key information such as title, age range labels, schedule summary, start date, price per child, and location name, and routes users into the groups page with a pre-applied query.
event_handlers: onClickGroupCard: Triggered when a group card is clicked; reads title from the clicked item and navigates to groups.html?query={encodedTitle}. The groups page can then use the query value to filter or highlight the matching GroupProgram records.
onClickViewAllGroups: Triggered by a 'View all groups and classes' link or button in this section; navigates to groups.html.

components.functionality: Shows a list of featured parent resources sourced from featuredResources, displaying titles, primary topics, age range labels, and reading times, and routes users into the resources library with a pre-filled query.
event_handlers: onClickResourceItem: Triggered when a resource list item is clicked; reads primaryTopic and navigates to resources.html?query={encodedPrimaryTopic}.
onClickViewAllResources: Triggered by a 'View all resources' link or button in this section; navigates to resources.html.

components.functionality: Lists featured donation campaigns using featuredDonationCampaigns, displaying each fund’s name, a short description, and a default suggested amount, and provides a CTA to navigate to the donation page with relevant parameters.
event_handlers: onClickDonationCta: Triggered when a campaign’s donate button is clicked; reads fundId and suggestedAmount from the clicked item and navigates to donate.html?fundId={fundId}&amount={suggestedAmount}.
onClickViewAllDonationOptions: Optionally triggered by a 'More ways to give' link; navigates to donate.html without additional parameters so the donation page can display all DonationFund options.

components.functionality: Displays a concise introduction to the organization’s mission and includes a link to the full About page for users who want more detail.
event_handlers: onClickLearnMore: Navigates to about.html when the 'Learn more about us' or equivalent link is clicked.

components.functionality: Provides secondary navigation and quick links to commonly accessed pages (such as Services, Groups, Resources, Locations, Pricing & Insurance, My Planner, Donate, and About), mirroring or complementing the header navigation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom layout: a full-width header at the top, followed immediately by a tall hero section that dominates the initial viewport. Beneath the hero, stack content sections in a single column flow: organization introduction, then featured services, groups, resources, donation campaigns, and finally the footer. All sections sit within full-width background bands, with a centered max-width content container (≈1100–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom layout: a full-width header at the top, followed immediately by a tall hero section that dominates the initial viewport. Beneath the hero, stack content sections in a single column flow: organization introduction, then featured services, groups, resources, donation campaigns, and finally the footer. All sections sit within full-width background bands, with a centered max-width content container (≈1100–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height to the header bar and 80–85% to the hero band, mirroring the mockup. Below the hero, each major section (services, groups, resources, donations) gets a generous vertical block with 64–96px of spacing before and after. Horizontally, content sits in a centered container that spans about 80–90% of the page width up to the max-width; within that, key panels (like primary actions) occupy about 50–60% width for focus while grids use the full container width with gutters.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low information density: few elements per section, ample padding (16–24px) inside cards and panels, and generous whitespace between rows and sections. Each section should have a clear focal element (a heading, a panel, or a grid) with supporting content kept minimal to avoid crowding, aligning with the calm, therapeutic tone.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the mockup’s flow: the eye starts at the logo on the top-left, moves across the header navigation, then drops down into a strong central hero focal point (ink-cloud title + primary actions). Scrolling down, the flow continues in a vertical rhythm: a brief intro section, then a sequence of clearly delineated content bands (services → groups → resources → donations) that guide users naturally towards practical actions and deeper information, ending in footer links.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a clean white header bar stretching full width across the top, containing a left-aligned logo and a single-row navigation aligned in the center-right. Directly beneath, a tall, slate-blue hero band fills most of the remaining initial viewport, visually dominated by a large, organic ink-cloud shape centered horizontally with the main title text set in two calm, bold lines in its center. Just below this title, also centered within the hero, sits a compact, white primary actions panel that surfaces key tasks like scheduling and finding therapists, with ample breathing room around it. As users scroll, the hero gives way to a white section where a centered all-caps heading like “YOUR SPACE THEATRE” introduces the organization, followed by a short, spacious intro paragraph. Below that, alternating soft white and subtly tinted bands present featured services, groups, resources, and donation campaigns in orderly grids and lists within a centered max-width container, each section separated by generous vertical whitespace. The page concludes with a grounded footer bar spanning full width, carrying quick links and contact details in a calm, compact layout that visually anchors the experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-page-controller acts as the overall page frame, spanning the full browser width and height and orchestrating a vertical stack of sections from top to bottom. It establishes a centered max-width content container of approximately 1100–1200px within each section and applies the implicit 12-column grid used across the page. At the very top, it places the header-navigation as a full-width white strip; directly below that, it renders the full-width hero band (background slate-blue) which contains the central ink-cloud illustration, main title, and the primary-actions-panel centered beneath the title. Below the hero band, the controller layouts the organization-intro-block, followed by the featured-services-list, featured-groups-list, featured-resources-list, and featured-donation-campaigns, each as separate horizontal bands with 64–96px vertical spacing between them. The footer-quick-links component is placed at the very bottom, spanning full width. The controller ensures consistent horizontal centering, top/bottom padding, and background color transitions between sections, while keeping the overall flow strictly linear and scroll-based.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural container; it scrolls with the page and defines the stacking order and section backgrounds without its own visible chrome.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation sits at the very top of the page as a full-width horizontal bar on a white background, occupying roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height (about 80–96px tall on desktop). Within a centered 1100–1200px container, the logo block is positioned on the far left and takes about one-third of the horizontal space; the navigation links sit in a single row across the remaining two-thirds, right-aligned or center-right aligned, following an F-pattern scan from left to right. Each nav item has 16–24px padding horizontally for easy scan and click, with substantial whitespace above and below the row so the header feels airy rather than cramped. The bottom edge of the header transitions directly into the top edge of the slate-blue hero band with a clear contrast line but no overlapping elements, echoing the mockup’s clean division between header and hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the top of the viewport on scroll for desktop, so the header bar remains fixed horizontally across the top as users move down the page; it does not overlap content but pushes it down, preserving a clean stacking order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The primary-actions-panel is centered within the hero band, positioned directly below the central ink-cloud title. Vertically, it sits in the lower-middle third of the hero: there is more space above it (toward the title) than below, creating a gentle visual pull from title down into actions. On desktop, the panel spans about 50–60% of the content width (roughly 6–7 columns of the 12-column grid) and is horizontally centered within the 1100–1200px container. It appears as a white or slightly translucent card against the slate-blue background, with rounded corners and generous internal padding of 16–24px on all sides. Inside, 2–3 primary buttons or action tiles are arranged in a single horizontal row with equal spacing between them, each button extending vertically to create comfortable click targets; there is at least 16–24px of whitespace separating the panel from the title above and similar spacing separating it from the bottom of the hero band. The overall effect is a calm, highly focused call-to-action zone that feels integrated with the hero but not crowded by other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; it scrolls out of view with the hero as the user continues down the page and does not float or stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-services-list occupies its own full-width section band below the organization-intro-block. The section sits on a white or very light neutral background, beginning roughly 64–96px below the bottom of the intro text to preserve generous breathing room. Within a centered 1100–1200px container, a section heading (e.g., “Services for Your Child”) is centered or slightly left-aligned at the top, followed by a grid of service cards. On desktop, the grid uses the implicit 12-column system: each card spans 4 columns, producing three cards per row with equal horizontal gutters (16–24px) between them and at least 24–32px vertical spacing between rows. Each service card has 16–24px internal padding, a calm solid background, and simple typography, with a concise title and one or two short lines of description to maintain low density. The list as a whole occupies roughly 60–70% of the viewport height when fully in view, with at least 64px padding at the top and bottom of the section to separate it visually from adjacent bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section aligned in the normal vertical flow; content reflows from a three-column grid on larger screens to fewer columns on smaller devices while remaining centered within the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-groups-list appears as the next major band directly below the services section, separated by 64–96px of vertical whitespace. To gently distinguish it, the background can shift to a very soft tinted color (e.g., a pale blue or warm grey) while still feeling calm and therapeutic. Inside a centered 1100–1200px container, a section heading and brief one-line description align left or center near the top, with ample space above and below. The groups themselves are displayed in a grid similar to services but potentially slightly more compact: still three items per row on desktop, each spanning 4 columns with 16–24px horizontal gutters, but with slightly shorter card heights. Each group card aligns title, age range, and schedule snippet vertically with clear spacing between elements, keeping only the most essential information visible. The overall section height is comparable to the services section, but the subtle background tint helps users visually distinguish the context as they scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll flow; grid breaks into fewer columns on smaller screens while maintaining centered alignment and consistent padding from the viewport edges.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-resources-list sits below the groups section with a clear gap of 64–96px, returning to a white or very light neutral background to maintain a simple banded rhythm. Within the centered 1100–1200px container, a section heading like “Resources for Families” is placed at the top, left-aligned for easier scanning, with a short supporting line below it if needed. Instead of a grid, resources are arranged as a vertical list occupying the central 8–10 columns of the grid, leaving soft margins on either side. Each resource item spans almost the full content width and is laid out as a horizontal row with the title on the left and a simple metadata line or subtle icon on the right, separated by ample horizontal spacing. Items are separated from each other by 16–24px of vertical space with an optional very light divider line, and each row has 12–16px internal vertical padding, maintaining a spacious, readable list. The section is visually lighter and more text-focused than the card grids above, functioning as a calm reading area before moving into the donation content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list that scrolls with the page; rows compress vertically and may stack metadata under titles on smaller viewports while remaining centered and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-donation-campaigns section appears towards the lower part of the page, below the resources list, separated by another 64–96px to preserve the calm rhythm. It occupies a full-width band with a gentle, slightly deeper tint (e.g., a soft slate or muted color) to signal a different type of content without feeling urgent or alarming. Inside the centered 1100–1200px container, a concise heading such as “Support Our Work” is centered at the top, with a single line of explanatory text beneath. Donation campaigns are presented as a grid of 2–3 wider cards per row on desktop: each card spans 4–6 columns depending on the number of items, with 24px gutters between them and generous top/bottom padding inside. Cards contain a short campaign name, one or two brief lines of description, and a clear but calm call-to-action button; visual density stays low, with plenty of negative space around text and buttons. The section has strong top and bottom padding (at least 64px each) to feel like a distinct, self-contained area before the user reaches the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the normal scroll flow; grid adapts by reducing the number of columns on smaller screens while preserving centered content and comfortable padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The organization-intro-block is the first content section immediately below the hero band. It begins where the slate-blue hero background ends and a white background section starts, with a vertical gap of about 64–80px between the bottom of the hero’s primary-actions-panel and the intro heading to keep the transition gentle and spacious. Within the centered 1100–1200px container, an all-caps heading like “YOUR SPACE THEATRE” is centered horizontally near the top, referencing the mockup’s visible heading below the hero. Below the heading, a single short paragraph or two lines of body text introduces the organization and its mission, constrained to about 60–70% of the container’s width (roughly 7–8 columns) and centered to maintain a calm, focused reading area with wide side margins. Vertical spacing is generous: at least 24–32px between heading and paragraph, and 64px before the next section begins. This block acts as a gentle bridge between the bold hero and the more information-heavy sections that follow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text section embedded in the scroll; on smaller screens, text remains centered with margins proportionally reduced but still maintaining clear whitespace above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-quick-links component anchors the page at the very bottom as a full-width horizontal bar with a slightly darker, grounding background (e.g., a dark slate or deep neutral tone) distinct from the sections above. It begins approximately 64–96px below the bottom of the donation campaigns section, ensuring a clear separation. Within a centered 1100–1200px container, quick links, basic navigation, and contact details are arranged in a simple multi-column layout: on desktop, 2–3 columns span the central 8–10 grid columns, leaving comfortable side margins. Each column has a short heading and a vertical list of links with 8–12px spacing between items; vertical padding inside the footer is generous (at least 32–48px top and bottom) to keep it readable and not cramped. The overall footer height is moderate, not competing with the hero or content sections, but visually it provides a clear endpoint and stable base to the vertical flow of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the document; it is not sticky and appears only when users scroll to the end of the page, maintaining the straightforward top-to-bottom flow.

name: Services Overview
title: Services Overview
description: Page that retrieves and lists all therapy services for children, helps parents compare them by age range and availability, and provides quick navigation to detailed pages, booking, groups, resources, and pricing for each service.

core_features: Call getServicesOverview on page load to retrieve the full list of therapy services and their basic attributes.
Display all services in a structured list where each service is represented by an interactive card.
Allow parents to filter the visible services based on age group labels and key attributes (evaluation availability, group options), with filters derived from the loaded data.
Highlight and optionally auto-focus a specific service based on a URL parameter (serviceId) for deep-linking from other pages.

Provide per-service navigation actions: open the service detail page, schedule an evaluation with the service preselected, view groups/classes, open parent resources, and view pricing for that service.
Keep filter and focus state synchronized with URL parameters so that the page can be bookmarked and browser navigation (back/forward) preserves context.
user_workflows:
Browse all services:
1) User navigates to services.html.
2) Page calls getServicesOverview and shows a loading state.
3) When services load, the list of all services appears without filters applied.
4) User scrolls the list, clicks a service name or "Learn more" to go to service_detail.html with the corresponding serviceId in the URL.

Filter services by age and evaluation availability:
1) User opens services.html (optionally with or without URL parameters).
2) After getServicesOverview resolves, the filter component derives available ageGroup options from the services' ageGroupLabels.
3) User selects one or more age group labels and/or toggles an "evaluations available" filter.
4) Filter state is applied to the in-memory services list and also written to the URL query parameters.
5) Only services matching the selected age groups and evaluationAvailable flag remain visible; user may clear filters to return to all services.

Start scheduling an evaluation from a service card:
1) User identifies an appropriate service in the list (optionally after filtering).
2) User clicks the "Schedule an Evaluation" action on that service card.
3) The page navigates to appointment_booking.html, adding serviceId (from the selected service) and appointmentType=initial_evaluation as URL parameters.

Explore groups and classes related to a service:
1) User finds a service that indicates it has group options (derived from hasGroupOptions=true).
2) User clicks the "View groups & classes for this service" action on that service card.
3) The page navigates to groups.html with relatedServiceId set to the selected service's serviceId in the URL.

Access parent resources for a service:
1) User finds a service of interest in the services list.
2) User clicks the "Parent resources for this service" action on that card.
3) The page navigates to resources.html with serviceId set to the selected service's serviceId in the URL.

Check pricing for a specific service:
1) User wants to understand the cost associated with a specific service.
2) User clicks "View pricing for this service" on that service card.
3) The page navigates to pricing_insurance.html with highlightServiceId set to that service's serviceId so the pricing page can emphasize that service.

Open the page focused on a specific service (deep link):
1) User arrives at services.html with a serviceId query parameter (e.g., from another page linking to this one).
2) On load, URL-state parsing stores this serviceId as the focused service.
3) After getServicesOverview completes, the list locates the matching service and applies a visual focus/highlight and scrolls it into view if needed.
4) User can then immediately use quick actions for that focused service or navigate to its detail page.

Return to a previously filtered view via browser back/forward:
1) User applies filters (age group, evaluation available, group options) and possibly focuses a specific service; URL parameters are updated accordingly.
2) User navigates away (e.g., to a detail page) and then uses the browser Back button to return to services.html.
3) On re-entry, the URL is parsed, filters are re-applied to the in-memory services data, and the same subset of services (and any focused service) is restored without needing extra API calls.
interactions: On page load, the data-loader component calls getServicesOverview with no parameters and manages loading and error states.

When the user changes an age group selection in the filter component, the component updates internal filter state, recalculates the filtered services list, and writes the selected age group(s) into the URL query parameters.
When the user toggles an "evaluations available" filter, the component filters to services where evaluationAvailable is true and writes this preference into the URL query parameters.
When the user toggles a "has group options" filter, the component filters to services where hasGroupOptions is true and writes this preference into the URL query parameters.
When the user clears all filters, the component resets filter-related URL parameters and restores the full services list.
When the user clicks on a service name or "Learn more" within a service card, the page navigates to service_detail.html, adding serviceId for that service to the URL.

When the user clicks "Schedule an Evaluation" on a service card where evaluationAvailable is true, the page navigates to appointment_booking.html with serviceId set to that service and appointmentType set to initial_evaluation.
When the user clicks "View groups & classes for this service" on a service card, the page navigates to groups.html with relatedServiceId set to that service's serviceId.
When the user clicks "Parent resources for this service" on a service card, the page navigates to resources.html with serviceId set to that service's serviceId.
When the user clicks "View pricing for this service" on a service card, the page navigates to pricing_insurance.html with highlightServiceId set to that service's serviceId.

When the URL query parameters change due to browser navigation (back/forward) or manual editing, the URL-state component re-parses parameters, updates filter state, and re-applies them to the displayed services list.
If getServicesOverview fails, an error state with a retry action is displayed; clicking retry triggers a new getServicesOverview call.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page has no incoming parameters for the data interface but uses URL query parameters to manage UI state. The following parameters are read and written:
- serviceId: When present, the page treats this as the 'focused' service. After getServicesOverview returns, the services list locates the service object whose serviceId matches the URL parameter, applies a highlight/focus state, and may scroll it into view. Clicking on another service can update the URL's serviceId to the newly focused one.
- ageGroup: Represents the currently selected age group filter. Its value is a label that must match one of the ageGroupLabels values present in the loaded services. On load, if ageGroup is present, the filter component pre-selects that age group and the services list shows only services whose ageGroupLabels array includes this label. When the user changes the age group selection, ageGroup in the URL is updated accordingly. If multiple age groups are supported, they can be encoded either as a repeated parameter or a delimiter-separated value, and the filter logic parses them to an array.
- evaluation: Represents whether the user wants to see only services with evaluations available. When evaluation indicates this filter is active, the list is restricted to services with evaluationAvailable=true. When the user toggles this filter, the evaluation parameter is added, updated, or removed from the URL, and the filtered view updates.
- groups: Represents whether the user wants to see only services that have related group options. When groups indicates this filter is active, the list is restricted to services with hasGroupOptions=true. Toggling the filter adds/removes this parameter and updates the list.
Filter application order is deterministic but does not require multiple API calls: the page always calls getServicesOverview exactly once per load/retry, caches the result in memory, and all filters (ageGroup, evaluation, groups) are applied in memory. URL parameters are the single source of truth for filter and focus state, so browser navigation and sharing the link reproduce the same filtered/focused view without additional interfaces.

components.functionality: On initialization, calls getServicesOverview to retrieve the list of services, stores the result in page-level state, and exposes loading, success, and error states to child components.

Parses URL query parameters on load and on URL changes to derive current filter state (ageGroup, evaluation, groups) and focused serviceId; updates the URL when filters or focus are changed by the user.

Provides interactive controls for filtering the services list based on ageGroupLabels, evaluationAvailable, and hasGroupOptions. All filter options are derived from the currently loaded services, and any change updates the in-memory filtered view and the URL state.

Receives the full services dataset from the loader and the active filter/focus state from the URL/state manager, computes the filtered subset, and renders one service-card component per service. It also marks the focused service (if any) based on serviceId from the URL.

Represents a single service with its name, shortName (if available), description, ageGroupLabels, typicalSessionLengthMinutes (if provided), and indicators for evaluationAvailable and hasGroupOptions. Includes quick action buttons/links for navigating to the service detail page, scheduling an evaluation, viewing groups/classes, viewing parent resources, and viewing pricing for that service.

Handles outbound navigation actions originating from a service-card. Uses the selected service's serviceId to construct destination URLs for service_detail.html, appointment_booking.html, groups.html, resources.html, and pricing_insurance.html with the appropriate query parameters.

Displays a message or guidance when the current filter combination results in zero visible services, and provides an action to reset filters. It is shown only when getServicesOverview has loaded successfully and the filtered services array is empty.
Shows loading indicators while getServicesOverview is in progress and shows an error state with a retry control if the interface call fails.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing header and full-width hero, the Services Overview content forms a vertically stacked column inside a centered, max-width container (~1100–1200px). At the top of this content column, a clear section heading and introductory line (not defined as components here) is followed directly by a horizontally centered filter bar. Beneath the filters, the services list occupies the main body of the page in a multi-card grid. Empty, loading, and error states occupy the same footprint as the list. Non-visual infrastructure components (loader, URL state manager, card navigation handler) sit logically at the top of the services section, supporting but not disturbing this linear top-to-bottom flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing header and full-width hero, the Services Overview content forms a vertically stacked column inside a centered, max-width container (~1100–1200px). At the top of this content column, a clear section heading and introductory line (not defined as components here) is followed directly by a horizontally centered filter bar. Beneath the filters, the services list occupies the main body of the page in a multi-card grid. Empty, loading, and error states occupy the same footprint as the list. Non-visual infrastructure components (loader, URL state manager, card navigation handler) sit logically at the top of the services section, supporting but not disturbing this linear top-to-bottom flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The services section roughly mirrors the hero’s calm space usage: generous margins above the section heading (about 64–96px from the bottom of the hero) and below each major block. The filters take up a modest horizontal band (about 15–20% of the initial post-hero viewport height), while the services list occupies the remaining vertical space and continues below the fold as needed. Within the container, content spans about 80–90% of the available width, staying off the extreme edges to preserve the solid color fields and breathing room seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains low-density and breathable. The filter bar has just a few controls arranged in a single row with 16–24px internal padding and generous gaps between controls. The services list uses a 12-column grid to display spacious cards (e.g., 3 cards per row at desktop), with 24–32px gutters between cards and 32–48px vertical spacing between rows. Empty, loading, and error states rely on a single icon or simple illustration, a short heading, and minimal explanatory text, all centered with generous white space to maintain a calm, non-overwhelming feeling for parents.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye flows from the top-left logo and horizontal nav (header, already defined) down to the centered hero title. From there, the eye moves further down to the Services heading and then immediately to the filter bar, which forms the first interactive focal point. Below the filters, the grid of service cards becomes the primary scanning zone, reading left-to-right across each row and then down, in a familiar F-pattern. Within each card, content is vertically stacked with a clear hierarchy (service name at top, age range and availability next, then calls to action), reinforcing a simple, linear reading order that echoes the hero’s strong central focal point and vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: After the white header bar and the full-width slate-blue hero with the ink-cloud title, the page transitions into a calm, white (or very light) content band. Centered within this band is a max-width container aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. At the top of this container, a simple, all-caps section label (e.g., “SERVICES OVERVIEW”) and a slightly larger title sit centered, with generous spacing above and below to echo the hero’s isolation of key text. Immediately underneath, a single-row filter bar stretches across about 70–80% of the container width, horizontally centered but with its controls left-aligned within it. Below this band, the services list fills the bulk of the page as a grid of large, soft-edged cards arranged in rows, each card occupying roughly one-third of the container width on desktop. There is ample negative space between card rows and around the edges of the grid, so each service feels approachable rather than cluttered. When there are no results, or data is loading, or an error occurs, that same central grid area is replaced by a centered, minimal state message that still respects the same vertical spacing and alignment, keeping the overall layout stable and predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual data-loader component is conceptually positioned at the very top of the Services Overview content region, directly below the hero and above the filters and list. It occupies no visible space but governs the content displayed within the main services container. Think of it as spanning the full width of the centered content container (~1100–1200px) and running horizontally above the filter bar: when active, it triggers the loading state that visually appears in the main list area below, while the overall vertical layout (hero → heading → filters → list) remains unchanged.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered structural component in the normal document flow; it does not occupy physical space but controls which visual child components (list, empty state, loading/error) are rendered in the main services content band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state-manager component sits logically alongside the data loader, at the top of the services content area, just under the hero section. It has no visible UI; instead, it synchronizes the filter state and list parameters with the URL. Visually, the space that would be its footprint is occupied by the section heading and the filter bar, ensuring that any changes in filters immediately reflect in the list below without introducing extra bars or clutter above or beside the filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-interactive in terms of layout; behaves like an invisible controller bound to the same horizontal slice as the heading and filters, without altering spacing or alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is placed near the top of the Services Overview content column, directly below the section heading. It is horizontally centered within the max-width container and spans about 70–80% of that container, leaving comfortable margins on both left and right. Vertically, it forms a low, wide band (roughly 64–96px tall), with 24px of padding inside and 16–24px gaps between individual controls (e.g., age range dropdown, availability toggle, and search). The content inside the bar is left-aligned, so the first filter starts around the left third of the container while the right side remains open or holds secondary controls, preserving the airy feel. There is about 32–40px of vertical space between the bottom edge of the filter bar and the top row of service cards, reinforcing a clear visual separation between control area (filters) and content area (list).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in the normal flow, not fixed or sticky by default, so as not to crowd the viewport; as the user scrolls, it moves up naturally while the list scrolls beneath it, maintaining the calm, uncluttered vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services list occupies the main body of the Services Overview section and sits directly below the filter bar, aligned to the same centered max-width container. It uses the underlying 12-column grid: on desktop, the list presents service cards in a three-column layout, each card spanning 4 columns with 24–32px horizontal gutters between cards and a similar amount of vertical spacing between rows. The grid starts about 32–40px below the filters to create a clear separation, and extends down the page, often beyond the initial viewport. The leftmost cards align with the container’s left grid margin, while the rightmost cards align with the right margin, preserving symmetrical white space on both sides. When the list is long, the user scrolls through rows of cards, but the cards never feel cramped thanks to consistent gutters and 16–24px internal padding within each card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normal scrolling content within the main page flow, expanding vertically as more services are loaded. When loading, empty, or error states are active, this list area is replaced in place by the corresponding state components, keeping the same overall footprint and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each service-card sits as an individual tile within the services list grid. On desktop, a card typically occupies one-third of the container width (4 of 12 columns), with 24–32px of space to the neighboring cards and 24–32px below before the next row starts. The card has clearly defined padding (around 16–24px on all sides) and a subtle background contrast against the page (e.g., white card on a very light slate or vice versa) that echoes the solid color fields of the hero but in a more subdued way. Content inside the card follows a vertical stack: the service name appears at the top left of the card, slightly larger and bolder, followed by age range and availability info on the next lines, then a concise description, and finally a row of subtle CTAs (e.g., ‘View details’, ‘Book’, ‘Groups’, ‘Pricing’) along the bottom. These CTAs may align left, with even spacing between them, leaving calm breathing room around the edges. The overall card height is moderate but can expand as needed with additional text, maintaining consistent top and bottom margins relative to neighboring cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully responsive within the grid: on smaller viewports, cards shift to two-column or single-column layouts but always maintain centered alignment within the main container and consistent vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation handler is an invisible, interactive layer applied to each service card. Spatially, it matches the card’s full footprint, extending from the card’s outer padding edge on all sides so that any click or tap within the card area can trigger navigation to the detailed service page or relevant sub-views. It does not introduce extra visual elements; interaction feedback is expressed through subtle changes in the card itself (e.g., a gentle elevation or border color on hover) that align with the calm yet responsive feel of the overall design. The navigation layer respects the card’s margins within the grid and never extends into the gutters between cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-card clickable overlay tightly bound to the card’s bounds, staying in the normal flow and resizing as the card resizes; it does not affect layout, only the interactive area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When no services match the current filters, this empty-state component occupies the same central area as the services list. It is vertically positioned where the top row of cards would normally begin—about 32–40px below the filters—and horizontally centered within the container. The content is stacked in the middle of this area: a simple icon or small illustration at the top, followed by a short, reassuring headline and a single brief line of helper text, all center-aligned. Surrounding this content is ample white space: at least 64–96px of blank space above and below the text block, so the message feels calm and non-alarming. Optional action buttons, such as “Clear filters” or “View all services,” appear below the text, center-aligned, maintaining the gentle spacing rules of other CTAs on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the services list region entirely when active, without shifting the filters or heading. As filters change, it smoothly gives way to the normal card grid in the same footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error component also takes over the same rectangular area reserved for the services list, beginning just beneath the filter bar. In a loading state, a centered spinner or progress indicator appears with a brief label (e.g., “Loading services…”), both centered horizontally and vertically within the visible part of the list area, with plenty of surrounding space so the user’s attention rests on this single focal point. In an error state, a modest icon, a short, clear error heading, and one or two lines of explanatory text are stacked in the center. An action button such as “Retry” is placed below, also center-aligned. The component never extends into the filter bar or hero space; instead, it provides a calm, contained feedback area where the cards would otherwise appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the list and empty-state within the same container slice. It appears in the normal flow, does not fix to the viewport, and maintains consistent top margin beneath the filters to avoid visual jumps when transitioning between states.

name: Service Detail
title: Service Detail
description: Displays detailed information about a selected therapy service and connects parents to booking, related therapists, groups, resources, and pricing using the serviceId URL parameter.

core_features: Load detailed information for a specific service using serviceId via getServiceDetail and render goals, methods, what to expect, and practical details.
Display age group information, typical session length, typical frequency, evaluation availability, and whether the service has group options.
Provide primary and (when appropriate) secondary booking CTAs that navigate to appointment_booking.html with serviceId and an appointmentType derived from the action.
Show a related therapists section using getServiceRelatedContent, including key therapist attributes and a link to therapists.html filtered by the current service.
Show related group programs using getServiceRelatedContent and allow navigation to groups.html with relatedServiceId set to the current service.
Show related parent resources using getServiceRelatedContent and allow navigation to resources.html filtered by the current service.
Show a pricing summary using getServiceRelatedContent and link to pricing_insurance.html highlighting the current service.
Handle missing or invalid serviceId, loading states, and error states without using local storage, relying entirely on URL parameters and interface responses.
user_workflows:
View service details:
1) User arrives on service_detail.html with a URL parameter serviceId.
2) On page load, the page reads serviceId from the URL.
3) Page calls getServiceDetail(serviceId) and getServiceRelatedContent(serviceId) in parallel or sequence.
4) While data is loading, a loading state is shown instead of content.
5) When getServiceDetail resolves, the page shows the service name, description, goals, methods, what to expect, age group labels, typical session length, typical frequency, and flags such as evaluationAvailable and hasGroupOptions.
6) When getServiceRelatedContent resolves, the page populates sections for related therapists, group programs, resources, and pricing summary.
7) If either call fails or returns no matching service, an error or fallback message is displayed and primary actions are disabled.

Schedule an initial evaluation:
1) User is viewing a valid service detail where evaluationAvailable in getServiceDetail is true.
2) The booking CTA component renders a primary button for evaluation using the service’s primaryCtaLabel when provided.
3) User clicks the primary evaluation button.
4) The click handler constructs a URL to appointment_booking.html including query parameters serviceId (from the current URL) and appointmentType set to initial_evaluation.
5) Browser navigates to appointment_booking.html with those parameters, where the appointment page preselects the service and evaluation mode based on the query string.

Request ongoing appointments (follow-up):
1) User is viewing a service detail where ongoing appointments are supported (e.g., evaluationAvailable is true or the implementation rules allow follow-up booking).
2) The booking CTA component renders a secondary button for ongoing appointments when the page logic determines it should be shown.
3) User clicks the follow-up button.
4) The click handler navigates to appointment_booking.html with serviceId from the current page and appointmentType set to follow_up.
5) The appointment booking page uses those parameters to configure the form for ongoing or follow-up sessions.

Explore therapists for this service:
1) User scrolls to the related therapists section populated from getServiceRelatedContent.relatedTherapists.
2) User reviews the list of therapists and their attributes.
3) User clicks the section-level link “View therapists for this service” (label determined by the implementation).
4) The click handler navigates to therapists.html with a query parameter serviceId equal to the current page’s serviceId.
5) therapists.html uses the serviceId to filter therapists to those providing the selected service.

Explore group programs related to the service:
1) User scrolls to the related groups section populated from getServiceRelatedContent.relatedGroups.
2) User views one or more group teasers, each showing information derived from the group item.
3) User either clicks on a specific group teaser or a section-level link to see all groups for this service.
4) The click handler navigates to groups.html with a query parameter relatedServiceId equal to the current page’s serviceId.
5) groups.html uses relatedServiceId to filter and display groups related to the selected service.

Explore parent resources related to the service:
1) User scrolls to the related resources section populated from getServiceRelatedContent.relatedResources.
2) User reviews a limited set of resources, each showing information derived from the resource item.
3) User clicks a section-level link such as “See more parent resources about this topic.”
4) The click handler navigates to resources.html with a query parameter serviceId equal to the current page’s serviceId.
5) resources.html uses serviceId to filter its resource listing to those relevant to the service.

View pricing and insurance information for the service:
1) User scrolls to the pricing summary section populated from getServiceRelatedContent.pricingSummary.
2) User reviews starting rate and insurance note information if available.
3) User clicks a link or button to see full pricing and insurance details for the service.
4) The click handler navigates to pricing_insurance.html with a query parameter highlightServiceId equal to the current page’s serviceId.
5) pricing_insurance.html uses highlightServiceId to emphasize or pre-focus on the selected service in its UI.

Handle invalid or missing serviceId:
1) User arrives on service_detail.html without a serviceId parameter or with a value that returns an error from getServiceDetail.
2) On load, the router logic detects that serviceId is missing or getServiceDetail returned an error (for example, no service found or a network error).
3) The page displays a generic error or ‘service not found’ state, hides or disables booking and related-content actions, and may offer a navigation link back to services.html.
4) No calls to getServiceRelatedContent are made if getServiceDetail indicates that the service is invalid, or related content results are ignored if they cannot be matched to a valid service.
interactions: On initial page load, a load event handler reads the serviceId URL parameter and triggers getServiceDetail(serviceId) and getServiceRelatedContent(serviceId).

If the user changes the serviceId in the URL (e.g., via internal navigation that updates the query string without full reload), a route-change handler re-runs the data loading sequence with the new serviceId.

Clicking the primary booking CTA button (for evaluation) triggers navigation to appointment_booking.html with serviceId copied from the current URL and appointmentType set to initial_evaluation, only when evaluationAvailable is true in the getServiceDetail response.

Clicking the secondary booking CTA button (for ongoing appointments) triggers navigation to appointment_booking.html with serviceId from the current URL and appointmentType set to follow_up, only when page logic deems this option available.
Clicking the therapists section-level link triggers navigation to therapists.html with serviceId from the current URL appended as a query parameter.
Clicking a related group teaser or the groups section-level link triggers navigation to groups.html with relatedServiceId from the current URL’s serviceId.
Clicking the resources section-level link triggers navigation to resources.html with serviceId from the current URL appended as a query parameter.
Clicking the pricing summary link or button triggers navigation to pricing_insurance.html with highlightServiceId from the current URL’s serviceId.

If any data-loading error occurs during getServiceDetail or getServiceRelatedContent, an error handler updates the page state to show an error message and may provide a link back to services.html; interactive elements that depend on the missing data are disabled or hidden.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the serviceId URL parameter (e.g., service_detail.html?serviceId=...). On every page load or route change, the page reads serviceId from the query string. If serviceId is present, the page calls getServiceDetail(serviceId) to obtain detailed service data and getServiceRelatedContent(serviceId) to obtain related therapists, groups, resources, and pricing. The serviceId from the URL is treated as the single source of truth; it is reused for all outgoing navigation links (to appointment_booking.html, therapists.html, groups.html, resources.html, and pricing_insurance.html) by injecting it directly into the target URL as specified. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; any change in service context must be reflected by changing the serviceId in the URL. Internal navigation that switches to a different service updates the query parameter, which triggers a reload of getServiceDetail and getServiceRelatedContent. Conditional rendering depends on fields from getServiceDetail: for example, evaluationAvailable and primaryCtaLabel determine whether and how the primary booking CTA is shown; typicalSessionLengthMinutes and typicalFrequencySummary determine whether practical details are displayed. The related-content sections render only when the corresponding arrays in getServiceRelatedContent (relatedTherapists, relatedGroups, relatedResources) contain items. The pricing summary renders only when pricingSummary is present. Error and loading states are maintained in memory for the current page load and are not persisted; they reset whenever serviceId changes.

components.functionality: Central controller component that reads the serviceId from the URL, calls getServiceDetail and getServiceRelatedContent, manages loading and error states, and passes resolved data to child components.

Displays top-level service information such as name, short name, description, and age groups, and shows key flags derived from getServiceDetail (e.g., evaluation availability and whether the service has group options).
Shows in-depth service content including goals, methods, and what to expect, using the respective fields from getServiceDetail.
Presents practical information about the service such as typical session length and typical frequency, and indicates whether the service is a group service using getServiceDetail fields.

Renders primary and optional secondary booking buttons based on getServiceDetail, and handles navigation to appointment_booking.html with appropriate query parameters for initial evaluations and follow-up appointments.

Displays a list of therapists related to the current service using getServiceRelatedContent.relatedTherapists, including therapist names, credentials, specialties, age groups served, years of experience, and primary location. Provides a section-level link to navigate to therapists.html filtered by the current service.

Shows group programs related to the service using getServiceRelatedContent.relatedGroups, including title, group type, age range label, start date, schedule summary, price per child, and location name. Clicking either a group teaser or a section-level action navigates to groups.html with relatedServiceId set to the current serviceId.

Displays related parent resources using getServiceRelatedContent.relatedResources, including title, primary topic, age range label, content type, and reading time. Provides a section-level link to navigate to resources.html filtered by the current service.

Shows a concise pricing summary for the service using getServiceRelatedContent.pricingSummary, including starting rate per session, currency, and insurance note when available. Offers a link or button to view full pricing and insurance details for this service.
Shows a loading indicator while getServiceDetail and getServiceRelatedContent are in progress and hides it when both have resolved or errored.
Displays error messaging when serviceId is missing or invalid, or when data loading fails, and can provide a navigation control to return to a higher-level listing page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom structure that mirrors the mockup: a strong, full-width hero band at the top of the page (hosting the service-header-summary and echoing the ink-cloud/title treatment), followed by clearly separated content sections stacked vertically: goals & methods, booking CTA, practical details & pricing, and then related therapists, groups, and resources. Loading and error states take over this main content region when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom structure that mirrors the mockup: a strong, full-width hero band at the top of the page (hosting the service-header-summary and echoing the ink-cloud/title treatment), followed by clearly separated content sections stacked vertically: goals & methods, booking CTA, practical details & pricing, and then related therapists, groups, and resources. Loading and error states take over this main content region when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Allocate the top 70–80% of the initial viewport (below the global site header) to a full-width hero section for the service-header-summary, with the remaining content sections extending below the fold. Each major section sits inside a centered content container (max-width ~1100–1200px) within full-bleed background bands. Vertical spacing between major sections is generous (64–96px), with 24–32px internal padding for cards and panels, preserving the open, therapeutic feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain low information density within each section: few elements, lots of negative space, and clear hierarchy. Primary panels (hero, booking CTA) should have large, readable headings, ample padding, and minimal supporting text. Secondary sections (goals/methods, practical details, pricing, related lists) are broken out into separate blocks or cards with clear headings and consistent spacing, avoiding crowded layouts or overly complex grids.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Lead the eye left-to-right through the global header (logo → nav), then down into a centered hero focal point (service name inside an ink-cloud-like shape), then progress down the page in a simple vertical flow: hero → all-caps section heading for goals/methods → booking CTA → practical details & pricing → related therapists → related groups → related resources. Maintain a central alignment for key titles and CTAs, but use left-aligned body text for readability. Loading and error states should temporarily become the primary focal point by centering their content in the same main axis as the hero.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global white header bar, the Service Detail page opens with a tall, full-width hero band in a calm slate-blue, occupying most of the first viewport. At its center, a dark, organic ink-cloud shape sits horizontally centered, with the service title and a concise summary set inside it, creating a strong central focal point. The rest of the content lives on a white background, inside a centered max-width container. Directly below the hero, an all-caps section title introduces the service’s goals and methods, followed by relaxed, readable text. Further down, a prominent, softly elevated booking panel spans most of the container’s width, making the primary action clear without feeling aggressive. Practical details and pricing are grouped just below in two side-by-side cards with generous padding and matching heights, visually tied as a pair of quick-reference panels. Underneath, three related sections—therapists, groups, and resources—stack vertically, each in its own airy band with a clear heading and a handful of neatly spaced cards or list items. Throughout, generous vertical spacing separates sections, and the central column stays comfortably narrow within the full-width bands, preserving a calm, therapeutic tone. When loading or in error, the main content region is replaced by centered feedback panels that occupy roughly the same footprint as the hero and first sections, keeping the overall balance of the page intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader component is structurally positioned at the top of the main content region, directly below the global site header and above the hero (service-header-summary). It conceptually wraps the entire stack of service-detail components (hero, content sections, CTAs, related lists), controlling whether the normal layout is shown or replaced by loading or error states. It occupies the full width of the page and a vertical range that matches the combined height of all service-detail content, but it remains visually transparent in the loaded state, allowing the visible components to define the on-screen layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural wrapper that spans the main content column; does not affect spacing when the page is fully loaded, but governs whether the loading or error state overlays/replaces the content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component forms the hero section at the top of the Service Detail page content. It sits directly below the global header, stretching full-width edge to edge in a solid slate-blue band that fills roughly 70–80% of the initial viewport height on desktop. Within this band, a centered content container (max-width ~1100–1200px) holds an organic, dark ink-cloud shape positioned in the middle horizontally and slightly above the vertical center, occupying about 60–70% of the container width. The service title appears on one to three lines inside the ink-cloud, centered, in large, calm typography. Supporting summary text (age range, brief description, location or format tags) sits either directly beneath the title within the shape or just under the shape, center-aligned, with generous 24–32px spacing. The top and bottom of the hero band maintain ample negative space, so the ink-cloud and title appear isolated and prominent, mirroring the mockup’s strong focal treatment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in the normal flow, fixed height relative to viewport (on desktop) with responsive height on smaller screens; remains at the top of the scrollable content and scrolls away naturally as the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the hero band, after a generous vertical gap of about 64px, this section begins the main white-content area. It sits inside a centered content container (~1100–1200px wide) on a white background that extends full-width behind it. At the very top of the section, an all-caps heading (e.g., 'SERVICE GOALS & METHODS') is center-aligned, echoing the 'YOUR SPACE THEATRE' heading seen under the hero in the mockup. This heading spans about 50–60% of the container width and has ample whitespace above and below (24–32px). Below the heading, the body content (paragraphs, bullet lists, subheadings) is left-aligned, occupying roughly 70–80% of the container width, centered horizontally, so there are comfortable side margins on larger screens. Vertical spacing between paragraphs and subheadings follows the 16–24px rhythm, supporting easy scanning. The overall section height is allowed to grow based on content but keeps a visible 64–80px gap before the next section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block section in the vertical flow, stretching with content height. No sticky behavior; it scrolls naturally beneath the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is placed below the goals/methods section, separated by about 64px of vertical whitespace. It appears alongside the pricing summary in a two-column layout within the same centered content container. On desktop, the practical-details panel occupies the left half of the 12-column grid (approximately 6 columns, ~50% of the container width), appearing as a softly elevated card with a white background, subtle shadow or border, and 24–32px inner padding. Its heading sits at the top left of the card, with key logistics (session length, frequency, location, recommended age, any prerequisites) arranged in a simple vertical stack, separated by 8–16px. The card height is matched to the pricing card on the right when possible, creating a balanced pair. On smaller screens, this card becomes full-width and stacks above the pricing summary with 32–40px vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of a responsive two-column pair: left card in a side-by-side arrangement on wider screens, stacking into a single column in normal document flow on narrow screens. No sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking CTA panel is positioned between the goals/methods section and the practical/pricing two-column pair, separated from the goals/methods section above by roughly 64px of whitespace. It sits inside the central content container and spans about 70–80% of the container width, centered horizontally so that equal margins appear on both sides, giving it a strong but calm presence. Visually, it is a prominent card or shallow band with a very light tint (e.g., pale slate-blue or warm neutral) to gently distinguish it from the white background. The panel has generous 24–32px padding on all sides. The CTA title (e.g., 'Book this Service') is left-aligned near the top of the panel, taking up the left two-thirds of the width, with a short line of supporting copy below. On desktop, the primary action button is placed to the right of the text in the remaining one-third of the width, vertically centered within the panel, making the right side of the panel the visual anchor for interaction while maintaining overall calm. On smaller screens, the button stacks below the text, full-width, and centered. Ample whitespace (24–32px) separates this panel from the content above and below, making it easy to spot without feeling pushy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level panel in the main vertical flow; may stretch to fit its content but does not stick or float. On responsive layouts, it remains a single, centered card that adapts from text-and-button-in-row (desktop) to stacked text and button (mobile).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section appears below the practical details and pricing cards, with about 64–80px of vertical whitespace separating it from the cards above. It is contained within the same central max-width container but may sit on a subtly tinted background band that extends full-width (very light neutral or extremely soft brand color) to gently indicate a new content group. At the top of the section, a clear heading like 'Therapists offering this service' is left-aligned within the container, spanning about 60–70% of its width, with 16–24px spacing above the list. Below the heading, a row or grid of therapist cards is arranged: on desktop, 2–3 cards per row, each card about 30–45% of the container width with equal horizontal gaps; on smaller screens, cards stack in a single column with 24px vertical spacing. Each card has ample padding and white background with minimal details (photo, name, role, a short qualifier), preserving low density. The bottom of the section leaves a 64–80px gap before the next related section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard content section that scrolls normally. The internal grid is responsive, collapsing to fewer columns on smaller widths while maintaining centered alignment inside the main container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned beneath the related therapists section with a clear 64px vertical separation, this section follows the same structural pattern to maintain visual consistency. It sits within the central content container, optionally on a white or very subtly tinted band that stretches full-width. A left-aligned heading such as 'Related Groups' appears at the top of the section, occupying about 50–60% of the container width, with generous space above and 16–24px below. Beneath the heading, group offerings are displayed in a low-density grid or stacked list: on desktop, 2 cards per row (each ~45–48% of container width), with comfortable horizontal and vertical gaps; on smaller screens, a single column. Each group card uses 16–24px internal padding, simple typography, and maybe one small icon or tag, but avoids visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard section in the vertical flow, mirroring the related therapists grid behavior: responsive card layout that adjusts column count based on available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section comes last among the related content, below the related groups, separated by about 64px of vertical space. It remains inside the same centered content container but can sit on a white background for a calm finish. The section heading (e.g., 'Helpful Resources') is left-aligned at the top and spans roughly half the container width, with 16–24px spacing before the resource items.
