The resources themselves may be displayed as a simple vertical list or a light grid of compact cards, each spanning about 70–80% of the container width on desktop and 100% on mobile, centered within the container. Items are separated by 16–24px vertical gaps to avoid crowding. Each item might include a title, brief description, and a subtle link indicator, but visual treatment remains understated, making this clearly tertiary to service details and booking.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard stacked section; items form a vertically scrolling list that fits naturally at the end of the main content. No special sticky or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component sits to the right of the practical details card in the same horizontal row, within the shared centered content container. On desktop, it occupies the right half of the 12-column grid (approximately 6 columns, ~50% of the container width), forming a visually balanced pair with the practical details card. It appears as a card with a white background, subtle elevation or border, and 24–32px internal padding. The pricing heading is left-aligned at the top, with key price information (per session, package options, notes on funding or rebates) displayed in a simple vertical stack, using larger type for primary price points and smaller type for notes. Horizontal alignment and vertical spacing match the practical details card, and both cards share the same vertical height when content allows, reinforcing their pairing. On smaller screens, this card stacks below the practical details card, full-width, with a 32–40px gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-hand card of a responsive two-column pair: shares a row with the practical details card on wider screens, then stacks beneath it in the normal document flow on narrower screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the page is in a loading state, this component visually replaces or overlays the entire main content region managed by the service-detail-loader. It occupies the full width of the page below the global header and extends vertically to cover at least the height of the hero plus the first one or two sections (roughly one full viewport height on desktop). The background remains white or a very light neutral, maintaining the calm tone. At the center of this area, both horizontally and vertically, a compact loading indicator (spinner or subtle animation) is displayed, with a short status message beneath it. Optionally, faint skeleton blocks can mimic the rough positions of the hero title and one or two content cards, aligned within the same central max-width container to preserve the expected layout proportions. Generous whitespace surrounds the loading indicator, keeping the density low.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the standard hero and content sections within the main scrollable area; scroll is typically minimal or unnecessary while loading as the content height is constrained to around one viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: If an error occurs loading the service, this component temporarily takes over the same main content region where the hero and details would normally appear. It spans the full width under the global header and ensures at least one viewport of vertical space so it feels like a self-contained screen. Within this area, a central content container (max-width ~700–800px) is horizontally centered. Inside, an error icon or subtle illustration sits near the top center, followed by a clear error heading (e.g., 'We couldn’t load this service') and a brief, reassuring message, all center-aligned. A primary button to retry or navigate back to the services list is placed directly below, centered with ample padding and margin. The surrounding whitespace is substantial—both above and below the error block—reinforcing focus and maintaining the calm, therapeutic atmosphere even in an error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the normal hero and content stack in the main scroll area while active; the error block is vertically centered within at least one viewport height, minimizing the need to scroll and making the message and recovery actions immediately apparent.

name: Appointment Booking
title: Appointment Booking
description: Page for parents/guardians to request therapy appointments or evaluations by selecting a service, choosing a clinic location, specifying schedule preferences or a specific available time slot, entering child and parent details, and submitting the request.

core_features: Initialize booking context by reading URL parameters (serviceId, appointmentType, therapistId, locationId) and loading dynamic configuration via getAppointmentBookingConfig.
Provide a service selector and appointment type selector populated dynamically from getAppointmentBookingConfig, with preselection logic based on defaultSelections and URL parameters.
Allow selection or confirmation of clinic location using configuration from getAppointmentBookingConfig, and provide navigation to locations.html to choose a different clinic.

Support two main scheduling modes: (a) slot-based evaluation booking using findAvailableAppointmentSlots; (b) schedule-preference requests without a specific slot, using preferred day of week and time range.
Offer date-range and time-constraint controls to search for available appointment slots for eligible appointment types by calling findAvailableAppointmentSlots.
Display available appointment slots returned by findAvailableAppointmentSlots and allow the user to select exactly one slot for slot-based booking flows.
Collect child information (name, age) and parent contact information (name, phone, email), plus optional additional notes, for all request types.

Determine the requestType (evaluation_specific_slot, therapist_preference, or general_inquiry) based on whether a slot is selected and whether a therapist is specified, then submit the request via submitAppointmentRequest.
Display submission results using the response from submitAppointmentRequest, including success/failure status, message, and nextStepsText, and provide navigation options (e.g., back to home).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Book a specific evaluation time slot
1. User arrives on appointment_booking.html, possibly from a service or pricing page, with URL parameters such as serviceId and appointmentType.
2. On page load, the page parses URL parameters and calls getAppointmentBookingConfig with serviceId, therapistId, and locationId from the URL (where present).
3. When configuration is returned, the page populates the service dropdown, appointment type dropdown, and location selector. It applies defaultSelections from the response and then applies any appointmentType value passed in the URL (if it matches a returned appointment type for the selected service).
4. The user confirms or changes the service and appointment type. If the appointment type corresponds to a slot-based initial evaluation (as determined by the configuration and front-end rules), the page switches to the slot-search mode.
5. The user selects a date range for the evaluation (bounded by datePickerConfig.minDate and datePickerConfig.maxDate) and optionally adjusts filters such as earliest allowed start time and whether to restrict to weekdays.
6. The user clicks a control (e.g., "Search times") that triggers findAvailableAppointmentSlots with the selected serviceId, derived appointmentKind (e.g., initial_evaluation for evaluation flows), selected locationId, chosen startDate and endDate, earliestStartTime, and weekdaysOnly.
7. The page displays a list of returned slots (with startDatetime, endDatetime, therapistName, locationName). If no slots are returned, an appropriate message and alternative instructions (e.g., use preference mode) are shown.
8. The user selects one slot. The page stores the selected appointmentSlotId and associates it with the current serviceId and locationId.
9. The user fills in childName and childAgeYears and enters parentName, parentPhone, parentEmail, and optional additionalNotes.
10. On submit, the page validates required fields and determines requestType = evaluation_specific_slot because a specific slot is chosen.
11. The page calls submitAppointmentRequest with: requestType = evaluation_specific_slot, the active serviceId, the selected appointmentSlotId, therapistId left undefined or null (the backend infers the therapist via the slot), the selected locationId, and all child and parent details including additionalNotes; preferredDayOfWeek and preferredTimeRange are omitted or null because a specific slot is chosen.
12. On success, the page displays the response message and nextStepsText, disables the form (or shows a submitted state), and offers navigation (e.g., a link to index.html). On failure, an error state is displayed and the user can correct input or retry.

Workflow 2: Request an appointment with a specific therapist (no fixed slot)
1. User arrives on appointment_booking.html from a therapist-specific context (e.g., therapist_profile.html) with therapistId and optionally serviceId and locationId in the URL.
2. On load, the page parses URL parameters and calls getAppointmentBookingConfig with serviceId, therapistId, and locationId.
3. When configuration is returned, the page:
   - Preselects the service if defaultSelections.serviceId is defined or if the URL serviceId matches one of the returned services.
   - Preselects appointmentType from defaultSelections or from the URL appointmentType value when applicable.
   - Preselects locationId from defaultSelections or from the URL.
   - Maintains the given therapistId in page state as the therapist preference for this request.
4. If the combination of service and appointmentType is not configured for slot-based booking (per front-end rules derived from configuration), the page shows schedule-preference controls instead of slot search.
5. The user selects a preferredDayOfWeek (using values aligned with the AppointmentRequest enum) and preferredTimeRange (using values aligned with the AppointmentRequest enum) to indicate when they are generally available.
6. The user fills in childName and childAgeYears, then parentName, parentPhone, parentEmail, and optional additionalNotes (which can include any notes about the therapist preference).
7. On submit, the page validates that required parent and service fields are present, and sets requestType = therapist_preference because a therapistId is present but no specific slot is selected.
8. The page calls submitAppointmentRequest with: requestType = therapist_preference, serviceId from the selector, appointmentSlotId omitted or null, therapistId from URL/page state, locationId from the selector, child details, preferredDayOfWeek, preferredTimeRange, additionalNotes, and parent contact details.
9. On success, the page shows the confirmation using message and nextStepsText, and offers navigation options (e.g., to index.html).

Workflow 3: General appointment inquiry or flexible scheduling request
1. User navigates to appointment_booking.html from a general entry point (e.g., index.html or pricing_insurance.html) without a preselected therapist and possibly without serviceId.
2. On load, the page parses any URL parameters and calls getAppointmentBookingConfig with serviceId, therapistId, and locationId if provided, otherwise undefined.
3. After configuration loads, the page shows the service dropdown with all available services and appointment types for each, as returned from getAppointmentBookingConfig. If defaultSelections.serviceId or URL serviceId is present, it is preselected.
4. The user selects a service and an appointmentType. If the selection does not use slot-based booking (for example, some follow-up or general consultation types), the page presents schedule-preference controls instead of slot selection.
5. The user chooses preferredDayOfWeek and preferredTimeRange, and optionally uses the date picker to indicate a broad date window if desired (e.g., via notes or an additional date-range selector), without triggering slot-specific calls.
6. The user fills in childName, childAgeYears, parentName, parentPhone, parentEmail, and any additionalNotes to describe needs or questions.
7. On submit, because there is no therapistId and no appointmentSlotId, the page sets requestType = general_inquiry.
8. The page calls submitAppointmentRequest with: requestType = general_inquiry, the chosen serviceId, appointmentSlotId omitted or null, therapistId omitted or null, selected locationId (if any), child details, preferredDayOfWeek, preferredTimeRange, additionalNotes, and parent contact details.
9. On success, the page displays confirmation and nextStepsText explaining how and when the clinic will follow up, and provides a link back to index.html or to other relevant pages.
10. If the user changes service or appointmentType after submission, the page resets the form state and clears the previous submission state, without resubmitting automatically.
interactions: On page load: Parse URL parameters (serviceId, appointmentType, therapistId, locationId) and trigger a call to getAppointmentBookingConfig. During the loading state, disable form interactions or show a loading indicator.

On configuration load (success): Populate service selector, appointment type selector, and location selector from the services and locations arrays. Apply defaultSelections, then override appointmentType selection with the URL-provided value if it is present in the selected service’s appointmentTypes.
On configuration load (error): Show an error state and provide a retry button. The retry button repeats the getAppointmentBookingConfig call with the same parameters.

On service selection change: Update the selected serviceId in page state, update the appointmentType dropdown to show only appointmentTypes associated with the selected service, adjust the selected appointmentType if the previous selection is no longer valid, clear any selected appointmentSlotId, and (if in slot-based mode) clear slot search results until the user performs a new search.

On appointment type change: Update the selected appointmentType value in page state, derive the appointmentKind needed for findAvailableAppointmentSlots when applicable, and toggle between slot-based mode (showing slot search controls) and preference-based mode (showing preferredDayOfWeek and preferredTimeRange). Clear any previously selected slots when changing appointmentType.

On location selection change: Update locationId in state, optionally clear slot search results for slot-based modes, and require a new search via findAvailableAppointmentSlots for the new location before allowing slot selection.

On "Find a different clinic" click: Navigate to locations.html using window location changes, optionally including a return URL parameter (e.g., locations.html?returnTo=appointment_booking.html) so that the user can come back to the booking page with a new locationId selected.

On date range or time filter change in slot-search controls: Update local state for startDate, endDate, earliestStartTime, weekdaysOnly, and enable or update the "Search times" action. The action is disabled until required parameters (serviceId, appointmentKind, locationId, and valid dates) are present.

On "Search times" (slot search) click: Call findAvailableAppointmentSlots with the current serviceId, appointmentKind derived from selected appointmentType, locationId, startDate, endDate, earliestStartTime, and weekdaysOnly. During the request, show a loading state and disable the button.

On received slot list from findAvailableAppointmentSlots: Render the list of slots. Each slot entry is selectable. If the list is empty, show a no-results message and suggest using schedule-preference mode or adjusting filters.

On slot selection: Update appointmentSlotId in page state to the chosen slot. Only one slot can be active at a time; selecting a new slot deselects the previous one. The submit button becomes enabled when required child and parent fields and appointmentSlotId are present in slot-based mode.
On preferredDayOfWeek selection: Store the chosen enum value in state (monday–sunday) and use it when calling submitAppointmentRequest for preference-based or general-inquiry requests.
On preferredTimeRange selection: Store the chosen enum value in state (e.g., morning, afternoon, after_3_pm, etc.) and use it when calling submitAppointmentRequest.

On child information input (name and age): Bind text/number input fields to childName and childAgeYears in page state. Use these values when submitting the request. Validate age as a non-negative number before submission.

On parent contact information input: Bind text inputs to parentName, parentPhone, and parentEmail in page state. Validate that these fields are non-empty and that parentEmail has a valid format (client-side check) before enabling the final submit button.
On additionalNotes input: Bind a multiline text field to additionalNotes in state. This field is optional and passed to submitAppointmentRequest when present.

On main form submission: Prevent default form submission behavior, run client-side validation (required fields, specific slot presence when in slot mode), determine requestType based on current mode (evaluation_specific_slot if appointmentSlotId is set and slot-based mode is active; therapist_preference if therapistId is present without a slot; general_inquiry otherwise), then call submitAppointmentRequest with all appropriate parameters.

On submitAppointmentRequest success: Transition the page into a confirmation state. Display the message and nextStepsText from the response. Optionally show the generated appointmentRequestId for user reference. Disable the editing of form fields or clearly separate the confirmation state from the editable form.
On submitAppointmentRequest failure: Display an error message, keep the form fields editable with the current values intact, and allow the user to correct errors or retry submission.
On "Back to Home" click after successful submission: Navigate to index.html using a standard link or location change.

On manual URL parameter change (e.g., user navigates to appointment_booking.html with new serviceId): On the next load, the component reinitializes using the new URL parameters and repeats the configuration-loading workflow.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily derived from and synchronized with URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page reads serviceId, appointmentType, therapistId, and locationId from the query string of appointment_booking.html. These values are passed (except appointmentType) to getAppointmentBookingConfig (serviceId, therapistId, locationId). When the configuration response arrives, the page initializes internal state: the list of services and their appointmentTypes, available locations, and defaultSelections (serviceId, appointmentType, therapistId, locationId). Default selections from the API are applied first; then, if a URL param is present for serviceId, therapistId, or locationId, that value overrides the defaults where it matches available options. For appointmentType, because it is not a parameter to getAppointmentBookingConfig, the page applies defaultSelections.appointmentType from the response, then checks whether the URL appointmentType matches one of the appointmentTypes for the selected service and, if so, overrides the selected appointmentType with the URL value. The selected serviceId, appointmentType, and locationId live in in-memory component state for interaction, while the canonical initial values remain those from the URL and configuration. When the user changes service or appointmentType via the UI, the page updates internal state and re-derives whether the flow is slot-based or preference-based. It does not automatically update the URL, unless explicitly designed to push updated query parameters (this can be optionally implemented using history APIs, still relying on URL parameters as the persistence mechanism rather than localStorage). For slot-based flows, the page derives appointmentKind needed by findAvailableAppointmentSlots from the selected appointmentType according to rules shared with the backend (for example, by direct mapping when values match or by convention). The slot search state (startDate, endDate, earliestStartTime, weekdaysOnly) is held in in-memory state until the user triggers a search. The results of findAvailableAppointmentSlots are not persisted in the URL; they remain transient UI state. The presence of a therapistId in the URL or defaultSelections indicates a therapist-preference context; if no slot is chosen, this leads to requestType = therapist_preference on submission. If neither therapistId nor appointmentSlotId is defined at submission time, the page uses requestType = general_inquiry. The AppointmentRequest itself is not stored locally; instead, submitAppointmentRequest returns appointmentRequestId and nextStepsText, which are used only to drive the confirmation UI. Navigation away from the page (to index.html or locations.html) is done through URL-based links, and any future return to appointment_booking.html must again rely on URL parameters to re-establish context.

components.functionality: On initial page load, parse URL query parameters (serviceId, appointmentType, therapistId, locationId), call getAppointmentBookingConfig with the applicable parameters, manage loading/error states, and pass the loaded configuration and derived initial selections to child components.

Display and manage selection of the therapy service and appointment type. Populate options from the services array returned by getAppointmentBookingConfig. Maintain the currently selected serviceId and appointmentType in state, react to change events by updating downstream components (location selector, scheduling mode, slot search availability), and respect initial values from defaultSelections and URL appointmentType.

Render a dropdown or equivalent selector of clinic locations using locations from getAppointmentBookingConfig, apply any defaultSelections.locationId or URL-provided locationId, and manage the currently selected locationId used in slot searches and submissions. Provide a control to navigate out to locations.html to choose a different clinic.

Determine whether the current combination of serviceId and appointmentType should use slot-based booking (for example, initial evaluations) or preference-based booking (for example, follow-ups or general inquiries). Exposes a simple mode flag (e.g., slotMode or preferenceMode) to other components. When serviceId or appointmentType changes, recalculates the mode and instructs slot-search and preference controls to show or hide appropriately, clearing incompatible state (such as appointmentSlotId) when modes switch.

When in slot-based mode, display controls for selecting a date range within datePickerConfig.minDate and datePickerConfig.maxDate, an earliest start time, and a weekdays-only toggle. Validate that the required parameters (serviceId, locationId, appointmentKind, startDate, endDate) are present and, upon user action, invoke findAvailableAppointmentSlots to search for open slots.

Display the list of available appointment slots returned by findAvailableAppointmentSlots, including startDatetime, endDatetime, therapistName, and locationName. Enable the user to select a single slot, updating the selected appointmentSlotId used for submission. Show appropriate messaging when no slots are available or when an error occurs while loading slots.

For non-slot-based flows, collect the user's preferredDayOfWeek and preferredTimeRange using options aligned with the AppointmentRequest enums. These values are bound to state and are included when calling submitAppointmentRequest for therapist_preference or general_inquiry request types.

Collect the child’s name and age in years (childName, childAgeYears). Validate any basic constraints (such as non-empty name and non-negative age) and expose these values to the submission controller.

Collect parent/guardian contact information, including parentName, parentPhone, and parentEmail, along with optional additionalNotes. Enforce that the three contact fields are present and that email passes basic client-side validation. Provide validation feedback and enable the submit button only when required fields are complete.

Coordinate final validation and submission of the appointment or evaluation request. Gathers state from the service-and-appointment-type-selector, location-selector, scheduling-mode-controller, slot-results-list (appointmentSlotId), schedule-preferences-form, child-details-form, and parent-contact-form. Determines requestType based on current state: uses evaluation_specific_slot when in slot-based mode and an appointmentSlotId is selected; uses therapist_preference when a therapistId is present but no slot is selected; defaults to general_inquiry when neither applies. Calls submitAppointmentRequest with the assembled parameters and manages submission loading, error, and success states.

Display feedback after submitAppointmentRequest completes. In success cases, show the response message, nextStepsText, and optionally appointmentRequestId. In error cases, show error messages and provide retry guidance. Manage transitions between editable form state and read-only confirmation state.

Provide navigation actions related to the booking context. After successful submission, show a link to index.html (Back to Home). Within the form, provide a link or button to locations.html to choose a different clinic, optionally including URL parameters that allow the user to be returned to appointment_booking.html with a selected locationId.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s header + full-width hero pattern. At the very top is the global site header (logo left, nav right, on white), followed immediately by a full-width slate-blue hero band with a centered ink-cloud shape containing the page title (e.g., “Book an Appointment”). Directly beneath the hero, the appointment flow is organized as a single, linear, top-to-bottom sequence of wide, centered form sections inside a max-width container (~1100–1200px). Components are grouped into a clear booking flow: (1) a compact step/navigation strip, then (2) service & type selection, (3) location, (4) scheduling mode and either slot search + results or schedule preferences, (5) child details, (6) parent details, and (7) submission + result. All major pieces sit in the normal document flow, stacked vertically, without sidebars.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s header + full-width hero pattern. At the very top is the global site header (logo left, nav right, on white), followed immediately by a full-width slate-blue hero band with a centered ink-cloud shape containing the page title (e.g., “Book an Appointment”). Directly beneath the hero, the appointment flow is organized as a single, linear, top-to-bottom sequence of wide, centered form sections inside a max-width container (~1100–1200px). Components are grouped into a clear booking flow: (1) a compact step/navigation strip, then (2) service & type selection, (3) location, (4) scheduling mode and either slot search + results or schedule preferences, (5) child details, (6) parent details, and (7) submission + result. All major pieces sit in the normal document flow, stacked vertically, without sidebars.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly the top 15–20% of the initial viewport height, the hero band fills most of the remaining initial viewport (around 50–60%), with the top of the first form section just peeking above the fold. Below the hero, the main content area sits on a white (or very light) background in a centered max-width container. Each key form section (service, location, schedule, child, parent, submission) appears as a wide, card-like block spanning about 70–80% of the container width, centered on the page. Vertical spacing between these blocks is generous—about 64–96px—while padding inside the blocks is 24–32px to keep interactive elements comfortable and calm. The loader and submission result panel, when active, occupy the full width of the content region and visually override or sit above the underlying sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse and calm. Each section focuses on a single decision cluster (e.g., just service selection, just scheduling) with minimal competing elements. Fields are aligned in a simple vertical rhythm with generous 16–24px spacing between field groups. There are no tightly packed multi-column layouts; if two columns appear, they still sit within a wide card with ample left/right padding. Only the slot results list has a higher element count, but items are visually grouped and separated with sufficient vertical spacing to maintain a therapeutic, unhurried feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the logo in the top-left of the header, moves across the top navigation, then drops down to the large, centered ink-cloud title in the hero. From there, the flow continues vertically: first to a shallow, centered booking navigation/step strip, then down through each form section in order. Key interactive choices that affect subsequent content (scheduling mode, submission controls) are centered and isolated with extra whitespace, reinforcing their importance. The layout preserves the mockup’s pattern of a simple F-scan in the header followed by a strong central focal point in the hero, then a clean, linear, downward reading path through the booking flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global header and a full-width slate-blue hero featuring a large, centered ink-cloud with the page title, the Appointment Booking page unfolds as a calm, vertical sequence of wide, centered cards on a white background. Just under the hero, a subtle, horizontally centered step indicator shows where the user is in the booking journey. Beneath that, each major step—selecting a service, choosing a location, deciding how to schedule, entering the child’s information, adding parent contact details, and finally submitting—is presented as its own well-padded block spanning about three-quarters of the content width, with generous white space separating sections. The scheduling area sits mid-page and either shows a vertical pair of cards (search controls above, results list below) or a single schedule-preferences card, depending on the chosen mode. Throughout, content density stays low, with plenty of breathing room around fields and between sections, echoing the mockup’s therapeutic, reassuring feel. When the page is loading or after submission, full-width overlays or status panels softly sit atop this structure, but the underlying linear layout remains centered, orderly, and easy to scan from top to bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay covering the entire booking content area, starting just below the global header and spanning full viewport width and height. It sits above the hero band and all subsequent form sections. At the vertical and horizontal center of the viewport, a small, unobtrusive loading card is displayed, roughly 20–25% of the viewport width, with 24px padding, rounded corners, and a soft shadow. Inside this card, a spinner or progress indicator is centered above a short line of explanatory text. The rest of the page is visually dimmed but still faintly visible beneath, reinforcing that the user should wait before interacting with the booking form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay positioning relative to the viewport; covers and disables interaction with the hero and all form sections while loading is in progress.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This form section is the first main card immediately below the hero band (with a 64–80px vertical gap) and just beneath the booking navigation links. It is centered within the max-width content container, spanning about 70–75% of the container’s width, leaving equal left and right margins for calm negative space. The section appears as a white card on the white/light background but is subtly distinguished by a soft border or shadow and 24–32px of internal padding. A section heading is top-aligned and centered or slightly left-aligned within the card, with the actual service and appointment-type fields stacked vertically underneath, following the same centered column. The bottom of the card sits at least 48px above the next section, ensuring clear separation and a sense of stepwise progression.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal document flow, expanding vertically as needed for its fields while maintaining its centered, fixed-width footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location-selector card sits directly below the service-and-appointment-type section, separated by 64px of vertical whitespace. It uses the same width and alignment: centered in the page, occupying about 70–75% of the content container, with matching left/right margins so it visually lines up with the card above. The card has similar visual treatment—subtle border or shadow, 24–32px internal padding—creating a consistent stack of steps. Inside, the location title/label appears at the top, with location selection controls (dropdown, map toggle, etc.) arranged in a simple vertical layout. The bottom margin of this card is again generous (48–64px) to clearly distinguish it from the scheduling-mode controller that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal document flow; no special behavior beyond maintaining alignment with neighboring cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The scheduling-mode controller is a compact, horizontally oriented control placed just below the location-selector card, with about 48–64px of vertical separation. It spans a slightly narrower width—around 50–60% of the content container—and is centered, so it sits neatly within the same vertical column established by the cards above. Visually, it can be a low-profile pill or toggle bar with two or three options (e.g., “Find a specific time” vs “Share schedule preferences”), with the toggle labels centered within the control. The surrounding whitespace is slightly larger than that of standard fields, framing this as a key decision point. Below this controller, there is a clear 40–56px gap before either the slot-search-controls + slot-results-list stack or the schedule-preferences-form begins, emphasizing the switch between modes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline block; however, it conditionally controls which subsequent sections are shown directly beneath it without changing its own position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the user chooses a specific-slot scheduling mode, the slot-search-controls section appears as a card directly beneath the scheduling-mode controller, separated by about 40–56px of whitespace. The card mirrors the earlier cards’ alignment: centered in the content container, occupying about 70–75% of the width, with consistent left/right margins and 24–32px internal padding. Within the card, filters and date/time selectors are arranged in a clean vertical stack, potentially with a simple two-column layout for related controls but always within the same visual column. There is at least 24px of padding at the bottom of the card before the slot-results-list begins, so the search controls read as a distinct “top” block of the scheduling area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal document flow, visible only when the specific-time scheduling mode is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The slot-results-list sits directly below the slot-search-controls card, with a modest vertical gap of about 24–32px to keep them visually connected as a single scheduling unit. It spans the same width as the card above (about 70–75% of the container) and is also centered. The list may appear as a tall, scrollable area or a stack of individual slot cards; in either case, items are arranged in a single column, with each slot card filling most of the list’s width, separated by 16–24px vertical spacing. The entire list region can be framed as another soft card or as an extension of the search card, with subtle background differentiation if needed. The list’s bottom edge maintains at least 64px of whitespace before the next major section (child-details or schedule-preferences, depending on flow), preventing visual crowding even if many slots are present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal flow; vertical height grows based on results, pushing subsequent sections downward while preserving top-to-bottom reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the user selects a schedule-preferences mode instead of specific slot search, this section occupies the same position as the slot-search-controls and slot-results-list would. It appears directly beneath the scheduling-mode controller, separated by about 40–56px of whitespace. The preferences form is displayed as a single, centered card spanning 70–75% of the container width, with 24–32px internal padding and a soft border or shadow. Inside, fields for preferred days, times, and flexibility are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing, maintaining the low-density feel. There is a generous 64px gap below this card before the child-details section, making the scheduling choice feel like a complete step in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal document flow; it replaces both slot-search-controls and slot-results-list when the preferences mode is active, occupying their combined vertical space but as a single card.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child-details form is a major section that always appears after scheduling, regardless of mode. It is positioned below either the slot-results-list or the schedule-preferences-form, with a generous vertical gap of 64–80px to mark a clear transition from scheduling to personal details. The form sits in a wide, centered card matching previous sections—about 70–75% of container width—with consistent internal padding (24–32px). A section heading (e.g., “Child Details”) is placed at the top-left of the card, with form fields arranged in a single dominant column beneath, possibly with occasional two-field rows but retaining a calm, spacious layout. The bottom of the card leaves at least 64px before the parent-contact form begins, preserving the visual hierarchy of steps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal document flow, expanding vertically to fit its fields while staying aligned with the other cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parent-contact form follows directly under the child-details card, separated by 64px of whitespace to signal a new but related step. It mirrors the same card style: centered, spanning 70–75% of the content container, with equal side margins and 24–32px internal padding. The section heading appears at the top, aligned with headings above to create a clean vertical spine. Contact fields—name, phone, email, preferred contact method—are stacked in a simple vertical layout with comfortable spacing. The bottom of this card leaves space (48–64px) before the submission controls, which appear as the final step in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal document flow; no special behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-submission controller sits at the bottom of the form stack, just below the parent-contact card with about 48–64px of vertical separation. It is narrower and more compact than the main cards, spanning around 40–50% of the content container and centered horizontally. This section may be a minimal bar or small card containing the primary “Submit Request” button (and possibly a secondary action like “Save for later”), with the main button centered. Extra whitespace surrounds this controller—both above and below—to isolate it visually and emphasize it as the concluding action. The space beneath it (to the page bottom) remains generous, ensuring the page ends in a calm, uncramped way even on tall screens.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal flow; scrolls with the page and does not stick, keeping the experience calm and non-intrusive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After an appointment is submitted, the submission-result panel appears directly above the appointment-submission controller, pushing it slightly downward but keeping both within the same centered column. The panel spans 70–80% of the content container width and is centered, similar to the major cards, but uses a distinct background color (e.g., soft green or neutral) to signal success or status. Internal padding of 24–32px provides room for a prominent status message, a brief explanation, and any follow-up guidance. There is a comfortable 24–32px gap between the bottom of this panel and the submission controller so that the call-to-action (if still present, e.g., “Book another appointment”) remains visible and clearly related. The panel is also separated by at least 64px from the parent-contact form above, clearly marking the transition from input to feedback.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in normal document flow; appears conditionally after submission, occupying space between the last form section and the submission controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-navigation links are a slim, horizontal navigation control placed immediately below the hero band and above the first form card (service-and-appointment-type). They sit within the same max-width container as the forms but span only about 60–70% of its width, centered on the page. This component appears as a low-profile stepper or set of text links representing each booking step (Service, Location, Schedule, Child, Parent, Review/Submit). The links are arranged in a single horizontal row, evenly spaced, with subtle indicators for the current step (e.g., bold text or a small underline). Generous whitespace (32–48px) above and below keeps this strip light and non-cluttered while still providing orientation. It visually anchors the transition from the bold hero to the more detailed form stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline block in normal document flow; clicking items may scroll to or highlight sections, but the component itself does not stick or float.

name: Therapist Directory
title: Therapist Directory
description: Searchable, filterable directory of therapists that helps parents find appropriate providers for their child. Users can filter by specialty, age group, location, language, and insurance, sort by experience, view therapist details, and quickly save therapists to favorites.

core_features: Load available filter options (specialties, age groups, locations, languages, insurance plans) using getTherapistFilterOptions and bind them to filter controls.

Parse incoming URL parameters (specialty, ageGroup, sortBy, serviceId, plus page, query, locationId, language, insurancePlanId if present) and initialize filter, search, sort, and pagination state from them.
Execute therapist searches with the current filter, search query, sort, and pagination state using searchTherapists and display results as therapist cards.
Provide filter controls for specialty, age group, location, language, and insurance plan that update URL parameters and trigger a new search when changed.
Provide a search input for free-text name/keyword search that updates the URL and triggers searchTherapists.
Provide a sort control that includes at least a 'Years of Experience (Most to Least)' option mapped to sortBy=experience_desc and updates URL/sends to searchTherapists.
Display therapist cards showing name, credentials, key specialties, age groups served, years of experience, primary location name, and languages, based on the searchTherapists response.
Allow users to click anywhere on a therapist card to navigate to therapist_profile.html with therapistId passed via URL parameter.
Provide an optional 'Add to Favorites' / 'Remove from Favorites' control on each therapist card that calls addTherapistToFavorites or removeTherapistFromFavorites.
Provide a link in the directory header to navigate to favorites.html for viewing saved therapists.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Landing on the directory with pre-applied specialty and age group from another page
1. User clicks a 'Find a Therapist' or similar CTA on index.html or resources.html that links to therapists.html with URL parameters such as ?specialty={value}&ageGroup={value}&sortBy={value}.
2. therapists.html loads and parses URL parameters (specialty, ageGroup, sortBy, serviceId, query, locationId, language, insurancePlanId, page).
3. Page calls getTherapistFilterOptions to populate dropdowns for specialties, age groups, locations, languages, and insurance plans.
4. The filter controls are initialized with the values from the URL (e.g., specialty and age group pre-selected; sortBy defaults to URL value or a page default).
5. Page calls searchTherapists with the current state (query, specialty, ageGroup, locationId, language, insurancePlanId, sortBy, page, pageSize).
6. Therapist cards render based on the response; user can adjust filters or sort as needed.

Workflow: Searching for a therapist by name and narrowing by insurance
1. On therapists.html, user enters a name or keyword into the search input.
2. User submits the search (press Enter or clicks a search button).
3. The page sets page=1, updates the URL to include query={userInput} and any existing filter/sort parameters, then calls searchTherapists with the updated parameters.
4. Results list updates to show therapists matching the new query.
5. User opens the insurance filter and selects an insurance plan option.
6. URL is updated to include insurancePlanId={selectedId}, page is reset to 1, and searchTherapists is called again with the updated filters.
7. Results list refreshes; currently applied filters are visible in the filter controls.

Workflow: Sorting by years of experience (most to least)
1. User opens the sort control (e.g., a dropdown) above the results list.
2. User selects the option labeled 'Years of Experience (Most to Least)'.
3. The page sets sortBy='experience_desc', updates the URL parameter sortBy, and calls searchTherapists with the same filters and query but the new sortBy value and page=1.
4. The therapist cards re-render in order of descending yearsOfExperience from the searchTherapists response.

Workflow: Viewing therapist details
1. While viewing search results, user identifies a therapist of interest.
2. User clicks anywhere on that therapist card (outside the favorites control).
3. The page navigates to therapist_profile.html?therapistId={selectedTherapist.therapistId}.
4. The therapist profile page uses therapistId to load and show full details (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Saving and unsaving a therapist to favorites
1. User sees a therapist card in the results list.
2. User clicks the favorites toggle control (e.g., 'Save' button or icon) on the card.
3. If the therapist is not currently marked as favorited in the page state, the page calls addTherapistToFavorites({therapistId}).
4. On success, the card's local favorite state is updated to 'favorited' so the control now shows the 'Remove from Favorites' state.
5. If the user clicks the control again, the page calls removeTherapistFromFavorites({therapistId}).
6. On success, the local favorite state for that therapist is set to 'not favorited' and the control updates accordingly.

Workflow: Navigating to saved therapists
1. User wants to see all previously saved therapists.
2. In the directory header, user clicks the 'View Saved Therapists' link or icon.
3. The browser navigates to favorites.html (no additional parameters required for this navigation).

Workflow: Paginating through results
1. After a search, user sees multiple pages of results (as indicated by totalResults and pageSize from searchTherapists).
2. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., 'Next', 'Previous', or a specific page number).
3. The page updates the URL parameter page={selectedPage} while keeping query, filters, and sortBy unchanged.
4. searchTherapists is called with the updated page value and existing parameters.
5. The results list updates to show the therapists for the newly selected page.
interactions: On initial page load (onMount): parse URL parameters (specialty, ageGroup, sortBy, serviceId, query, locationId, language, insurancePlanId, page). Call getTherapistFilterOptions to populate filter controls. After filter options load, initialize the filter controls to match URL parameters and call searchTherapists with the current state.

Filter interactions (onChange for specialty, age group, location, language, insurance plan controls): when a user changes any filter, update the corresponding URL parameter, reset page=1, and call searchTherapists with the updated filters, current query, and current sortBy.

Search input interactions (onSubmit and onChange): onChange updates local form state only; onSubmit sets query URL parameter, resets page=1, and calls searchTherapists with the new query and current filters/sortBy.

Sort control interaction (onChange): when the user picks a new sort option, set sortBy (e.g., 'experience_desc' for 'Years of Experience (Most to Least)'), update the sortBy URL parameter, reset page=1, and call searchTherapists with unchanged filters/query but new sortBy.
Therapist card click interaction (onClick): clicking in the main body of a therapist card (excluding its favorite control) navigates to therapist_profile.html?therapistId={card.therapistId}.

Favorites toggle interaction on therapist card (onClick): clicking the favorites control on a card prevents navigation and instead either calls addTherapistToFavorites or removeTherapistFromFavorites based on the card's current favorite state; on success, the local favorite state for that card is updated.

Pagination interactions (onClick): clicking 'Next', 'Previous', or a page number sets the page URL parameter to the selected page, then calls searchTherapists with the new page value while maintaining existing query, filters, and sortBy.
Header link interaction to favorites (onClick): clicking 'View Saved Therapists' navigates the browser to favorites.html.

URL change handling (onPopState / back-forward navigation): when the user uses the browser back/forward buttons, the page re-parses the current URL parameters and re-runs searchTherapists and re-initializes filter/sort/search controls to match the URL state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page's authoritative state for search, filters, sorting, and pagination is encoded in URL parameters. On load and on any URL change, the page parses these parameters to set its internal state and to decide how to call searchTherapists.

URL parameters used and their effects:
- specialty: if present, sets the value of the Specialty filter control. When calling searchTherapists, this value is passed as the specialty parameter.
- ageGroup: if present, sets the Age Group filter control. When calling searchTherapists, this value is passed as the ageGroup parameter.
- locationId: if present, sets the Location filter control. When calling searchTherapists, this value is passed as the locationId parameter.
- language: if present, sets the Language filter control. When calling searchTherapists, this value is passed as the language parameter.
- insurancePlanId: if present, sets the Insurance filter control. When calling searchTherapists, this value is passed as the insurancePlanId parameter.
- query: if present, sets the text in the search input. When calling searchTherapists, this value is passed as the query parameter.
- sortBy: if present, sets the selected option in the sort control; for example, a value of 'experience_desc' corresponds to sorting by years of experience descending. This value is passed as the sortBy parameter to searchTherapists. If absent, the page uses a default sort option and may omit sortBy or use a default value when calling searchTherapists.
- page: if present, defines the current page of results. This value is passed as the page parameter to searchTherapists. If absent, the page defaults to the first page (page=1).
- pageSize (optional): if supported by the implementation, this can be read from the URL to control how many therapists are requested per page. If absent, a default pageSize is used when calling searchTherapists.
- serviceId: if present (e.g., from service_detail.html), it indicates that results should be constrained to therapists who provide a particular Service. This parameter does not map directly to a searchTherapists argument; instead, the back-end or page-level configuration applies this constraint when resolving searchTherapists or when instantiating the page, so that the list of therapists returned by searchTherapists already reflects the serviceId constraint. The serviceId is preserved in the URL when filters or sorts change, so the constraint remains active.

State transitions:
- Any user change to filters, search query, sort option, or pagination updates the corresponding URL parameter(s) and triggers a new call to searchTherapists with values derived from the URL.
- The page maintains a transient in-memory map of therapistId -> favoriteState (e.g., 'favorited' or 'notFavorited') purely for UI toggling. This transient state is updated only after successful responses from addTherapistToFavorites or removeTherapistFromFavorites and is not stored in URL parameters or localStorage.
- When navigating via the browser back/forward buttons, URL parameters change; the page listens for these changes, re-parses them, updates control values, and re-calls searchTherapists so that the visible state always matches the URL.

components.functionality: Displays the page title, a summary of current results (e.g., number of therapists found) derived from the latest searchTherapists response, and a navigation link to view saved therapists.
event_handlers: onMount -> no API call; reacts to updates from therapist-results-list component

components.functionality: Provides a text input for free-text search (e.g., therapist name, keyword). On submit, updates the query URL parameter, resets pagination, and triggers searchTherapists with the new query and existing filters/sort.
event_handlers: onChange:searchInput -> update local input state (not URL yet)

onSubmit:searchForm -> update URL.query, set URL.page=1, call searchTherapists({ query: URL.query, specialty: URL.specialty, ageGroup: URL.ageGroup, locationId: URL.locationId, language: URL.language, insurancePlanId: URL.insurancePlanId, sortBy: URL.sortBy, page: 1, pageSize: URL.pageSize or default })

components.functionality: Displays and manages filter controls for specialty, age group, location, language, and insurance. Filter options are dynamically loaded via getTherapistFilterOptions. When filters change, related URL parameters are updated, pagination resets, and searchTherapists is called.
event_handlers: onMount -> call getTherapistFilterOptions() to populate all filter dropdowns; once loaded, initialize each control selection from corresponding URL parameter if present

onChange:specialtyFilter -> set URL.specialty to the selected value (or remove if cleared), set URL.page=1, call searchTherapists with updated specialty and existing query, other filters, sortBy, and page=1
onChange:ageGroupFilter -> set URL.ageGroup, set URL.page=1, call searchTherapists with updated ageGroup and existing parameters
onChange:locationFilter -> set URL.locationId, set URL.page=1, call searchTherapists with updated locationId and existing parameters
onChange:languageFilter -> set URL.language, set URL.page=1, call searchTherapists with updated language and existing parameters
onChange:insuranceFilter -> set URL.insurancePlanId, set URL.page=1, call searchTherapists with updated insurancePlanId and existing parameters

onClick:clearAllFilters (optional) -> remove specialty, ageGroup, locationId, language, insurancePlanId from URL, set URL.page=1, call searchTherapists with only query and sortBy (plus any serviceId constraint handled on the back-end)

components.functionality: Allows users to choose how therapist results are ordered, including an option mapped to sortBy='experience_desc' for 'Years of Experience (Most to Least)'. Updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers searchTherapists when changed.
event_handlers: onChange:sortSelect -> set URL.sortBy to the selected value (e.g., 'experience_desc'), set URL.page=1, call searchTherapists({ query: URL.query, specialty: URL.specialty, ageGroup: URL.ageGroup, locationId: URL.locationId, language: URL.language, insurancePlanId: URL.insurancePlanId, sortBy: URL.sortBy, page: 1, pageSize: URL.pageSize or default })

components.functionality: Executes therapist searches via searchTherapists based on current URL-derived state and renders a list of therapist cards. Updates result count for the directory-header and provides data to the pagination component.
event_handlers: onMount -> after filter options are loaded and URL parameters parsed, call searchTherapists({ query: URL.query, specialty: URL.specialty, ageGroup: URL.ageGroup, locationId: URL.locationId, language: URL.language, insurancePlanId: URL.insurancePlanId, sortBy: URL.sortBy, page: URL.page or 1, pageSize: URL.pageSize or default }); any serviceId constraint is applied by the back-end or page configuration.
onURLChange (e.g., browser back/forward or programmatic updates) -> re-call searchTherapists with parameters derived from the new URL.
onSearchParametersChanged (from search-form, filter-panel, sort-control, pagination) -> re-call searchTherapists with updated parameters and re-render the list.

components.functionality: Displays information for a single therapist returned by searchTherapists, including name, credentials, key specialties, age groups served labels, years of experience, primary location name, and languages. Supports navigation to the therapist profile and favoriting/unfavoriting.
data_binding: FavoriteTherapist (transient local UI state: therapistId -> isFavorited)
event_handlers: onClick:favoritesToggle (if isFavorited is false) -> call addTherapistToFavorites({ therapistId: therapist.therapistId }); on success, set local isFavorited=true for this therapistId
onClick:favoritesToggle (if isFavorited is true) -> call removeTherapistFromFavorites({ therapistId: therapist.therapistId }); on success, set local isFavorited=false for this therapistId

components.functionality: Displays pagination controls based on totalResults and pageSize from searchTherapists. Allows the user to navigate between pages of therapist results by updating the page URL parameter and re-calling searchTherapists.
event_handlers: onClick:pageNumber -> set URL.page to the selected page number, call searchTherapists({ query: URL.query, specialty: URL.specialty, ageGroup: URL.ageGroup, locationId: URL.locationId, language: URL.language, insurancePlanId: URL.insurancePlanId, sortBy: URL.sortBy, page: URL.page, pageSize: URL.pageSize or default })
onClick:nextPage -> if current page < totalPages, increment URL.page, call searchTherapists with new page
onClick:previousPage -> if current page > 1, decrement URL.page, call searchTherapists with new page

components.functionality: Centralizes parsing and updating of URL parameters so that all components use a consistent source of truth for query, filters, sort, and pagination. Listens for browser navigation events and notifies dependent components to re-run searchTherapists when URL state changes.
event_handlers: onMount -> parse current window.location.search into structured state and expose it to other components (e.g., via a shared context).

onUpdateParameters (called by filters, search form, sort control, pagination) -> merge new parameters into the existing URL querystring, update the browser URL (e.g., using history.pushState or replaceState), and trigger an event so therapist-results-list can call searchTherapists.

onPopState -> when browser back/forward is used, re-parse URL parameters and notify dependent components (search form, filter panel, sort control, results list, pagination) to update their UI and re-call searchTherapists as needed.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Apply the mockup’s header + hero pattern as: (1) a full-width directory header band at the top of the page (below the global site header) functioning as a calm hero for the Therapist Directory; (2) immediately beneath, a centered search form spanning the middle of the page; (3) below that, a two-column content band where filters sit in a left sidebar and results occupy a wider right column; (4) pagination at the bottom of the results column. All components follow a linear top-to-bottom reading order with a strong central focus for the main title and search tools, then a left-to-right scan between filters and results.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Apply the mockup’s header + hero pattern as: (1) a full-width directory header band at the top of the page (below the global site header) functioning as a calm hero for the Therapist Directory; (2) immediately beneath, a centered search form spanning the middle of the page; (3) below that, a two-column content band where filters sit in a left sidebar and results occupy a wider right column; (4) pagination at the bottom of the results column. All components follow a linear top-to-bottom reading order with a strong central focus for the main title and search tools, then a left-to-right scan between filters and results.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use full-width color bands for the directory header (hero-style) and the main directory content area, each containing a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) on a 12-column grid. The directory header band should occupy roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height to echo the spacious hero feel, with the remaining viewport height given to the search form and the top of the filters/results area. Within the content container, dedicate about 25–30% of the horizontal width to the filter sidebar (3–4 columns) and 70–75% to the results area (8–9 columns). Maintain 64–96px vertical spacing between major sections (header band ↔ search form ↔ filters/results band ↔ pagination) to preserve a calm, open feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low and breathing room high. Each major component (directory header, search form, filter panel, results list, pagination) should have generous internal padding (16–24px) and clear separation from adjacent elements using the 8px spacing scale, with major section gaps of 64–96px. Filters are grouped into clear blocks rather than tightly packed controls; therapist cards use ample white space around content to avoid visual clutter. The overall impression is of a structured but gentle interface, appropriate for parents making careful decisions.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the top-left page context (global site header, outside this feature) into the centered directory header ink-cloud and page title, creating a strong central focal point similar to the mockup’s hero. From there, the eye naturally drops straight down to the centered search form, then into the main content band where attention first hits the left filter sidebar and then sweeps right to the sort control and therapist results. Once in the results area, the flow is vertically down the list of therapist cards, ending in the centered pagination. This preserves the mockup’s pattern of a brief horizontal scan in the header area, followed by a strong central hero, followed by a structured vertical reading path.

layout.overall_description: The Therapist Directory page opens with a full-width, slate-blue header band dedicated to this section, sitting just below the global site header. In the center of this band, within the usual 1100–1200px content width, a large ink-cloud shape is horizontally centered, and the page title 'Therapist Directory' appears in two calming lines inside it, mirroring the mockup’s hero treatment. Generous empty space above, below, and to the sides of the ink cloud keeps the header quiet and focused. Immediately beneath this band, the background returns to white and a wide, centered search form sits in the upper-middle of the page, spanning around two-thirds of the content width with generous vertical padding. Below the search form, the layout resolves into a light, full-width content band: a filter sidebar on the left occupies roughly one quarter of the width, while a taller results column on the right consumes the remaining space. At the top of this right-hand column, a slim sorting and meta-information row aligns with the start of the filter panel, and below it a vertical stack of roomy therapist cards fills the column. The list extends downward with consistent spacing between cards, and at the bottom, a simple, centered pagination control caps the page. Throughout, there is ample vertical separation between sections, clear alignment to an invisible 12-column grid, and a calm, therapeutic tone with minimal visual noise.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The directory header is a full-width horizontal band positioned at the very top of the Therapist Directory page content, immediately below the global site header (logo and main navigation). It uses a solid slate-blue background that spans edge-to-edge across the screen. Inside this band, the main content is constrained to a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid. In the vertical center of this band, an organic ink-cloud shape is horizontally centered, occupying roughly the middle third of the viewport width and about one third of the band’s height. The page title 'Therapist Directory' is set on two lines inside the ink-cloud, both horizontally and vertically centered within the shape, with generous padding of clear space around the text to maintain focus. There is ample vertical margin within the band: roughly 64–80px of space from the top of the band to the top of the ink-cloud, and a similar margin from the bottom of the ink-cloud to the bottom of the band, echoing the hero treatment of the reference mockup. The overall band height is roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height, making this the dominant visual entry point before users scroll to the search and content sections below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section that scrolls away normally with the page; content is centered within a fixed max-width container and remains responsive within the 12-column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The therapist search form sits directly below the directory header band, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 64px to create breathing room between the hero and the interactive tools. It is positioned in the center of the page within the same max-width content container used throughout. The form itself spans roughly 60–70% of the container width (about 7–8 of 12 columns), leaving equal white space to its left and right, which reinforces the calm, centered feel seen in the mockup’s hero. The form may appear as a single, slightly elevated white card on a white or very light background, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. Primary inputs (e.g., main search bar, location field) are arranged in a single horizontal row on large screens, with enough horizontal spacing (16–24px) between fields to prevent crowding; secondary controls can sit on a second row, still centered under the primary row. There is a comfortable 32–48px margin below the form before the start of the main filter/results band, ensuring the search form feels like a distinct, easy-to-spot tool rather than part of a dense cluster.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that sits in normal document flow, centered within the content container; on smaller screens it can naturally stack its fields vertically while maintaining its central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The therapist filter panel is positioned below the search form as the left column of the main directory content band. This band spans the full viewport width but is visually separated from the search area above with a subtle background tone shift (e.g., very light neutral) and a vertical gap of about 48–64px. Within the centered 12-column container, the filter panel occupies roughly 3–4 columns (about 25–30% of the width) aligned flush to the left edge of the container. Its top aligns horizontally with the top of the therapist sort control in the results column, creating a clean horizontal baseline. The panel itself reads as a vertical card or a set of stacked filter blocks, extending downward with consistent padding (24px inside the panel edges) and clear segmentation between filter groups (e.g., specialty, age group, location), each separated by 24–32px of vertical space. The overall width is constrained and not overly tall visually per screenful, keeping each individual filter legible and scannable, with no more than 2–3 interactive elements per horizontal row. This left-side placement and substantial vertical extension mirror the mockup’s strong top-to-bottom flow while anchoring the user’s attention before they scan right to the results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the filter panel behaves as a sticky sidebar within the main content band: once the user scrolls past the search form, the panel remains pinned a short distance below the top of the viewport (approx. 24–32px offset), while the results list scrolls independently on the right. On smaller screens, it can revert to a non-sticky, stacked section above the results to preserve usability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The therapist sort control is located at the top of the right-hand results column, horizontally aligned with the top edge of the filter panel to its left. Within the 12-column content container, the results column occupies roughly 8–9 columns (about 70–75% width) starting from the right edge of the filter panel with a modest 24–32px gutter between them. The sort control forms a shallow horizontal bar stretching across the full width of the results column but using a light visual touch: for example, a small line of text showing the number of matches on the left and a compact 'Sort by' dropdown aligned to the right, with 16–24px vertical padding. There is minimal decoration, ensuring that this control does not compete with the search form or therapist cards; instead, it quietly caps the results list and adds structure. Below the sort control, a 16–24px gap separates it from the first therapist card, making the hierarchy clear while maintaining the overall spacious, low-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the results column, scrolling away with the therapist cards; visually anchored to the top of the results area and aligned with the filter panel to create a stable horizontal rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The therapist results list occupies the full height of the right-hand column beneath the sort control. It is aligned to the same left and right edges as the sort bar, forming a clean vertical column that begins just to the right of the filter panel’s gutter. Each therapist card within the list spans the entire width of this column, creating a clear single-column stack of results that is easy to scan vertically. There is a consistent vertical gap of 24–32px between cards, and a slightly larger buffer (32–40px) between the sort control and the first card to emphasize the separation between controls and content. The list extends downward and may surpass the initial viewport height, encouraging a natural scroll. The overall density remains low: even with many cards, the combination of full-width columns, ample vertical spacing, and a clear left alignment ensures that the list feels ordered and approachable rather than crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrolling content within the right column; it grows and shrinks based on the number of results, pushing the pagination control down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each therapist card is a rectangular, full-width tile within the results column, aligned flush with the column’s left and right edges. The card uses a white background on the light content band, with subtle rounding of the corners and a faint shadow or border to separate it from the background. Internally, there is generous padding of 24–32px on all sides, ensuring the therapist’s name, photo, specialties, age range, location, languages, and insurance badges have room to breathe. The internal layout can follow a calm, left-to-right structure: a photo or initial-based avatar on the far left, key details (name, role, specialties) stacked in the center, and secondary actions (save to favorites, view details) aligned to the right, all on the same baseline. Vertical spacing between lines of text follows the 8–16px scale, keeping information readable without feeling dense. The cards adapt in height depending on content but maintain consistent top and bottom padding, preserving a rhythm as users scroll down the list. A 24–32px gap below each card allows the background to peek through, reinforcing the visual separation between results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static items within the results list; they maintain their position in order based on sorting and filtering, with no special fixed or floating behavior. Cards can expand slightly in height for additional details in responsive states without breaking the overall column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination control appears at the bottom of the results column, directly under the last therapist card and aligned to the same left and right edges. There is a larger vertical gap of about 48–64px between the final card and the pagination, visually signaling the end of the current page of results and preventing the controls from feeling cramped. The pagination itself is centered horizontally within the results column: page numbers, previous/next arrows, and any 'Load more' or 'Show more results' actions are arranged in a single row, with equal spacing between elements and 12–16px padding around each clickable area. The control does not dominate the page visually; instead, it serves as a calm, clearly identifiable endpoint, with sufficient white space above and below so users can easily find it after scrolling through the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control anchored to the bottom of the results list within the right column; it moves downward as more cards are loaded or as the window height changes, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is a non-visual component with no direct spatial footprint in the layout. Conceptually, it sits around the entire Therapist Directory feature, monitoring changes to the search form, filter panel, sort control, and pagination and synchronizing these states with the page URL (e.g., query parameters). It ensures that when users interact with the visible components, the URL accurately reflects their current view and that reloading, sharing, or navigating back restores the same filter and results configuration. While it does not occupy any visible space, it is effectively 'wrapped' around the directory content in the structure of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; logically fixed to the page’s directory context, listening for events and updating the URL and internal state without affecting the physical layout or scroll behavior.

name: Therapist Profile
title: Therapist Profile
description: Displays detailed information for a single therapist, allows parents to save or unsave the therapist as a favorite, and provides a direct path to request an appointment with that therapist.

core_features: Load and display a therapist’s full profile using the therapistId URL parameter and the getTherapistProfile interface.
Show key therapist details including name, credentials, bio, specialties, services provided, age ranges served, years of experience, primary clinic location, languages, and accepted insurance plans.

Provide a clearly visible favorites control (button or heart icon) that adds the therapist to, or removes the therapist from, the user’s favorites list using addTherapistToFavorites and removeTherapistFromFavorites.
Provide a prominent “Request Appointment” action that navigates to appointment_booking.html with the therapistId preselected via URL parameter.
Offer navigation back to the therapist directory (therapists.html) and to a general clinics list (locations.html) from the therapist’s location summary.
Handle loading, success, and error states when fetching therapist data, and prevent favorites actions when no valid therapistId is available.
user_workflows:
View therapist profile from directory:
1. User clicks a therapist entry on therapists.html, which navigates to therapist_profile.html?therapistId={id}.
2. On page load, therapist_profile.html reads therapistId from the URL.
3. The page calls getTherapistProfile(therapistId) and shows a loading state.
4. When data is returned, the profile sections (summary, bio, specialties, services, location, insurance) render using the response.
5. User can scroll through sections to review information.
6. User can click “Back to Therapist Directory” to return to therapists.html.

Add a therapist to favorites:
1. While viewing a therapist profile, the user sees a favorites control (e.g., “Add to Favorites” button or heart icon) in the header.
2. User clicks the favorites control.
3. The page checks that a therapistId is present from the URL and that the profile has loaded.
4. The page calls addTherapistToFavorites(therapistId).
5. While waiting, the favorites control enters a pending state (e.g., disabled with a spinner) to prevent duplicate clicks.
6. On success, the component updates its local state to mark the therapist as favorited and updates the button label/icon to reflect the new state.
7. If the call fails, the control returns to its previous state and an error message is shown in a status area on the page.

Remove a therapist from favorites:
1. If the therapist is indicated as already favorited (determined by URL hint or prior in-session action), the favorites control is shown as “In Favorites” or with a filled heart state.
2. User clicks the favorites control again to remove.
3. The page calls removeTherapistFromFavorites(therapistId).
4. While waiting, the control enters a pending state.
5. On success, the local favorite state switches to not-favorited and the control label/icon updates accordingly.
6. On failure, the previous state is restored and an error message is shown.

Request an appointment with this therapist:
1. On the therapist profile, user clicks the “Request Appointment” button.
