Immediately below the hero, on a white background, the search form is centered as the key interaction entry point, followed by a horizontal filter bar, then results summary, card list, and pagination. Loading, empty, and error states occupy the same central content zone where the card list normally appears.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height is reserved for the global site header, with 30–40% for the library hero (resources-header-controls) below it, echoing the mockup’s strong hero presence. The remaining area in the initial viewport is occupied by the search form and the top of the results section. All main sections honor generous vertical spacing: 64–96px between hero and search, search and filter bar, filter bar and results summary, and between the card list and pagination. Horizontally, content sits in a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) for all components except the full-bleed hero background color, which stretches edge-to-edge.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout deliberately keeps information density low, with few elements per vertical slice and ample white space. The hero shows only the title and simple controls, with large margins around the central ink-cloud shape. The search form is a single, prominent field (or minimal field cluster) with clear breathing room. The filter bar groups controls in a single line where possible, with generous spacing between filter chips. The card grid shows 2–3 cards per row on desktop with wide gutters, padded cards, and clear hierarchy so parents can scan calmly without visual overload.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first hits the top-left site logo (global header), moves right across the main navigation, then drops down into the hero where the large, centered ink-cloud with the library title becomes the dominant focal point. From there, the flow continues straight downward: first to the centered search bar, then to the horizontal filter bar, then to the results summary and into the grid of cards. Within each card, the eye moves from the title at the top-left to secondary metadata and then to the save control. This preserves the mockup’s combination of an F-pattern across the header and a strong central focus in the hero, followed by a clear top-to-bottom reading path.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global header, a full-width slate-blue band spans the screen, calm and uncluttered. In its center floats an organic dark ink-cloud shape, sized to roughly 60–70% of the content width, with the words “Parent Resources Library” set in large, centered type inside it, mirroring the original hero treatment. This entire hero area is spacious, with generous vertical padding so the title has room to breathe. Beneath the hero, the background switches back to white and the main content is constrained inside a centered, ~1140px-wide column. At the top of this column, a wide, centered search form invites parents to start exploring. Just below, a horizontal filter bar stretches across the same width, aligning options from left to right, while keeping plenty of white space above and below. The results summary follows as a subtle, left-aligned line of text introducing the grid of resource cards. The cards themselves sit in a soft, light-neutral field with 2–3 cards per row on desktop, each card surrounded by ample margins and inner padding so nothing feels cramped. At the very bottom of the grid, centered pagination controls appear with generous top margin. When loading, error, or empty states occur, they take over the central card area, remaining vertically and horizontally centered to maintain the serene, focus-forward feel of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-header-controls component forms the content of the hero section directly beneath the global site header. It sits inside a full-width slate-blue band that stretches edge-to-edge. Within this band, a centered content container (max width ≈ 1100–1200px) vertically and horizontally aligns the hero content. An organic, dark ink-cloud shape is placed in the horizontal center, occupying roughly 60–70% of the content width and about one-third of the hero height. The text label for the page (e.g., “Parent Resources Library”) is centered within this shape on one or two lines, set in large, high-contrast type. Any library-specific navigation (such as tabs for “All resources” and “Saved”) is placed just below the ink-cloud, also centered, spanning about 40–60% of the content width. These nav items are arranged in a single horizontal row with even spacing and minimal, calm styling (pills or text tabs), leaving at least 24–32px of vertical padding above and below. The hero has generous top and bottom padding (64–96px each) so that the ink-cloud and its title feel visually isolated and easy to focus on, echoing the original mockup’s spacious hero treatment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width hero region that scrolls out of view with the page; the content within is centered and non-sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-search-form is positioned immediately below the hero, on a white background, inside the same centered max-width container (~1100–1200px). It is the first interactive element after the hero and is visually prominent. On desktop, the search bar is centered horizontally and spans about 60–70% of the container width, leaving wide margins on both sides. The form itself is a single row: a large input field (occupying roughly four-fifths of the bar) and an adjacent search button (one-fifth), or a single, full-width input with an embedded icon, depending on implementation. Vertically, there is generous white space: 48–64px of margin between the bottom of the hero and the top of the search form, and 32–48px between the search form and the filter bar below. This creates a calm, clear focal step in the flow from hero to search to filters. On smaller screens, the search spans nearly 100% of the container width with consistent side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, sitting between the hero and filter bar; not sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-filter-bar sits directly below the search form, separated by roughly 32–48px of vertical space. It shares the same centered max-width container as the search. On desktop, the filter bar is a single horizontal strip spanning nearly the full container width, aligned to the left edge: filter controls (e.g., dropdowns, chips, toggles) are left-aligned, while any sort or view options can be right-aligned in the same row. Each control has comfortable horizontal spacing (16–24px between elements), and the bar itself has 16–24px of vertical padding to prevent crowding. The overall height remains relatively slim to maintain clarity yet roomy enough to be touch-friendly. The visual treatment is subtle—perhaps a very light background tint or bottom border line—to delineate it from the white page without adding visual noise. There is 24–32px of space between this bar and the results summary below, preserving the vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Ideally becomes sticky at the top of the viewport after the user scrolls past the hero and search form, remaining fixed within the page’s content column so filters are always available while scrolling the results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-results-summary appears just below the filter bar, inside the same centered content column. It is left-aligned and spans roughly 70–80% of the container width, with any supplementary controls or indicators (like a ‘clear filters’ link) aligned to the right on the same baseline if needed. Vertically, there is about 24px of space above and below the summary, making it feel like a subtle separator between filtering controls and the actual list. The text is concise and set in a smaller, secondary type style (e.g., ‘Showing 24 resources for “anxiety”’) so it informs without competing with hero or card titles. This placement allows the eye to move naturally from filter decisions straight into an understanding of what is currently being shown.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; flows with the document immediately above the resources list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-list occupies the main body of the page below the results summary. It fills the centered max-width container and uses an implicit 12-column grid. On desktop, each resource card typically spans 4 columns, resulting in a 3-up grid with comfortable horizontal gutters (24–32px) and vertical spacing between rows (32–48px). On narrower tablets, cards adjust to 6 columns (2-up); on mobile, cards span the full width of the container in a single column. A subtle, very light background tint behind the entire list area can help visually group the cards while keeping the tone soft and clean. The top of the list sits about 16–24px below the results summary, and the bottom transitions into pagination with 48–64px of spacing to signal the end of the list. The overall feeling is airy and orderly, avoiding visual clutter even when there are many items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid within the scrollable page; its height grows based on the number of items. It is the anchor region that gets replaced or obscured by loading, empty, or error states.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each resource-card is a rectangular, white card placed within the resources-list grid. It respects the 12-column system: on desktop it spans 4 columns with internal padding of 16–24px on all sides and rounded corners to maintain a friendly yet professional feel. Within the card, content is laid out vertically: at the top-left, a bold title that runs one to two lines; just below, secondary metadata (age range, topic, duration) in a smaller type; further down, an optional short description or key takeaway. At the top-right corner of the card, aligned with the title’s baseline, sits the save handler control (icon button), clearly visible but compact. The card’s vertical content is evenly spaced, with 8–16px between text blocks and 16–24px between the card’s bottom content and any CTA (e.g., ‘View resource’ link) placed at the bottom. The cards are separated from each other by consistent gaps, so even in a 3-up configuration, each card feels isolated enough to scan easily.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static elements within the grid; their dimensions respond to viewport size but keep internal spacing consistent. They do not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resource-save-handler appears inside each resource-card as a small but prominent icon button, such as a bookmark or heart. It is positioned at the top-right corner of the card’s content area, horizontally aligned with the title’s first line and inset by about 8–16px from the card’s right and top padding edges. The control measures roughly 32–40px in both width and height to remain touch-friendly, and it may have a circular or pill-shaped background when active for clarity. Its placement ensures that it is always visible without dominating the card, and it does not disrupt the main left-aligned text column. On hover or focus, it subtly elevates (shadow or color shift) while still fitting within the calm aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the card’s layout (not fixed on the viewport); always anchored to the top-right corner of its parent card and moves as the card resizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-pagination component is placed below the resources-list, with generous vertical separation: about 48–64px of space from the last row of cards. It is horizontally centered within the same max-width container, occupying about 40–60% of the width on desktop. The controls (previous/next buttons and page numbers) are arranged in a single horizontal line with evenly spaced elements, maintaining at least 16px gaps between interactive items. The overall height is modest but clear, with 16–24px of vertical padding around the controls to keep them easy to tap. This centered placement and generous whitespace visually mark the end of a result page while keeping the interface calm and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the content; it scrolls with the page and remains within the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-empty-state replaces the resources-list region whenever there are no results. It is anchored in the same centered max-width container and takes up a similar vertical footprint as a few rows of cards to maintain balance. Within this area, the content is centered horizontally, and on desktop it is also vertically centered within the visible list area when possible. A simplified version of the ink-cloud motif or a gentle illustrative icon can appear above a clear, concise heading such as ‘No resources match your filters’, followed by a brief explanation and a primary action (e.g., ‘Clear filters’) centered beneath. Vertical spacing is generous: 16–24px between illustration, heading, body text, and actions, and 48–64px margins separating this block from the filter bar above and pagination area below (if present). The result is a calm, reassuring message that feels like an intentional state, not an error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same spatial region as the resources-list; shown only when there are zero results. It is static within the flow but may visually center itself within the available vertical space of the list area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-loading-state overlays or replaces the resources-list area when data is being fetched. It is constrained to the same centered container and width as the list. On desktop, it presents either a set of skeleton cards arranged in a 2–3 column grid matching the expected card layout, or a single, centered spinner with a brief ‘Loading resources…’ message. If using skeletons, they are spaced like real cards, preserving the low-density grid feel. The vertical area used approximates one to two full screens of content so the user perceives the upcoming structure without feeling overwhelmed. A subtle dimming of the underlying list region (if re-fetching) keeps the focus on the loading indicator while not feeling harsh.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Aligned with and sized like the resources-list; may function as an overlay within that region, intercepting interactions while loading. It scrolls with the page but typically appears near the top of the list area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-error-state appears in place of the resources-list when an error occurs. It is positioned in the same centered container and width, starting roughly where the list would begin, just below the results summary. The error block is horizontally centered and uses a compact, vertically stacked layout: an icon or subtle highlight line to indicate an error, a short, direct heading (e.g., ‘We’re having trouble loading resources’), a brief explanatory sentence, and one or two actions such as ‘Try again’. Spacing between these elements is 12–16px, with 32–48px of padding above and below the block to preserve breathing room. Colors are chosen to convey importance (a soft red accent) while staying within the site’s calm, therapeutic palette.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the resources-list in the normal document flow; it is static and does not overlay the hero or filter bar. It scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The resources-url-sync component has no visual footprint; it operates behind the scenes to keep filters, search queries, pagination, and save states reflected in the URL. Its ‘layout’ effect is indirect: as it updates parameters, it can trigger the page to scroll back to the top of the results area (just above the resources-list) after major state changes such as new searches or page changes, reinforcing the top-to-bottom visual flow. By not rendering any visible UI, it preserves the clean, uncluttered aesthetic and allows the layout to remain focused on the core interactive components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and therefore non-spatial; it does not occupy any space in the layout and does not affect positioning directly, only via behavioral changes such as scrolling.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays a single resource article for parents, with full content, metadata, save/remove to reading list, print/email tools, and navigation back to the resources library or reading list.

core_features: Load and display full article details based on the resourceId URL parameter using getArticleDetail.
Show article metadata including topic, age range label, content type, estimated reading time, and created date from getArticleDetail.
Toggle saving or removing the current article from the reading list using saveResourceToReadingList and removeResourceFromReadingList, reflecting current saved state from getArticleDetail.isSaved.
Provide a link to return to the resources list (resources.html), preserving any relevant navigation context from the URL when available.
Provide a link to open the user’s reading list (reading_list.html) from the article page.
Offer basic share tools such as printing (browser print dialog) and email sharing (mailto link with the current page URL).
Offer navigation to topic-related content via links that send the user back to resources.html pre-filtered by the article’s topic or tags.
user_workflows: Viewing an article from the resources list: 1) User clicks an article link on resources.html, which navigates to article_detail.html with a resourceId URL parameter. 2) On page load, Article Detail parses resourceId and calls getArticleDetail(resourceId). 3) When data is returned, the page shows the title, body, topic, ageRangeLabel, contentType, readingTimeMinutes, createdAt, and topicTags. 4) The isSaved value from getArticleDetail determines whether the save control shows a “Save to Reading List” or “Remove from Reading List” state.

Viewing an article from the reading list: 1) User clicks an article on reading_list.html, navigating to article_detail.html with resourceId. 2) Article Detail loads the article via getArticleDetail(resourceId) and renders content as above. 3) User can then remove the article from the reading list using the same save/remove toggle, which calls removeResourceFromReadingList.

Saving an article to the reading list: 1) User is viewing an article where isSaved is false, so the save control indicates that the article is not in the reading list. 2) User clicks the save control. 3) The page disables the control (and may show a loading state), then calls saveResourceToReadingList(resourceId). 4) If success is true, the page updates its internal saved state to true and updates the control label and style accordingly. 5) If success is false, the page shows a non-blocking error message and keeps the state as not saved.

Removing an article from the reading list: 1) User is viewing an article where isSaved is true, so the control indicates the article is already in the reading list. 2) User clicks the control to remove. 3) The page disables the control temporarily and calls removeResourceFromReadingList(resourceId). 4) If success is true, the page updates saved state to false and updates the control label and style. 5) If success is false, the page displays an error message and leaves the saved state as true.

Printing or emailing the article: 1) User clicks a print button, which triggers the browser’s print dialog for the current article view; no API calls are made. 2) User clicks an email share control, which opens the default mail client with a mailto link that includes a subject derived from the article title and a body including the current article_detail.html URL; no API calls are made.

Navigating back to the resources list or reading list: 1) User clicks “Back to Resources” to go to resources.html; if the current URL includes any filter or context query parameters (other than resourceId), these can be preserved and appended to the resources.html link so the list can restore prior filters. 2) User clicks “View My Reading List” to go to reading_list.html with no additional parameters required.

Exploring related topics: 1) User clicks a topic tag or a primary topic link shown with the article. 2) The page navigates to resources.html with URL parameters that indicate which topic or tag to filter by. 3) The resources.html page is responsible for interpreting those parameters and showing a filtered list.
interactions: On page load, the controller component reads the resourceId URL parameter; if present, it calls getArticleDetail(resourceId) and manages loading and error states.
If resourceId is missing or invalid, the page displays an error state with a link back to resources.html instead of attempting API calls.

The article header area displays the article title and uses body, primaryTopic, topicTags, ageRangeLabel, contentType, readingTimeMinutes, and createdAt from getArticleDetail for article meta information.
The article content area renders the body field from getArticleDetail, preserving any structure provided in that field.
The save/remove toggle button reads the current saved state from getArticleDetail.isSaved (or from updated in-page state after a toggle) to determine its label and accessible description.
Clicking the save state toggle when the article is not saved calls saveResourceToReadingList(resourceId) and, upon a successful response, updates the saved state and UI.
Clicking the save state toggle when the article is already saved calls removeResourceFromReadingList(resourceId) and, upon a successful response, updates the saved state and UI.
If saveResourceToReadingList or removeResourceFromReadingList return success=false, the page displays the returned message near the control while leaving the previous state intact.
A print action control is available; clicking it invokes the browser print dialog for the current article view.
An email share action opens a new email draft using a mailto link with the current page URL and article title included; the user can then send it manually.
A “Back to Resources” link navigates to resources.html, optionally including any preserved query parameters that describe the previous filter or source context.
A “View My Reading List” link navigates directly to reading_list.html.

Each topic tag and the primary topic label are interactive; clicking them navigates to resources.html with URL parameters specifying the chosen topic or tag so that the resources page can show related articles.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page inspects the URL for a resourceId parameter. If resourceId is present, the page calls getArticleDetail(resourceId) once and stores the returned values (including isSaved) in the page state. The article display components are bound to this state. The isSaved field determines the initial label and appearance of the reading list toggle control. When the user clicks the toggle, the page checks the current isSaved state: if false, it calls saveResourceToReadingList(resourceId); if true, it calls removeResourceFromReadingList(resourceId). On a successful response, the page updates its in-memory isSaved value and rerenders the toggle; the URL does not change. If resourceId is absent or the API response indicates an error, the page enters an error state where the article content is hidden and a fallback navigation link to resources.html is shown. Additional URL parameters other than resourceId are treated as navigation context (for example, indicating that the user came from resources.html with certain filters); these parameters are not used in API calls but can be preserved when generating the 'Back to Resources' link so that the user is returned to a consistent view. The print and email actions do not read or alter URL parameters. Navigation to resources.html or reading_list.html uses standard href links that may include query parameters only to convey topic/tag filters or to restore prior filter context; no localStorage is used for state persistence on this page.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, data loading, error handling, and global state for the Article Detail page.
On page load, reads the resourceId URL parameter and calls getArticleDetail(resourceId). Exposes loading, success, and error states to child components.
Displays an error message and a navigation link back to the resources list if resourceId is missing or getArticleDetail fails.
Displays the article title and core metadata such as primary topic, age range label, estimated reading time, content type, and created date.
Shows the article’s primary topic and topicTags as clickable tags that navigate to resources.html filtered by the chosen topic or tag.
Renders the full article body text returned in getArticleDetail.body.

Shows a button to save or remove the article from the reading list. The label and state are determined by getArticleDetail.isSaved and updated based on responses from saveResourceToReadingList and removeResourceFromReadingList.
Displays inline success or error messages when saving or removing the article from the reading list, using the message field from save/remove API responses.
Provides navigation controls including a 'Back to Resources' link (to resources.html, with any preserved query parameters) and a 'View My Reading List' link (to reading_list.html).

Offers actions for printing the current article view and sharing via email. The print action triggers the browser print dialog; the email action opens a mail client with a mailto link including the article title and current URL.
Provides a link to view more resources on the article’s primary topic by navigating to resources.html with a topic-related query parameter.
Shows a loading state while getArticleDetail is in progress and hides when data is loaded or an error occurs.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The article detail page adopts the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a calm, spacious upper region that functions like a hero for the article title, followed by supporting metadata and tools, then the full article body, and finally supplemental navigation (related link). Within the page’s main content band, elements are organized inside a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) that sits inside full-width background bands. The article header acts as the primary focal band (analogous to the ink-cloud hero), with navigation-back-links placed just above it and utility actions (share/print, reading list toggle) aligned around the header/meta area but visually secondary to the title and content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The article detail page adopts the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a calm, spacious upper region that functions like a hero for the article title, followed by supporting metadata and tools, then the full article body, and finally supplemental navigation (related link). Within the page’s main content band, elements are organized inside a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) that sits inside full-width background bands. The article header acts as the primary focal band (analogous to the ink-cloud hero), with navigation-back-links placed just above it and utility actions (share/print, reading list toggle) aligned around the header/meta area but visually secondary to the title and content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top of the article view gives roughly 60–70% of the above-the-fold area to the article header band (the hero-like treatment for the title) and 30–40% to navigation, meta, and utility controls around it. The header band itself has generous vertical padding (48–80px top and bottom), ensuring the title and any subtitle sit in a large, calm field of color reminiscent of the ink-cloud hero. Below this, the article body occupies most of the vertical space, with wide margins and comfortable line length. Utilities and meta rows share the width with the content but are compressed vertically to keep focus on the main title and body.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays low throughout: each horizontal band (back navigation, header, meta + tools, body, footer/related link) contains only a few elements, with 24–32px of spacing between rows and 16–24px padding within interactive components. There is generous whitespace above and below the article header, around the body text, and between functional clusters (e.g., between tags and tools, between the article end and the related topic link). This mirrors the calming, therapeutic tone from the mockup and maintains easy scannability for parents and educators.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye first catches the back navigation in the top-left of the content area, then travels horizontally across any parallel links. From there, the eye is pulled downward into the large, centered article header band which acts as the main focal point (like the ink-cloud hero). Within that band, the title is either center-aligned or strongly emphasized in the main content column, holding attention. After reading the title, the user naturally moves down to the meta row (topic tags on the left, tools on the right), then flows straight into the main body text. The reading-list status message and related-topic link are treated as gentle, tertiary cues at the periphery of this flow, catching attention after or between reading segments rather than competing with the title or body.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global header, the article detail page opens into a calm, spacious content area centered on the screen. At the top of this area, a slim, left-aligned back-navigation row anchors the user in the resource library context. Just beneath it, a wide, full-width band with a soft slate-blue background stretches across the viewport; within this band, a centered content column holds the article title in large, simple typography, surrounded by generous vertical padding that echoes the original ink-cloud hero’s strong central focus and negative space. As the user scrolls slightly, this header band gives way to a clean white content area where a single horizontal row presents topic tags on the left and share/print plus reading-list toggle tools on the right, all aligned to a 12-column grid. Below this row, the article body flows down the page in a single, readable column with ample margins and relaxed line spacing. Near the bottom of the article, a subtle related-topic link appears aligned to the left, acting as a gentle next-step cue. Loading and error states temporarily replace the body region with centered feedback, while a semi-transparent overlay and spinner can appear above everything when the article is initially loading. Overall, the layout feels open, structured, and reassuring, with a strong vertical rhythm and a clear central focus on the title and content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-detail-controller serves as the main page wrapper for all article-related content. It occupies the full width of the viewport but constrains its inner content to a centered max-width container of approximately 1100–1200px, aligned with the site’s global grid. Vertically, the controller begins just below the global site header/hero and extends to the bottom of the page, providing consistent top padding of 64px before the first in-page element (navigation-back-links). It defines the vertical rhythm between sections: 32px between minor rows (e.g., tags and tools) and 64–96px between major sections (header band, meta/tools row, body, footer/related link). All other components sit within this controller in a standard top-to-bottom document flow, maintaining the calm, linear structure from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard layout container in normal document flow, providing max-width constraints and section spacing but not rendering visible chrome itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-loader governs the loading states within the main content column. When active, it reserves the vertical space where the article-header, meta, and body would appear, preventing layout shifts. In its simplest visual form, it can show skeleton lines or muted placeholder blocks centered within the content column, spanning roughly 60–80% of the column width and reaching 40–60% of the viewport height, aligned with the area where the article header and the first screen of body content would normally be. This keeps the page’s perceived structure consistent with the hero-like opening while reassuring the user that content is on its way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the normal document flow, temporarily replacing the article-header, meta, and body regions when loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-error-state appears in place of the main article content when an error occurs. It is centered within the same max-width content column, starting at roughly the same vertical position where the article-header would normally begin. The error panel occupies about 50–60% of the column width, centered horizontally, with 48–64px of padding inside and at least 64px of vertical whitespace above and below it to maintain the spacious, therapeutic feel. The message text is center-aligned, with any retry or navigation actions placed directly beneath the primary message in a single column. This ensures the user’s focus is gently drawn to the error message without clutter, while still fitting naturally into the established vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the standard article-header, meta, and body sections within the normal flow when in an error state.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header functions as the hero-like focal section for the article. It is placed directly below the navigation-back-links, separated by approximately 32px of vertical space. Visually, it is implemented as a full-width horizontal band with a calming slate-blue background, spanning edge-to-edge across the viewport. Inside this band, the content is constrained to the centered 1100–1200px container. The band’s height takes up roughly 60–70% of the visible area below the global header on first load, with 64–96px of padding at the top and bottom, creating a generous buffer around the title. The title text sits within the content column and may be center-aligned horizontally within the column (echoing the centered ink-cloud title) or left-aligned but visually dominant, occupying about 60–70% of the column width. Any subtitle or brief description sits beneath the main title with 16–24px spacing, maintaining a low-density feel. The header’s spaciousness and strong color field ensure it is the primary visual anchor before users move down into details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the normal document flow, forming a full-width color band that remains at the top of the article content and scrolls away with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-meta-topic-tags row is positioned directly beneath the article-header band, with a vertical gap of about 48px to visually separate the hero-like title area from the meta information. It sits within the centered content column on a white background. The tags appear as a horizontal list starting at the left edge of the content column, occupying roughly the left half of the grid (about 6 of 12 columns). Each tag has comfortable internal padding (8–12px vertically, 12–16px horizontally) and 8–12px spacing between tags, maintaining a light, airy feel. If tags wrap to a second line, they maintain the same left alignment, with 8–12px vertical spacing between lines. This left-anchored cluster provides an immediate contextual read for the article without overpowering the title above or the tools to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of a shared horizontal meta/tools row, aligned to the left, flowing normally with the document.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-body begins just below the meta + tools row, separated by about 32–40px of vertical space. It occupies the main content column, aligned centrally within the page and spanning roughly 8–10 of the 12 grid columns for optimal readability (leaving generous left and right margins within the 1100–1200px container). Body text follows a relaxed line length and line height to reinforce the therapeutic tone. Paragraphs are separated by 16–24px, with larger 32–40px gaps before and after major subheadings. Inline elements (like bullets or callouts) are indented but still contained within the main text width, avoiding any busy sidebars. The body area stretches vertically as needed, acting as the tallest section on the page, and is framed by comfortable whitespace above (beneath the meta row) and below (before the related-topic-link).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content region in the normal document flow, expanding vertically with the article length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-toggle sits in the same horizontal band as the article-meta-topic-tags, but aligned to the right side of the content column to balance the layout. It occupies the rightmost 3–4 grid columns, positioned either to the right of the share-print-tools or grouped closely with them. The toggle appears as a medium-sized button or pill-shaped control with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, maintaining comfortable click/tap targets. Vertically, it aligns with the center of the tags row, so the left side of the row is tags and the right side is tools and the toggle, all within the same visual band. There is at least 16px of horizontal spacing between the toggle and the share-print-tools cluster to prevent crowding. This placement keeps the toggle immediately discoverable but visually subordinate to the article title and body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned control within the meta/tools row, participating in normal flow and moving with the page on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-status-message appears directly beneath the reading-list-toggle, left-aligned with the toggle’s label. It is placed in a narrow horizontal strip that occupies the same right side of the content column, starting about 8–12px below the toggle. The message text uses a smaller, muted font and wraps within that right-side area without extending into the tag region on the left. Vertically, this status strip does not introduce a large new section; instead, it subtly extends the meta/tools band by 16–24px, maintaining the low-density feel while providing immediate, local feedback about the reading list state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline feedback beneath the toggle in normal document flow; appears and disappears without altering the larger layout structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-back-links row is the first in-page element within the article-detail-controller, positioned approximately 64px below the global site header. It is a slim horizontal band confined to the content column on a white background. The primary back link (e.g., “← Back to Resources”) is aligned to the left edge of the content column and occupies no more than 30–40% of the column width. If there are multiple links (e.g., back to library and back to reading list), they are arranged inline from left to right with 16–24px spacing between them, preserving the light, linear feel. There is ample whitespace above (24–32px) and below (32px) this row, distinguishing it as a quiet orienting element before the bold article-header band begins. The typography is modest relative to the article title, ensuring the back links guide but do not compete for attention.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, top-of-content navigational row in normal flow; scrolls away as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The share-print-tools are positioned in the same horizontal band as the article-meta-topic-tags and reading-list-toggle, occupying the upper-right section of the content column. They sit just to the left of or directly adjacent to the reading-list-toggle, forming a small cluster of icons or compact buttons. This cluster spans roughly 2–3 grid columns and aligns vertically with the tags on the left. Each icon/button has 8–12px of internal padding and at least 8px of horizontal spacing between items. The entire tools cluster is aligned to the right edge of the content column, with 16–24px of horizontal gap separating it from the reading-list-toggle if they are not grouped. This arrangement keeps tools easily accessible but visually understated, making them secondary to reading the article itself.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-aligned utility cluster within the meta/tools row, participating in normal document flow and scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-topic-link is placed near the bottom of the article content, acting as a gentle next-step cue once the reader finishes the article. It appears within the same content column as the body text, aligned to the left edge, and separated from the last paragraph of the article-body by 48–64px of vertical space to clearly mark the transition from primary content to secondary navigation. The link text may be styled slightly larger or bolder than body text but remains restrained. It spans no more than 60% of the column width to keep it light and avoids additional surrounding elements so the area stays uncluttered. Below the link, there is at least 64px of bottom padding before any page footer or subsequent sections, maintaining the open, calming layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Simple navigation link within the normal document flow, positioned at the end of the article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-loading-indicator appears when the article is initially being fetched or during major state transitions. Visually, it is centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport, overlaying the existing content area. It may present as a simple spinner or progress indicator, placed inside a small, unobtrusive circular or square container. The overlay itself is a very light, semi-transparent veil that covers the entire article-detail-controller region but allows the underlying structure (header band, etc.) to remain faintly visible, reinforcing continuity. This indicator does not modify the layout of underlying components; instead, it floats above them until loading completes, at which point it disappears to reveal the stable, spacious article layout beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, appearing above all article components during loading and not affecting their underlying layout.

name: Saved Reading List
title: Saved Reading List
description: Page where parents can view, open, and manage their saved educational resources and articles.

core_features: Load the user’s saved reading list using getSavedReadingList and display each resource with title, primary topic, age range label, content type, and estimated reading time.
Allow users to open a saved resource by clicking its title, navigating to article_detail.html with the appropriate resourceId URL parameter.
Allow users to remove individual resources from their saved reading list by calling removeResourceFromReadingList and updating the displayed list.
Provide optional client-side sorting and filtering (e.g., by content type, topic, age range, reading time) whose state is reflected in URL query parameters.
Handle empty states when there are no saved resources (or when filters result in zero matches) and provide navigation to resources.html to discover more resources.
Support basic pagination or chunked display of saved items, controlled through URL parameters so the user can return to the same portion of their list.
user_workflows: View saved resources: 1) User navigates to reading_list.html. 2) On page load, the page-init controller reads any URL query parameters (sort, filters, page). 3) The page calls getSavedReadingList with no parameters. 4) When data is returned, the controller applies client-side sorting and filtering based on the URL parameters. 5) The saved-reading-list-results component renders the current page of items with title, primaryTopic, ageRangeLabel, contentType, and readingTimeMinutes visible.

Open a saved resource: 1) User finds an item in the list. 2) User clicks the resource title (or an explicit 'Open' action linked to the same behavior). 3) The click handler navigates the browser to article_detail.html with a query string like article_detail.html?resourceId={resourceId}. 4) The user reads the full article or resource on the detail page.

Remove a resource from the list: 1) User locates the resource they no longer want to keep. 2) User clicks a 'Remove' action associated with that item. 3) The remove-resource-confirmation component optionally asks for confirmation. 4) On confirmation, the handler calls removeResourceFromReadingList with the item’s resourceId. 5) If the API response indicates success, the item is removed from in-memory state and the list is re-rendered (or getSavedReadingList is called again). 6) If this was the last item, the empty state is shown instead.

Filter and sort saved resources: 1) User adjusts controls in the saved-reading-list-toolbar (e.g., selects a content type, chooses a sort option). 2) The toolbar updates the browser URL query parameters (e.g., ?contentType=article&sort=readingTime_desc&page=1) using history.replaceState or a full reload. 3) The page-init controller reacts to the URL change (either via reload or by listening to popstate) and re-applies the filters and sorting to the in-memory list from getSavedReadingList. 4) The results component updates to reflect the new ordering and filtered subset.

Use pagination to browse large lists: 1) After initial load, if the total saved items exceed the per-page display limit, pagination-controls determine which subset is visible (e.g., items for page=2). 2) When a user clicks 'Next' or a specific page number, pagination-controls update the page query parameter (e.g., ?page=2&sort=title_asc). 3) The controller re-renders the list with only the items for that page. 4) Using the browser back button restores previous page and filter state based on the URL.

Recover from an empty list and discover more resources: 1) If getSavedReadingList returns an empty array, or all items are filtered out by current filters, the saved-reading-list-empty-state component is shown. 2) The component displays a link or button that navigates to resources.html (optionally including a from=reading_list query parameter). 3) User clicks this link and is taken to resources.html to browse and save new resources. 4) After saving items elsewhere, returning to reading_list.html will show the updated list upon the next call to getSavedReadingList.
interactions: On page load (reading_list.html), a controller component triggers getSavedReadingList, manages loading and error states, and coordinates URL parameter parsing.
Clicking a saved resource title in saved-reading-list-results triggers navigation to article_detail.html with the clicked item’s resourceId as a URL query parameter.
Clicking a remove action for an item triggers the remove-resource-confirmation logic, and upon confirmation, calls removeResourceFromReadingList(resourceId) and updates the in-memory list and UI.

Changing sorting options in saved-reading-list-toolbar (e.g., selecting sort by readingTimeMinutes ascending or descending) updates the sort query parameter in the URL and re-applies sorting to the loaded data.

Changing filters (content type, age range, or topic text) in the toolbar updates corresponding query parameters (e.g., contentType, ageRange, topic) and re-filters the already loaded list client-side.
Clicking pagination controls (previous, next, specific page number) updates the page query parameter and updates which slice of the saved resources is displayed.
Clicking a 'Clear filters' action in the toolbar removes filter-related query parameters from the URL and resets the list to show all saved results.
Clicking a 'Discover more resources' link or button navigates directly to resources.html (optionally with a from=reading_list query parameter for context).

Using the browser’s back/forward buttons restores prior combinations of sort, filter, and page parameters, and the controller re-applies these to the loaded list without additional API calls (unless a full page reload occurs).
page_functionality.state_logic: The Saved Reading List page itself does not require any mandatory URL parameters, but it uses optional query parameters to control how the list from getSavedReadingList is presented. On initial load, the controller parses the current window.location.search and extracts supported parameters such as: sort (e.g., title_asc, title_desc, readingTime_asc, readingTime_desc, topic_asc), contentType (matching ResourceItem.content_type enums), ageRangeLabel (matching the ageRangeLabel field or a partial string), topic (applied as a case-insensitive substring match against primaryTopic), page (integer index for pagination, defaulting to 1 if missing or invalid), and pageSize (optional integer to override a default items-per-page value). These parameters are purely presentational and do not change which data is requested from the API; getSavedReadingList is always called without arguments and returns the complete saved list for the user. After data is retrieved, the page applies the following sequence based on URL state: 1) Filter the array client-side according to topic, contentType, and ageRangeLabel conditions. 2) Sort the filtered array based on the sort parameter, with a deterministic fallback order when sort is missing or invalid. 3) Apply pagination by selecting the subset of items corresponding to (page, pageSize). Whenever the user changes any filter, sort, or pagination control, the components update the query string (e.g., via history.pushState or a full page navigation) so that the current state is encoded in the URL. On each navigation or popstate event, the controller re-parses the query parameters and reapplies the same filter/sort/page logic to the already loaded data. If getSavedReadingList needs to be re-run (e.g., after removing an item or on a full reload), the page repeats the same state logic so the user’s view is consistent with the URL. Navigation to article_detail.html passes resourceId in the query string (article_detail.html?resourceId={id}), and navigation to resources.html may optionally include a from parameter (resources.html?from=reading_list) for external pages to interpret, without affecting this page’s internal logic.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, calls getSavedReadingList, parses URL parameters, maintains in-memory list and UI state (loading, error, filters, sorting, pagination), and refreshes data after removals.

Provides controls for sorting, filtering, clearing filters, and quick navigation to discover more resources. It reads the current state from URL parameters (provided by the controller) and updates the URL when users change options.

Displays the current page of saved resources after filters and sorting have been applied, showing each item’s title, primaryTopic, ageRangeLabel, contentType, and readingTimeMinutes, and exposing actions to open or remove each resource.

Displays when there are no saved resources returned by getSavedReadingList or when the current filters yield zero results, and offers a navigation action to resources.html so the user can find and save new resources.

Manages pagination for the saved resources list by calculating total pages from the filtered results and the configured pageSize, rendering page selectors, and updating the page parameter in the URL when the user changes pages.

Handles the confirmation flow for removing a saved resource. When invoked for a specific resourceId, it confirms user intent, calls removeResourceFromReadingList, then notifies the controller to update the list or refetch data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom flow, mirroring the mockup: global header at the very top (logo left, nav right, already part of the site), then immediately beneath it a full-width hero band dedicated to the Saved Reading List title with a centered ink-cloud shape and centered title text. Below the hero, the main content area runs in a single column within a centered max-width container, stacking in this order: a subtle section label or heading (handled by the page controller), the saved-reading-list-toolbar as a horizontal strip, the saved-reading-list-results or saved-reading-list-empty-state occupying the main vertical space, and finally the saved-reading-list-pagination-controls anchored at the bottom of the content area. The remove-resource-confirmation sits above all content as a modal overlay when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom flow, mirroring the mockup: global header at the very top (logo left, nav right, already part of the site), then immediately beneath it a full-width hero band dedicated to the Saved Reading List title with a centered ink-cloud shape and centered title text. Below the hero, the main content area runs in a single column within a centered max-width container, stacking in this order: a subtle section label or heading (handled by the page controller), the saved-reading-list-toolbar as a horizontal strip, the saved-reading-list-results or saved-reading-list-empty-state occupying the main vertical space, and finally the saved-reading-list-pagination-controls anchored at the bottom of the content area. The remove-resource-confirmation sits above all content as a modal overlay when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header uses approximately 15–20% of the viewport height, consistent with the mockup. The Saved Reading List hero occupies a prominent band directly under this, roughly 30–40% of the remaining initial viewport height on desktop, with large vertical padding and generous side margins inside a full-width slate-blue background. The main content area beneath the hero fills the rest of the page, with a centered content container of about 1100–1200px width. Within this container, the toolbar occupies a compact horizontal zone (about 10–15% of the vertical content area), the results or empty state occupy the majority (around 60–70%), and the pagination controls sit in a slimmer band at the bottom (about 5–10%), with 64–96px vertical spacing between major sections to maintain the calm, open feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and calm: few elements per horizontal row, and generous white space around each cluster. The hero contains only the ink-cloud illustration and the main title, with ample negative space above, below, and to the sides. The toolbar presents a small set of controls in a single row with 16–24px internal padding, spaced out to avoid visual clutter. The results list uses vertically stacked resource cards with clear separation (24–32px gaps), and the empty state centers a single, visually clear message and illustration. Pagination controls are compact and unobtrusive, with enough spacing to be easily clickable but not visually heavy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the global header logo in the top-left, moves rightward across the navigation, then is drawn downward to the full-width hero. Within the hero, the eye lands on the central ink-cloud and its bold, centered title. From there, the eye moves straight down into the main content container, first registering the section label and toolbar, then scanning downward through the vertically stacked resources. The flow is predominantly vertical and calm, with a strong central focal point (the hero) followed by a clear, linear progression: filters, then content, then pagination. When the remove-resource-confirmation appears, it takes over the focal point by dimming the background and centering the dialog, temporarily interrupting the vertical flow in a controlled, deliberate way.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s existing white header bar, a full-width slate-blue hero band spans edge to edge, visually anchoring the Saved Reading List page. In the center of this colored band, an organic ink-cloud shape floats horizontally, occupying roughly the central third of the viewport width, with generous padding above and below it so it feels calm and unhurried. The page title 'Saved Reading List' sits centered on two lines inside this ink-cloud, in clear, simple type. Beneath the hero, the background returns to white and a centered content container (about 1100–1200px wide) holds all functional elements. At the top of this container, there is comfortable vertical breathing room before the content controls begin. The toolbar stretches horizontally across most of the container, with filters and sorting options laid out in a single, uncluttered row. Below that, the main area is dedicated either to a vertically stacked list of resource cards aligned to the left within the container or, if there are no items, a centered, friendly empty-state message. The cards have ample vertical spacing and soft margins so the column feels light and easy to scan. At the bottom of the list, pagination controls sit centered in the container with enough space above and below to feel distinct but not detached. At any point, if a user chooses to remove an item, a semi-transparent overlay dims the entire page and a moderate-sized confirmation dialog appears dead center, floating above the hero and content, maintaining the simple, therapeutic aesthetic with plenty of internal padding and minimal, focused actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the overall Saved Reading List page region directly beneath the global site header. It establishes two major vertical zones: a full-width hero band at the top and a main content section below. The hero band spans the entire viewport width and approximately 30–40% of the viewport height on desktop, with a slate-blue background. Centered within this band, the controller positions the ink-cloud illustration horizontally and vertically within the inner content area (not touching the edges), ensuring generous top and bottom padding (roughly 64–96px). The page title is centered inside the ink-cloud on two lines. Below the hero, the controller creates a white-background main content section with a centered max-width container (about 1100–1200px) that aligns with the site-wide grid. Within this container, it stacks the toolbar at the top, then either the results list or the empty state in the middle, and the pagination controls at the bottom, maintaining 32–64px of vertical spacing between each. It also ensures that on taller screens, the main content section can extend with scrolling while the hero remains at the top of the document flow, not fixed, preserving a calm, natural scroll experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static layout container that controls the full-width hero band and the centered main content column; it scrolls with the page and does not use fixed or sticky positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The toolbar sits at the top of the main content section immediately beneath the hero, aligned within the centered max-width content container. There is a generous gap of about 64px between the bottom edge of the hero band and the top of the toolbar, echoing the breathing room observed in the mockup between major sections. The toolbar spans approximately 100% of the content container width (leaving standard page margins to the viewport edges) and has an internal height defined by 16–24px vertical padding. Within the toolbar, controls (e.g., a search field, filter dropdowns, and sort options) are arranged in a single horizontal row: primary controls such as search and core filters are left-aligned, while secondary controls like sort or view toggles sit on the right side of the same row, reflecting the left-to-right scan from key controls to refinements. The toolbar background remains white to blend with the content area, but it may be visually separated with a subtle bottom border or soft shadow. On larger screens it occupies roughly the visual weight of a slim strip across the top of the content area; on scroll, it moves with the page rather than remaining sticky.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal band inside the main content column, full-width relative to the container, scrolling with the rest of the page; not sticky or floating.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the main central portion of the page’s content area directly below the toolbar. There is a comfortable vertical gap (approximately 32–48px) between the bottom of the toolbar and the top of the first resource card. The list is positioned within the same centered max-width container, and each resource is presented as a card aligned with the left edge of the container, forming a single vertical column that is easy to scan. Each card spans about 100% of the content container width on desktop, with internal padding of 16–24px, a white background, and subtle separation (light border or shadow) from the page background. Cards are separated from each other by 24–32px of vertical spacing, contributing to the low-density, calm feel. The right side of each card aligns consistently, but the overall list leaves generous white space on both sides within the full viewport, matching the mockup’s central focus pattern. The list extends downward and can run multiple screen heights, supporting natural scrolling; pagination controls appear only after the last visible card, ensuring that the user’s attention is first fully on the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A vertically scrolling stack of full-width cards within the centered container; it grows downward with content and does not use fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the same spatial zone as the results list and only appears when there are no saved resources. It is positioned below the toolbar with a similar vertical offset (about 32–48px), but instead of multiple cards, it presents a single, centered content block within the main container. This block is horizontally centered and spans roughly 50–60% of the container width, leaving ample white space to the left and right. Inside the block, there is an optional simple illustration or icon aligned above a concise message, with the primary message title centered and any supporting text stacked beneath, also centered. A primary call-to-action button (e.g., to browse resources) is placed centrally under the text, with 16–24px padding and generous space around it. The overall empty-state block has substantial internal padding (24–32px) and is positioned so that, on an average desktop viewport, it sits roughly in the middle of the visible content area beneath the hero, giving it a reassuring, focused presence without feeling cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the results list; it resides in the same vertical region, centered within the main content container, and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are located at the bottom of the main content column, directly beneath the last resource card when results are present. There is a clearly perceptible gap (about 32–48px) between the last card and the pagination row, giving a sense of closure to the list before the navigation options. The pagination controls are horizontally centered within the max-width container and occupy a compact horizontal band. Individual page buttons, next/previous arrows, or load-more actions are arranged in a single row, with 8–16px spacing between interactive elements to maintain clarity and touch-friendly targets. The overall height of the pagination area is modest, with 16–24px vertical padding above and below the controls, preserving the low visual weight while keeping them discoverable. On shorter pages, this area appears just above the bottom of the viewport when scrolled to the end; on longer pages, it anchors the end of the content column. It does not overlap or float over content but remains part of the natural vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static row anchored at the bottom of the content column, centered within the container; it scrolls into view at the end of the results and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-resource-confirmation appears as a modal dialog centered in the viewport, overlaying the entire page, including the hero and the main content. When active, a semi-transparent dark overlay covers the full browser window from edge to edge, softly dimming the slate-blue hero band and the white content area beneath it. The dialog itself is a medium-sized rectangular card positioned at the exact center of the viewport, occupying roughly 30–40% of the viewport width and auto-sizing in height based on its content, but typically not exceeding the central third of the viewport height. It uses a white background with rounded corners and substantial internal padding (24–32px) to match the spacious, therapeutic aesthetic. The confirmation title is placed at the top of the dialog, left-aligned within the card, with a short explanatory message beneath. Action buttons (e.g., Cancel and Remove) are aligned horizontally at the bottom-right of the dialog, with 16–24px spacing between them, while preserving generous empty space around the text and buttons. On scroll, the background content can remain fixed or subtly locked, but the dialog itself remains fixed at the center of the viewport, ensuring the user’s focus stays on this critical decision.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position modal centered in the viewport with a full-screen dimmed overlay behind it; it sits above all other components in the z-order and does not move with page scrolling while active.

name: Groups & Classes Listing
title: Groups & Classes
description: Search, filter, sort, and browse available group programs and classes, then navigate to a selected group’s detail page.

core_features: Initialize filters (age group, locations, price ranges, date range presets) by calling getGroupFilterOptions on page load.
Accept incoming URL parameters (query, ageGroup, startDate, endDate, maxPrice, sortBy, relatedServiceId) and apply them as the initial search state.
Provide a search field for free-text group searches (e.g., by topic or group type) bound to the query parameter.
Offer filter controls for age group, date range (using presets and/or custom dates), price (maxPrice), and location, all synchronized with URL parameters.
Provide a sort control bound to sortBy, with options such as sorting by soonest start date or price.
Call searchGroupPrograms with the current search, filter, sort, and pagination state to retrieve matching group programs.
Display the returned groups as clickable cards showing key attributes (title, age range label, schedule summary, start/end dates, price per child, location name, active status).
Support pagination by calling searchGroupPrograms with page and pageSize, and allow the user to move between pages.
Enable navigation to group_detail.html with the selected groupProgramId when the user clicks a group card.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Initial load from navigation without pre-applied filters:
1. User clicks a “Groups & Classes” navigation item elsewhere on the site without URL parameters.
2. Browser loads groups.html with no query parameters.
3. The page calls getGroupFilterOptions to fetch available age group, location, price range, and date range preset options.
4. The page derives initial state from the (empty) URL: query, ageGroup, startDate, endDate, maxPrice, sortBy, relatedServiceId are all unset.
5. The page sets default values for sortBy and page/pageSize internally (without writing static data into the dataset) and calls searchGroupPrograms with these initial parameters.
6. The list of groups returned by searchGroupPrograms is rendered as cards, and the pagination component is initialized according to totalResults, page, and pageSize.
7. User sees unfiltered results with default sorting and can begin interacting with search and filters.

Workflow 2 – Deep-linked filtered search from another page:
1. User clicks a contextual link on index.html or service_detail.html that navigates to groups.html with one or more URL parameters set (e.g., query, ageGroup, maxPrice, startDate, endDate, relatedServiceId).
2. Browser loads groups.html with these URL parameters in place.
3. The page calls getGroupFilterOptions to populate filter controls.
4. The page parses URL parameters and applies them to the search form and filter controls (e.g., sets the search field to the query value, selects the matching ageGroup, selects or pre-fills date range and maxPrice, and reflects sortBy if present).
5. If query is not present but relatedServiceId is present, the page may map relatedServiceId into the query value before calling searchGroupPrograms so the backend can interpret it as a service-specific filter.
6. The page calls searchGroupPrograms with the derived query, ageGroup, date range, maxPrice, sortBy, and pagination parameters.
7. The resulting filtered list of groups is displayed, and the user can adjust filters or search further; any subsequent changes update the URL.

Workflow 3 – Searching and changing filters on the listing:
1. User types text into the search field and submits the form.
2. The page updates the URL’s query parameter to match the search text, resets page to the first page, and preserves other filter parameters (ageGroup, startDate, endDate, maxPrice, sortBy, relatedServiceId).
3. The page calls searchGroupPrograms with updated query and the current filter/sort/pagination parameters.
4. When searchGroupPrograms returns, the list of group cards updates to show new results, and the results summary component reflects the new totalResults.
5. Similarly, when the user changes any filter (age group, date range, maxPrice, location) or sort option, the page updates the corresponding URL parameters, resets page to the first page, and re-calls searchGroupPrograms with the new parameters.
6. User can repeatedly adjust search and filters, with each change reflected in both the UI controls and the URL.

Workflow 4 – Pagination through results:
1. After a search or filter action, the user sees that there are multiple pages of results (based on totalResults and pageSize from searchGroupPrograms).
2. User clicks a pagination control (e.g., “Next”, “Previous”, or a specific page number).
3. The page updates the page parameter in the URL to the selected page and keeps all other parameters (query, ageGroup, startDate, endDate, maxPrice, sortBy, relatedServiceId) unchanged.
4. The page calls searchGroupPrograms with the new page value and existing search/filter/sort values.
5. When results return, the group list updates to show the selected page of groups while filters, search, and sort controls remain as previously set.

Workflow 5 – Navigating to a group’s detail page:
1. User reviews the list of group cards returned by searchGroupPrograms.
2. User clicks on a specific group card.
3. The page reads the groupProgramId from the clicked card’s bound data.
4. The browser navigates to group_detail.html with groupProgramId set as a URL parameter.
5. The new page (outside the scope of this design) can then load and display detailed information for that group.

Workflow 6 – Using date range presets and custom dates:
1. From the filter controls, user selects a date range preset loaded from getGroupFilterOptions.dateRangePresets.
2. The component reads the selected preset’s startDate and endDate values and updates the URL parameters startDate and endDate accordingly, clearing or updating any conflicting date values.
3. The page resets pagination to the first page and calls searchGroupPrograms with updated startDate and endDate.
4. If the user instead sets a custom date range, the component updates startDate and endDate in the URL based on the custom input, then triggers searchGroupPrograms similarly.

Workflow 7 – Handling empty or inactive results:
1. User applies a combination of filters that returns zero results from searchGroupPrograms (totalResults is zero or groups array is empty).
2. The list component displays no group cards and may show a functional, non-marketing notice bound only to the fact that the groups array is empty.
3. User adjusts filters or clears some parameters (e.g., removes maxPrice or widens dates) via the filter controls.
4. Each adjustment updates URL parameters and triggers a new call to searchGroupPrograms until matching groups are returned.
interactions: Typing in the search field and submitting the form triggers an update of the query URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and calls searchGroupPrograms with the new query and current filters/sort.
Changing the age group filter updates the ageGroup URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and calls searchGroupPrograms with the selected age group and other current parameters.
Selecting a date range preset updates the startDate and endDate URL parameters using the preset’s values, resets page, and calls searchGroupPrograms.
Editing custom date range inputs updates startDate and endDate in the URL, resets page, and calls searchGroupPrograms.
Adjusting the price filter updates the maxPrice URL parameter, resets page, and calls searchGroupPrograms.
Selecting a location filter updates the locationId representation in the URL (e.g., via a parameter), resets page, and calls searchGroupPrograms.
Changing the sort option updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page, and calls searchGroupPrograms while keeping existing query and filters.
Clicking a pagination control updates the page URL parameter and calls searchGroupPrograms with the chosen page and unchanged other parameters.
Clicking a group card reads the bound groupProgramId and navigates to group_detail.html with groupProgramId set as a URL parameter.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s functional state is fully derived from and synchronized with URL parameters plus interface responses, without relying on localStorage.

On initial load, the page reads the following URL parameters:
- query: string controlling the free-text search input and the query parameter to searchGroupPrograms.
- ageGroup: string controlling the selected age group filter and the ageGroup parameter to searchGroupPrograms.
- startDate and endDate: strings representing the current date range filter, passed to searchGroupPrograms.
- maxPrice: number representing the maximum price filter, passed as maxPrice to searchGroupPrograms.
- sortBy: string representing the active sort option, passed as sortBy to searchGroupPrograms.
- relatedServiceId: string used to contextualize the listing. If query is not set, relatedServiceId can be assigned to query (or appended to it) before calling searchGroupPrograms so that the backend can filter based on the related service.

Pagination state is managed via URL parameters as well:
- page: number indicating the current page of results, passed to searchGroupPrograms.
- pageSize: number indicating how many results per page should be requested, passed to searchGroupPrograms. If not present in the URL, a default pageSize is applied internally, and may optionally be written to the URL once determined.

State synchronization rules:
- After every user-initiated change (search submit, filter change, sort change, pagination), the page updates the appropriate URL parameters and then re-calls searchGroupPrograms using the full current state.
- When query, ageGroup, startDate, endDate, maxPrice, locationId, or sortBy change, the page parameter is reset to the first page before calling searchGroupPrograms.
- When only page changes (pagination), other parameters remain unchanged.
- The results (totalResults, page, pageSize, and groups array) returned from searchGroupPrograms are treated as the single source of truth for what is rendered in the listing, results summary, and pagination controls.
- The filter options (ageGroups, locations, priceRanges, dateRangePresets) from getGroupFilterOptions are treated as the source of truth for which filter values are selectable. URL parameter values that do not match available options are handled gracefully (e.g., UI may show them as unselected while still passing them to searchGroupPrograms, or may clear them when the user interacts with the control).

The browser’s back and forward buttons naturally manipulate URL parameters. The page reacts to these navigation events by re-reading URL parameters and re-triggering searchGroupPrograms (and, if necessary, getGroupFilterOptions if not yet loaded), ensuring that state is always consistent with the URL.

components.functionality: On page load (and any hard refresh), calls getGroupFilterOptions to retrieve available age group options, location options, price ranges, and date range presets. Exposes this data to the filter components and ensures filters are not rendered with static options.

Displays and manages the free-text search input. Initializes its value from the query URL parameter (or from relatedServiceId if query is absent), and on submit updates the URL’s query parameter, resets pagination, and triggers a new searchGroupPrograms call.

Provides interactive controls for age group, date range (presets and custom), price, and location. Initializes selected values from URL parameters, maps filter selections to URL parameters (ageGroup, startDate, endDate, maxPrice, locationId), and triggers searchGroupPrograms with updated filters whenever a control changes.

Manages sort selection for the listing. Initializes from the sortBy URL parameter or an internal default if absent, updates sortBy in the URL when changed, resets pagination, and triggers searchGroupPrograms with the new sort option.

Central component that composes current URL-derived state (query, ageGroup, startDate, endDate, maxPrice, locationId, sortBy, page, pageSize) and calls searchGroupPrograms. Provides the returned totalResults, page, pageSize, and groups list to child components for rendering summary, list, and pagination.

Displays a functional summary of the current result set, such as the number of groups found and which filters are active, based solely on searchGroupPrograms.totalResults, current page, pageSize, and URL parameters. Does not include any static or example group data.

Renders each group returned by searchGroupPrograms as a clickable card. Each card is bound to the group’s groupProgramId, title, groupType, ageRangeLabel, startDate, endDate, scheduleSummary, pricePerChild, locationName, and isActive flag.
