There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the weekly-summary-panel and the top of this sidebar. The sidebar extends downward and can be significantly taller than the summary panel, accommodating a scrollable list of saved plans if needed. Visually, it appears as a taller card with a white background, subtle border or shadow, and generous interior padding. Each saved plan entry has ample line height and vertical spacing (at least 12–16px between items) to keep the list from appearing crowded. The sidebar feels clearly secondary to the week view: it is narrower, offset to the right, and begins lower in the visual hierarchy, but still easily discoverable when scanning vertically down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block in the right-hand column. The internal list may scroll if there are many saved plans, while the overall column scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, this sidebar reflows to full width and appears below both the week view and weekly summary, separated by generous vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-status-banner appears directly beneath the planner-header-toolbar within the same centered content container, acting as a slim, full-width notification band that spans the entire container width (close to 100% of the 1100–1200px, respecting side padding). There is a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px between the toolbar’s bottom edge and the top of the banner, preserving the airy feel while visually tying the two together. The banner’s height is relatively small, around 40–56px, with 12–16px of vertical padding and a calm but noticeable background color (a soft tinted hue rather than a harsh alert color) to reflect the therapeutic tone. Text is centered vertically within the band and can be left-aligned with a simple, unobtrusive icon if needed. When present, the banner creates a gentle horizontal line across the page, similar to a subtle section label in the mockup, drawing the eye momentarily before the user moves down to the main planner grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that appears and disappears within the normal document flow, pushing other content down when active. It does not stick to the viewport; it scrolls away with the rest of the content to keep the layout feeling calm and non-intrusive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-link-block is positioned near the bottom of the planner page, acting as a quiet navigational bridge to the broader therapy services section. It appears after the main planner workspace—specifically, below the lowest point of either the weekly grid or saved-plans-sidebar, whichever is taller. There is a generous vertical margin of about 64–96px of white space above it, giving the sense of a clear separation from the working area. The block itself is centered within the content container and may span about 40–60% of the container width, aligning with the mockup’s centered all-caps sub-heading ('YOUR SPACE THEATRE'). The link text is set in uppercase or small caps, horizontally centered, with sufficient padding (16–24px above and below) so it reads as a distinct, yet understated call to action. There may be a subtle divider line above it or a very light background tint to suggest it’s a footer-like section without adding visual weight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block at the end of the content flow. It remains centered and non-sticky, serving as a final focal point when users reach the bottom of the page.

name: Donate / Support Us
title: Donate / Support Us
description: Page for configuring and submitting a donation or pledge to a selected fund, with dedication and contact options.

core_features: Load donation page content (impact overview, default gift type, suggested amounts, and available funds) using getDonationPageContent.
Preconfigure the selected fund and amount based on optional URL parameters (fundId, amount).
Let users choose gift type (one-time or recurring) and enter a donation amount.
Let users select a specific active fund to support from a dynamically loaded list.
Let users add an optional dedication (none / in honor of / in memory of) and honoree name.
Collect donor contact information (name, phone, email) and preferred contact method.
Validate required fields and enforce simple logical rules (e.g., honoree name required when a dedication type is selected).
Submit the pledge via submitDonationPledge and display confirmation and next-step information from the response.
Provide navigation to an information page about impact via a “Learn how your gift helps” link to about.html.
user_workflows:
Configure a new pledge from navigation or direct link:
1. User opens donate.html (via header, footer, or CTA link).
2. Page reads URL parameters fundId and amount (if present).
3. Page calls getDonationPageContent to load impactOverview, defaultGiftType, suggestedAmounts, and the list of funds.
4. After data loads, the page selects the default gift type from defaultGiftType unless the user changes it.
5. The selected fund is initialized from fundId (if it matches an active fund); otherwise, a default fund (e.g., first active fund) is selected.
6. The amount field is prefilled with the URL amount value if it is present and valid; otherwise, the amount is left empty.
7. User reviews the impactOverview and (optionally) clicks “Learn how your gift helps” to visit about.html.
8. User chooses gift type (one-time or recurring) and enters an amount.
9. User selects a fund from the loaded funds list.
10. User optionally selects a dedication type and enters an honoree name if applicable.
11. User fills in name, phone, email, and chooses a preferred contact method.
12. User submits the pledge form.
13. Page validates inputs; if valid, it calls submitDonationPledge with the form values.
14. On success, the page shows a confirmation message using message and nextStepsText from the response and may display the returned donationPledgeId reference.
15. On failure (success=false or network error), an error state/message is shown and the form remains editable.

Using a campaign-specific link with preset fund and amount:
1. User clicks a campaign CTA (e.g., from index.html) linking to donate.html with query parameters (e.g., ?fundId=...&amount=...).
2. donate.html loads and reads the URL parameters.
3. After getDonationPageContent resolves, the component attempts to match fundId to an active fund; if found, that fund is preselected.
4. The amount input is prefilled with the parsed amount parameter if valid.
5. The user may adjust gift type, amount, fund, dedication, and contact details as desired before submitting.
6. Submission and feedback proceed as in the general workflow.

Returning to learn more about impact:
1. User is on donate.html and wants more detail about how gifts are used.
2. User clicks the “Learn how your gift helps” interaction.
3. Browser navigates to about.html (no API call required for this interaction).
4. If the user returns to donate.html (e.g., back button), the page re-runs the initial load logic (URL parameters + getDonationPageContent) and restores the form to its initial or current state as implemented.
interactions:
Page load interaction:
- Trigger: donate.html is loaded.
- Behaviour: Read URL parameters fundId and amount. Invoke getDonationPageContent. When data is returned, initialize in-page state (selected gift type, fund, amount, and impact overview). Render all interactive components.

Gift type selection interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks on a gift type option (e.g., one-time or recurring).
- Behaviour: Update internal state giftType. Reflect the selected option visually. This value is later passed as giftType to submitDonationPledge. Does not change the URL.

Fund selection interaction:
- Trigger: User changes the selected fund in the fund dropdown.
- Behaviour: Update internal state fundId with the chosen fund’s identifier (from getDonationPageContent.funds). This value is passed as fundId to submitDonationPledge.

Amount entry interaction:
- Trigger: User types into or edits the donation amount input.
- Behaviour: Update internal state amount. On blur or on submit, validate that amount is a valid positive number. Display validation errors if invalid. The validated numeric value is passed as amount to submitDonationPledge.

Dedication selection and honoree entry:
- Trigger: User chooses a dedication type (none / in honor of / in memory of).
- Behaviour: Update internal state dedicationType. If dedicationType is none, clear and disable the honoreeName field. If dedicationType is not none, enable the honoreeName field and require non-empty input before submission.
- Trigger: User types into the honoree name field.
- Behaviour: Update internal state honoreeName.

Donor contact information entry:
- Trigger: User types into donor name, phone, or email fields.
- Behaviour: Update donorName, donorPhone, donorEmail in state. Validate presence and basic format (e.g., email pattern) on blur and/or before submission.

Preferred contact method selection:
- Trigger: User selects a contact method option (email, phone, sms).
- Behaviour: Update internal state preferredContactMethod. This value is passed to submitDonationPledge.

Form submission interaction:
- Trigger: User activates the main submission control (e.g., clicks "Submit pledge").
- Behaviour:
  1. Run client-side validation for required fields: fundId, giftType, amount (positive), dedication constraints, donorName, donorPhone, donorEmail, preferredContactMethod.
  2. If validation fails, prevent API call and show inline errors.
  3. If validation passes, call submitDonationPledge with the current state values.
  4. While waiting, show a loading state and disable the submit button to prevent duplicate submissions.
  5. On success (success=true), show confirmation content using message and nextStepsText and store donationPledgeId in state. Optionally, the form fields can be locked or replaced with a summary view.
  6. On failure (success=false or error), display an error message and allow the user to try again.

Impact information navigation interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks the “Learn how your gift helps” control within the impact overview component.
- Behaviour: Navigate to about.html with a standard link. No additional parameters are required, but the implementation may include them if needed in the future.

Suggested amount selection interaction (if used by the UI):
- Trigger: User clicks one of the suggested amount options populated from suggestedAmounts.
- Behaviour: Update the amount input with the selected numeric value and set the internal amount state accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s primary state is driven by URL parameters and API responses.

URL parameters:
- fundId (string, optional):
  - On page load, read fundId from the URL query string.
  - After getDonationPageContent returns, compare the parameter value to the fundId of each active fund in the funds array.
  - If there is an active matching fund, set the initial selected fund in state to that fund’s fundId.
  - If there is no match, fall back to a default selection (e.g., the first active fund in the sorted funds list) without altering the URL.

- amount (number, optional):
  - On page load, read amount from the URL query string and parse it as a number.
  - If the parsed value is a valid positive number, set it as the initial amount state and prefill the amount input.
  - If it is missing or invalid, leave the amount state empty or at a neutral default; no further action is taken and the URL is not modified.

API-driven state:
- On initialization, the page calls getDonationPageContent.
- The response impactOverview is stored in state and rendered in the impact overview component.
- defaultGiftType is stored and used to initialize the giftType state (e.g., one_time or recurring) unless the user changes it.
- suggestedAmounts is stored to drive suggested amount UI elements; user selection of one of these values directly updates the amount state.
- funds is stored and used to populate the fund selector; only active funds (isActive=true) are offered to the user. The list order uses displayOrder or the server-provided ordering.

Form state and submission:
- Form fields (giftType, fundId, amount, dedicationType, honoreeName, donorName, donorPhone, donorEmail, preferredContactMethod) are kept in client state as the user interacts.
- When the user submits, the current state values are validated and then sent to submitDonationPledge as parameters.
- The API response (success, donationPledgeId, message, nextStepsText) is stored in state and drives the display of a confirmation or error area.

State transitions do not modify URL parameters after initial read; the URL only influences initial defaults. All subsequent changes are kept in in-memory page state. The page uses only the getDonationPageContent and submitDonationPledge interfaces for data, and all fund-related identifiers are taken from these responses rather than hardcoded.

components.functionality: Initializes the donation page by reading URL parameters, calling getDonationPageContent, and seeding child components with the resulting data and initial state.
Displays the impactOverview text returned from getDonationPageContent and provides a navigation control to learn more about how donations are used.
Lets the user select between available gift types (e.g., one-time or recurring). Initializes its value from defaultGiftType returned by getDonationPageContent.
Shows a dropdown or equivalent control listing all active funds from getDonationPageContent.funds and allows the user to choose which fund to support.
Captures the donation amount. Initializes from the URL amount parameter (if valid). Supports updates from user typing or from suggested amount selections.
Displays clickable options based on suggestedAmounts from getDonationPageContent, allowing the user to quickly set the donation amount.
Lets the user choose a dedication type (none, in honor of, in memory of) and, when applicable, enter an honoree name that will be stored with the pledge.
Collects donorName, donorPhone, donorEmail, and preferredContactMethod for follow-up and confirmation of the pledge.
Coordinates the various form sections (gift type, fund, amount, dedication, and contact information), handles validation, and submits the pledge via submitDonationPledge.
Shows the result of the pledge submission using the success flag, message, nextStepsText, and donationPledgeId returned from submitDonationPledge.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same header + full-width hero structure as the mockup. The header (logo left, navigation right/center) sits on white, spanning full width. Below it, the hero is a full-width slate-blue band taking most of the initial viewport, with the ink-cloud illustration centered horizontally and the page title (e.g., “Support Our Work”) centered inside the cloud on two lines. Directly beneath the hero, the main donate content is arranged in a single, linear top-to-bottom column within a centered max-width container (about 1100–1200px). Inside this column, the order is: (1) impact-overview-panel as a short introductory section, (2) submission-feedback-panel (only visible after interactions), (3) donation-pledge-form containing the gift-type-toggle, fund-selector, amount-input + suggested-amounts-list, dedication-section, and donor-contact-form. All components sit on white or very light backgrounds with clear separation between major sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same header + full-width hero structure as the mockup. The header (logo left, navigation right/center) sits on white, spanning full width. Below it, the hero is a full-width slate-blue band taking most of the initial viewport, with the ink-cloud illustration centered horizontally and the page title (e.g., “Support Our Work”) centered inside the cloud on two lines. Directly beneath the hero, the main donate content is arranged in a single, linear top-to-bottom column within a centered max-width container (about 1100–1200px). Inside this column, the order is: (1) impact-overview-panel as a short introductory section, (2) submission-feedback-panel (only visible after interactions), (3) donation-pledge-form containing the gift-type-toggle, fund-selector, amount-input + suggested-amounts-list, dedication-section, and donor-contact-form. All components sit on white or very light backgrounds with clear separation between major sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height and the hero band occupies the remaining 80–85%, mirroring the mockup. Below the hero, the donation content is placed in one primary band that stretches downward as needed. Within this band, the impact-overview-panel occupies the top part of the content column (about 20–25% of the first screenful below the hero), the donation-pledge-form occupies the majority of the below-hero area (about 60–70% of the first screenful and more as the form grows), and the submission-feedback-panel uses a slim slice of space above the form (about the height of a small alert). Horizontally, all main components are centered within the max-width container and generally span about 60–75% of the container width on desktop (6–9 of 12 grid columns), leaving generous side margins. Inner form controls typically span the full form width, with occasional two-column groupings on wide screens if needed but keeping a calm, open feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse and spacious. Below the hero, only one or two primary elements appear per vertical segment: first a single impact overview block, then a single feedback strip (when shown), then one self-contained donation form card. Within the form, fields are grouped into clear sections (gift details, dedication, contact), each separated by 32–48px of vertical space. Individual controls have 16–24px internal padding and ample spacing between them (16–24px), so there is never a dense stack of small fields. Text lines are kept short-to-medium in width, and paragraphs are limited, matching the calm, therapeutic tone. The layout avoids multi-column complexity for critical decision points (gift type, fund, amount), opting instead for a reassuring, linear flow.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the logo in the top-left of the header, moves across the navigation to the right, then is drawn downward to the central ink-cloud and hero title. From that strong central hero focal point, the user scrolls down into the content column: first encountering the impact-overview-panel (providing emotional and contextual framing for the gift), then the submission-feedback-panel (if present, to acknowledge their action), and finally the donation-pledge-form as the main interactive focal area. Inside the form, the flow is strictly top-to-bottom: gift-type-toggle at the top, then fund-selector, then amount-input paired visually with the suggested-amounts-list, followed by the dedication-section, and concluding with the donor-contact-form and submit action. This supports a simple F-pattern: quick scan of section headings and labels on the left, with details and inputs aligned beneath, keeping the experience straightforward for busy parents and educators.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar white header and bold slate-blue hero with its centered ink-cloud title, the page transitions to a calm white content band. In this band, a centered column of content sits within a wide but clearly bounded container, leaving generous margins to the left and right. At the top of this column, a single impact-overview-panel card gently introduces why donations matter, using a short paragraph and perhaps a simple stat or quote. Beneath it, a slim submission-feedback-panel appears when relevant, spanning the same central width and visually similar to a soft alert. The main donation-pledge-form sits just below, presented as a spacious, lightly elevated card that occupies roughly two-thirds of the page width, aligned to the center. Within this card, form sections stack vertically with generous spacing: a prominent, centered gift-type-toggle at the top, followed by a full-width fund-selector, then a clearly grouped amount area where the amount-input and suggested-amounts-list are visually tied together. Below this, a dedication-section appears as an optional block with its own mini-heading, followed by a donor-contact-form section that collects personal details in a calm, uncluttered manner. Throughout, the linear top-down flow, large white spaces, and solid color fields echo the therapeutic feel of the hero, ensuring the user always sees just a few elements at a time and can move comfortably from understanding the impact to completing their gift.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-page-loader overlays all other donate-page components whenever initial data is being fetched or a submission is in progress. It sits on top of the content band that begins just below the header (and may also lightly cover the lower portion of the hero, depending on implementation), stretching from the left to the right edges of the viewport and from just under the header down to the bottom of the screen. The overlay uses a semi-opaque light background to slightly dim the underlying page while keeping the overall white and slate-blue palette intact. At the center of the viewport—both horizontally and vertically—a small, clean loading indicator (spinner or simple text) appears within a subtle, rounded rectangle that is about 280–320px wide and 120–160px tall. This central panel respects the spacing system with 24px internal padding on all sides and large breathing room around it, so the loader feels calm and non-intrusive. When visible, the loader visually dominates the page, gently preventing interaction with the underlying donation-pledge-form and related components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay positioned above all other components, covering the main content area from just below the header to the bottom of the viewport; blocks scrolling and interactions while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The impact-overview-panel is the first content element the user encounters after scrolling past the hero. It is centered within the max-width content container and positioned with generous vertical spacing: about 64–80px below the bottom edge of the hero and about 40–56px above the next component. Horizontally, it spans roughly 70% of the container width (about 8–9 of 12 grid columns), leaving comfortable white margins on both sides for a calm, framed feel. The panel itself reads like a soft card: it may have a very light tinted background and 24–32px of internal padding, but it sits flush within the white band so it doesn’t compete with the hero. The content inside is arranged in a simple vertical stack—perhaps a small uppercase label, a short heading, and one or two concise paragraphs or bullet points—centered or left-aligned depending on the design language but always with generous line spacing. This panel’s height is modest (around 25–35% of the first screenful below the hero), ensuring that the user can see the start of the form area just below, keeping the connection between impact and action clear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element participating in normal document flow within the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The gift-type-toggle sits at the very top of the donation-pledge-form card, acting as the first interactive decision the user makes. Within the form’s inner width, it is horizontally centered and spans roughly 40–60% of the form width (about 4–7 grid columns inside the card), giving it the feel of a prominent pill-shaped switch. It is placed about 32–40px below the top edge of the form card, with similar spacing (24–32px) separating it from the fund-selector immediately below. On desktop, the toggle’s labels (e.g., “One-Time” and “Monthly”) are arranged side by side in a single row, with each option occupying about half of the toggle’s width. The toggle has 12–16px of vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding inside each option, making it comfortable and touch-friendly while remaining visually simple. By being the only component in its horizontal band and centered within the form, it creates a clear focal point that echoes the centered hero title, gently guiding the user into the donation configuration flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level control, centered within the form card; resizes to full width (100%) on narrow screens while maintaining vertical stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fund-selector is placed directly below the gift-type-toggle inside the donation-pledge-form, aligned with the form’s main left and right edges. It begins about 24–32px underneath the toggle, maintaining a comfortable vertical gap. The selector spans the full inner width of the form card (approximately 60–70% of the page width, matching the card itself) so it reads as a single, clear choice area. The label (e.g., “Choose a Fund”) sits left-aligned at the top of the control, with 8–12px of space above the dropdown or list input. The input area itself has 16–20px of internal vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding, with enough height to feel robust and easy to tap. On large screens, the control remains a single column, avoiding side-by-side inputs to keep the layout simple and in line with the mockup’s low density. There is about 24–32px of space below the fund-selector before the start of the amount-input section, allowing each decision to feel distinct and manageable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element aligned to the form’s content column; stretches to 100% width of the form card on all screen sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The amount-input is positioned just below the fund-selector within the donation-pledge-form and forms the first part of a visually cohesive ‘gift amount’ block. It is aligned with the left and right edges of the form card and is separated from the fund-selector by about 24–32px of vertical space. On desktop, the amount-input is a full-width input row: the label (e.g., “Donation Amount”) is left-aligned above, with a comfortable 8–12px gap before the numeric input field. The input box itself has 16–20px vertical and 24px horizontal padding, matching other form elements, and sits roughly in the center of this block with plenty of breathing room on both sides. Immediately below the input field, with a modest gap of 12–16px, is the suggested-amounts-list, making it clear that the quick-select suggestions relate directly to this field. This arrangement allows the amount-input to remain the primary element in its vertical slice, with the suggestions reading more like supportive options than competing controls. On smaller screens, the amount-input simply stacks above the suggestions, maintaining the same vertical relationship and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level form field within the form card, stacking vertically with related elements; adapts to full width on all screen sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The suggested-amounts-list sits directly beneath the amount-input, forming a unified ‘gift amount’ section. It begins approximately 12–16px under the bottom edge of the amount-input field, with its left edge aligned to the left edge of the amount-input and the right edge aligned to the form card’s right side. The list is presented as a horizontal row (or two rows if needed) of pill-shaped buttons or chips representing common amounts. On desktop, the chips flow left-to-right, each with 8–12px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, and 8–12px gaps between them. They collectively occupy roughly 70–80% of the form card width; if there are many options, they wrap to a second line but remain anchored to the left, leaving whitespace on the right. Above the list, a subtle label or helper text can be aligned to the left, making the connection to the amount-input obvious. Vertical spacing of about 24–32px separates this block from the dedication-section below, ensuring that the quick-select suggestions feel like part of the amount decision and not crowded against the next form area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline-block/list behavior within the form card; items wrap naturally to additional lines as needed while maintaining left alignment and vertical stacking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dedication-section appears below the gift amount area inside the donation-pledge-form, separated from the suggested-amounts-list by a generous 32–40px vertical gap to signal a new conceptual section. It spans the full inner width of the form card and is visually grouped as a fieldset-like block: a small, left-aligned section heading (e.g., “Dedicate this gift”) at the top, possibly in uppercase or slightly heavier weight, with 8–12px of space before any explanation text or toggle that reveals the detailed dedication fields. The main fields (such as honoree name, message, or recipient email) are stacked vertically with 16–24px gaps between them, maintaining the low-density look. If the dedication is optional or collapsible, the collapsed state shows only the heading and a simple control (like a checkbox or link) aligned within the same width; when expanded, the section grows downward but always stays within the same centered column. There is ample space (32–40px) below the bottom of this section before the donor-contact-form begins, creating clear visual separation and helping users feel that each step is manageable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block section within the form card; may have an internal collapsible behavior but occupies its own vertical slice in the normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donor-contact-form occupies the lower portion of the donation-pledge-form card and is the final major section before submission. It starts about 32–40px below the dedication-section (or directly below the amount section if dedication is not used), spanning the full inner width of the form card. At its top, a clear, left-aligned section heading (e.g., “Your Details”) introduces the fields. On desktop, the contact fields can be lightly grouped into one or two columns while maintaining a spacious feel: for example, first and last name could share a row, each occupying about half the width (with a 16–24px gap between them), while email and phone each take a full-width row below. Address fields might be similarly grouped but should never exceed two columns per row to avoid clutter. Each input has 16–20px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing from the next, and the whole contact section leaves a comfortable margin (32–40px) above the form’s submit button area at the very bottom. On mobile, all fields stack to a single column, aligning with the general linear flow and preserving the calm, readable layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level form section within the form card; may internally arrange some fields in two-column rows on larger screens but behaves as a single vertical section in the overall page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The donation-pledge-form is the central interactive focal point of the donate page and appears below the impact-overview-panel (and below the submission-feedback-panel when that is visible). It is positioned in the middle of the page within the max-width container, with its outer edges aligning to roughly 60–70% of the page width (about 7–9 of 12 grid columns) and centered horizontally. Vertically, it begins around 40–56px below the bottom of the preceding component, ensuring generous breathing room. The form is presented as a cohesive card or panel with a very subtle elevation or border and 32–40px of internal padding on all sides. The internal layout is strictly vertical: starting at the top with an optional small title or instruction, then the gift-type-toggle, followed by the fund-selector, then the amount-input and suggested-amounts-list grouped together, then the dedication-section, and finally the donor-contact-form and submit controls. Each internal section is separated by 24–40px of vertical space, and there is a clear, larger gap (32–48px) between the main form content and the final submit button row, making the end of the process easy to see. The overall height of the form extends beyond a single screen on desktop, but the linear, low-density structure means users see one clear cluster of decisions at a time as they scroll. The form aligns visually with the impact panel above, reinforcing a single centered column of content that echoes the centered ink-cloud hero above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level form card within the main content column; scrolls with the page and remains centered within the max-width container on all screen sizes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-feedback-panel is positioned between the impact-overview-panel and the donation-pledge-form, occupying the same central column and width as the form card. When visible (e.g., after a successful or failed submission), it appears about 24–32px below the impact-overview-panel and about 24–32px above the top edge of the donation-pledge-form. Horizontally, it spans roughly the same 60–70% width as the form, centered within the page. The panel looks like a slim, calm alert bar or card with a slightly tinted background (e.g., soft green or amber, depending on status) and 16–24px internal padding, with text aligned left for easy scanning. Its height is modest—roughly one to three lines of text plus padding—so it does not compete with the hero or form for attention but is still immediately noticeable when the user returns to this section after an action. When not needed, the panel collapses entirely, and the donation-pledge-form moves up, maintaining the same centered column alignment with the impact-overview-panel above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in the normal document flow; appears and disappears without affecting the alignment or width of surrounding components, only adjusting the vertical spacing between them.

name: About & Contact
title: About & Contact
description: Page for viewing the organization’s background, mission, philosophy, policies, accessibility information, and for submitting a general contact inquiry.

core_features: Load and display dynamic About content (mission, history, philosophy) using getAboutPageContent.
Load and display dynamic policy content (privacy policy, terms, accessibility) using getAboutPageContent.
Display general contact information (main phone, main email, mailing address) using getAboutPageContent.contactInfo.
Provide in-page navigation and anchors for key sections, including Privacy Policy, Terms, and Accessibility (e.g., about.html#privacy_policy).
Provide a general contact form bound to submitGeneralContactRequest with fields for name, email, phone, topic, and message.
Handle form submission states (idle, submitting, success, error) and show API feedback (success message and referenceId) from submitGeneralContactRequest.
Support URL-driven state for focusing sections and pre-filling contact form fields (e.g., section, contact_topic, contact_name, contact_email, contact_status, contact_ref).
Provide navigation links to Services, Our Team, Locations, and Donate pages from within the About & Contact content.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View general About information
1) User navigates to about.html (via footer navigation or direct link).
2) On page initialization, the page calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
3) Once the response is received, the page populates the mission, history, and philosophy sections with the returned content.
4) The in-page section navigation is generated/activated so the user can jump to Mission, History, Philosophy, Policies, Accessibility, and Contact.
5) User scrolls or uses in-page links to read content; no additional API calls are required for subsequent sections.

Workflow 2: View a specific policy section via URL or anchor
1) User navigates to about.html with either a URL hash (e.g., #privacy_policy, #terms, #accessibility) or a query parameter (e.g., ?section=privacy_policy).
2) On page initialization, the page calls getAboutPageContent.
3) After content is rendered, the page checks the URL parameter section and/or the hash fragment.
4) If a matching section is found, the page scrolls/focuses to that section and optionally highlights it.
5) User can still use in-page navigation to move to other sections without additional API calls.

Workflow 3: View contact information
1) User navigates to about.html (any variant of URL).
2) getAboutPageContent is called and returns contactInfo (mainPhone, mainEmail, mailingAddress).
3) The Contact Information area is populated with the returned values; click actions on phone/email (e.g., tel: and mailto:) are enabled without extra API calls.
4) User may copy or use these details to contact the organization outside the website.

Workflow 4: Submit a general contact inquiry
1) User navigates to about.html, optionally with query parameters such as contact_topic, contact_name, or contact_email.
2) The page calls getAboutPageContent to render all About and policy content (this is independent of the form).
3) The General Contact Form component reads URL parameters and pre-fills corresponding fields (e.g., topic, name, email) if present; otherwise fields remain empty.
4) User completes required fields (name, email) and optional fields (phone, topic, message).
5) On form submit, client-side validation runs (e.g., required fields are non-empty, email format check). If validation fails, errors are shown and submit is blocked.
6) If validation passes, the page calls submitGeneralContactRequest with the form’s name, email, phone, topic, and message values.
7) While waiting for the response, the form enters a submitting state (e.g., submit button disabled) but does not navigate away.
8) On success (success=true), the component displays a confirmation message including referenceId and server message, and updates the URL to include contact_status=success and contact_ref=<referenceId> so the success state can be preserved on refresh or share.
9) On error (success=false or network failure), the component displays an error message and allows the user to correct the form and resubmit, without clearing user input unnecessarily.

Workflow 5: Restore contact form state from URL after redirect or refresh
1) User lands on about.html with query parameters such as contact_status=success and contact_ref set from a prior submission, or from an external redirect.
2) getAboutPageContent is called to load and display About and policy content.
3) The contact form/status component reads URL parameters.
4) If contact_status=success is present, the component shows a success banner including the reference ID from contact_ref, and the form fields start in a cleared, idle state.
5) If contact_status=error is present (optional behavior), the component shows an error banner and keeps any pre-fill parameters in the form.
6) User can submit a new inquiry or ignore the status and use other parts of the page.

Workflow 6: Navigate from About & Contact to other site sections
1) User reads About content and sees contextual links such as “View Our Therapy Services”, “Meet Our Team”, “Find a Clinic”, and “Support Our Work”.
2) On click of each link, the browser navigates directly to the corresponding page: services.html, therapists.html, locations.html, or donate.html.
3) No additional API calls are made from About & Contact for these navigation actions.
interactions: Click in-page section navigation items (e.g., Mission, History, Philosophy, Privacy Policy, Terms, Accessibility, Contact) to scroll to and focus corresponding sections.
Use URL hash anchors (e.g., #privacy_policy, #terms, #accessibility) to jump directly to policy and accessibility sections after content is loaded.
Use query parameter section (e.g., ?section=privacy_policy) to auto-scroll and highlight specific sections after getAboutPageContent has populated the page.
View dynamically loaded mission, historyHtml, and philosophyHtml content, rendered as HTML from the getAboutPageContent response.
View dynamically loaded privacyPolicyHtml, termsHtml, and accessibilityHtml content, rendered as HTML into their respective sections.
Copy or interact with dynamically loaded contactInfo fields: click on mainPhone (e.g., tel: link), mainEmail (e.g., mailto: link), and view mailingAddress.
Fill out the General Contact Form fields (name, email, phone, topic, message) with manual input or with initial values pre-filled from URL parameters.
Submit the General Contact Form via a submit button, which triggers client-side validation and then calls submitGeneralContactRequest.
Observe the form entering a submitting state (e.g., disabling inputs or submit button) until the submitGeneralContactRequest call resolves.
On successful submitGeneralContactRequest, view a confirmation banner showing referenceId and message from the API, and optionally see the URL updated with contact_status=success and contact_ref.
On failed submitGeneralContactRequest (API error or network error), view an error message, keep the form data, and attempt resubmission after corrections.
Click contextual call-to-action links within the About & Contact content to navigate to services.html, therapists.html, locations.html, or donate.html.

Refresh the page or access about.html with pre-defined URL parameters (e.g., contact_topic, contact_name, contact_email, contact_status, contact_ref) to have the contact form and status areas initialized accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: This page is driven by the getAboutPageContent data and by URL parameters and hash fragments. On every load, the page calls getAboutPageContent to retrieve mission, historyHtml, philosophyHtml, privacyPolicyHtml, termsHtml, accessibilityHtml, and contactInfo. Once the response is received, the sections are rendered. URL query parameters control additional state: section defines which section should be auto-scrolled to and highlighted (e.g., section=privacy_policy; combined with anchors like #privacy_policy); contact_topic, contact_name, contact_email, and contact_phone (if present) pre-fill the corresponding General Contact Form fields; contact_status and contact_ref define whether a success or error state banner should be shown above the form and which reference ID to display. Hash fragments (#privacy_policy, #terms, #accessibility, etc.) are listened for after content rendering and cause scrolling/focus to the matching section IDs. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; all shareable or restorable state is represented in the URL.

components.functionality: Initialize the About & Contact page by calling getAboutPageContent and distributing the returned data (mission, historyHtml, philosophyHtml, privacyPolicyHtml, termsHtml, accessibilityHtml, contactInfo) to dependent components.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getAboutPageContent with no parameters when about.html is loaded.
onAPISuccess: Store the returned content in page-level state and notify dependent components to render.
onAPIError: Set an error state that can be read by content sections to show a generic loading error message.

components.functionality: Provide in-page navigation to main sections (Mission, History, Philosophy, Privacy Policy, Terms, Accessibility, Contact) and synchronize with URL parameters and hash fragments.
data_binding: Page state: available section IDs (e.g., mission, history, philosophy, privacy_policy, terms, accessibility, contact)
event_handlers: onInit: Read the section query parameter and hash fragment and request scroll/focus to the corresponding section after content is loaded.
onClickNavItem: Update the URL hash and/or section query parameter for the chosen section and trigger scrolling to that section.
onHashChange: When the hash changes (e.g., user uses browser back/forward), scroll to the corresponding section.
onContentLoaded: After about-data-loader completes, apply any pending auto-scroll based on URL state.

components.functionality: Render the mission, history, and philosophy content of the organization using HTML strings from getAboutPageContent.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Re-render the sections whenever new mission, historyHtml, or philosophyHtml content is received from about-data-loader.
onAnchorFocus: Respond to navigation requests (e.g., from section-navigation) to ensure the correct subsection is scrolled into view.

components.functionality: Render policy-related content for Privacy Policy, Terms, and Accessibility using HTML strings from getAboutPageContent, and expose section anchors (e.g., #privacy_policy, #terms, #accessibility).
event_handlers: onDataChange: Render or update privacy, terms, and accessibility HTML content when data from about-data-loader changes.
onAnchorFocus: When invoked with a section identifier (privacy_policy, terms, accessibility), scroll that section into view and optionally apply a highlight.
onHashChange: If the URL hash matches one of the policy section IDs (e.g., #privacy_policy), ensure the matching section is focused.

components.functionality: Display the organization’s general contact information, including main phone, main email, and mailing address, retrieved from getAboutPageContent.contactInfo.
event_handlers: onDataChange: Render or update the phone, email, and mailing address whenever contactInfo changes.
onClickPhone: Optionally initiate a phone call by navigating to a tel: link using contactInfo.mainPhone.
onClickEmail: Optionally initiate an email by navigating to a mailto: link using contactInfo.mainEmail.

components.functionality: Provide an input form for users to submit general contact or inquiry messages, and send the data to submitGeneralContactRequest.
data_binding: Form fields: name (string), email (string), phone (string), topic (string), message (string)
URL query parameters: contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, contact_topic (for pre-fill)
Page state: form status (idle, submitting, success, error), validation errors
event_handlers: onInit: Read contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and contact_topic from URL parameters and initialize the corresponding form fields, if present.
onChangeField: Update local form state when the user types into the name, email, phone, topic, or message fields.
onValidate: Before submission, validate required fields (e.g., name, email) and email format; set validation errors in state if invalid.
onSubmit: If validation passes, call submitGeneralContactRequest with the current form values and set form status to submitting.

onAPISuccess: When submitGeneralContactRequest resolves with success=true, update form status to success, store referenceId and server message, clear form fields as appropriate, and request URL update to include contact_status=success and contact_ref=referenceId.
onAPIError: When submitGeneralContactRequest fails (success=false response or network error), update form status to error, preserve user input, and attach an error message for display.
onURLParamChange: If the URL changes (e.g., user navigates back/forward with different contact_* values), optionally refresh pre-filled form fields when the form is in idle state.

components.functionality: Display success or error messages related to the general contact form, driven by both the latest submitGeneralContactRequest response and URL parameters.
event_handlers: onInit: Read contact_status and contact_ref from URL parameters; if contact_status=success, display a success banner including the reference ID; if contact_status=error, display an error banner.
onFormAPISuccess: When the general-contact-form reports a successful submission, display a success banner using the API response message and referenceId.
onFormAPIError: When the general-contact-form reports an error, display an error banner with a generic or API-provided message.
onDismiss: Hide the banner when the user closes it; optionally update in-page state without changing the URL.

components.functionality: Provide navigation links from the About & Contact page to core sections of the site: Services, Our Team, Locations, and Donate.
data_binding: Static route targets: services.html, therapists.html, locations.html, donate.html
event_handlers: onClickViewServices: Navigate browser to services.html when the user clicks the “View Our Therapy Services” link.
onClickMeetTeam: Navigate browser to therapists.html when the user clicks the “Meet Our Team” link.
onClickFindClinic: Navigate browser to locations.html when the user clicks the “Find a Clinic” link.
onClickSupportWork: Navigate browser to donate.html when the user clicks the “Support Our Work” link.

components.functionality: Centralize reading and writing of URL parameters and hash fragments that affect the About & Contact page state, without using localStorage.
data_binding: URL query parameters: section, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone, contact_topic, contact_status, contact_ref
event_handlers: onInit: Parse current URL parameters and hash fragment; provide initial state to section-navigation and general-contact-form components.
onSectionChange: When a section-navigation item is clicked, update the URL hash and/or section parameter without reloading the page.
onContactSubmitSuccess: After successful submitGeneralContactRequest, update the URL query parameters (e.g., set contact_status=success and contact_ref) using history.pushState or equivalent.

onHistoryChange: When the user navigates using browser back/forward, re-parse URL state and notify components (e.g., section-navigation, general-contact-form, contact-status-banner) so they can update accordingly.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column desktop grid with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) inside full-width color bands.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same top-to-bottom sequencing as the mockup: (1) global site header (logo left, main nav right, white background), (2) a full-width hero band with a slate-blue background and a centered ink-cloud title for this About & Contact page, (3) directly beneath the hero, a full-width white band containing a centered, horizontal section-navigation for the About/Contact subsections, then (4) linear stacked content bands: mission/history/philosophy, policies, and finally a combined contact band housing the contact info panel, contact status banner, and contact form side by side. The page ends with a related-links navigation band. Data-loading and URL state components sit above or behind this flow, controlling behavior rather than adding visible blocks.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same top-to-bottom sequencing as the mockup: (1) global site header (logo left, main nav right, white background), (2) a full-width hero band with a slate-blue background and a centered ink-cloud title for this About & Contact page, (3) directly beneath the hero, a full-width white band containing a centered, horizontal section-navigation for the About/Contact subsections, then (4) linear stacked content bands: mission/history/philosophy, policies, and finally a combined contact band housing the contact info panel, contact status banner, and contact form side by side. The page ends with a related-links navigation band. Data-loading and URL state components sit above or behind this flow, controlling behavior rather than adding visible blocks.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a generous hero that fills most of the first viewport beneath the header (roughly 70–80% of the viewport height), with a large centered ink-cloud focal area. Below the hero, each major content band (mission/philosophy, policies, contact, related links) spans the full browser width but constrains content to a centered 1100–1200px container. Within each band, vertical padding is generous—about 72–96px top and bottom—to match the calm, airy feel. In the contact band, space is divided into a 1/3 width left column for the contact-info panel and a 2/3 width right column for the contact form and status banner, following a 12-column grid (4 columns vs 8 columns).
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep information density low, mirroring the mockup: few elements per band, clear headings, and lots of negative space. Text blocks in mission/history/philosophy and policies should be in a single main column, with comfortable line lengths (about 60–70 characters) and generous spacing between headings, paragraphs, and subsections (16–32px). The contact form uses standard field spacing (16–24px between fields) with sufficient padding inside inputs and buttons (16–24px) to preserve the therapeutic, unhurried feeling.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Retain the mockup’s visual flow: the eye starts at the top-left logo, moves across the main header navigation, then is drawn down into the large, centered ink-cloud hero title. From that central focal point, the user’s gaze continues downward to the centered section-navigation, which acts as a gateway into the About & Contact subsections. The vertical flow then moves linearly: mission/history/philosophy, then policies, then the contact area (first reading the left-hand contact info, then moving naturally to the right-hand contact form). The page concludes with related links centered at the bottom, offering gentle onward paths without clutter. Sticky behavior of the section-navigation can support a secondary F-pattern as the user scrolls, always providing a clear orientation to where they are on the page.

layout.overall_description: The About & Contact page sits within a calm, modern frame: a white header bar at the very top carries the logo on the left and a simple horizontal navigation on the right, spanning the full width of the viewport but visually anchored by a centered 1100–1200px content container. Immediately beneath, a full-width slate-blue hero band fills most of the first screen; in its center, an organic dark ink-cloud shape floats with the page title set in two lines of clear typography, creating a strong, singular focal point. Below the ink-cloud, with generous breathing room, a white band introduces a centered horizontal section-navigation that labels the major parts of the page (Mission & Philosophy, Policies, Contact), echoing the all-caps ‘YOUR SPACE THEATRE’ feel from the mockup. As you scroll down, the layout resolves into a series of full-width horizontal bands: a white band for mission/history/philosophy content, a very light tinted band for policies, and a slightly distinct but still soft band for the contact area. Within each band, content is constrained to the central grid, with plenty of vertical padding and a single main text column to maintain low visual density. In the contact band, the layout splits into two columns—contact details on the left, form on the right—with the contact status banner sitting just above the form fields. The page tapers off with a simple, centered related-links band at the bottom. Throughout, the design leverages clean typography, large margins, and a consistent grid to create a therapeutic, reassuring experience where each section is clearly separated yet visually connected in a calm vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a full-viewport overlay when active, sitting above all other page content, including the header and hero. It covers the entire browser window with a semi-transparent white or very light slate veil to preserve the calm aesthetic while dimming the underlying layout. At the exact center of the viewport, a simple, unobtrusive loading indicator (e.g., a spinner and short label) is placed, aligned both vertically and horizontally. When inactive, this component occupies no visible space and does not affect the spacing of the header, hero, or content bands.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport, covering 100% width and height when loading; otherwise non-rendered or visually hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation sits directly below the hero band, occupying a full-width horizontal band on a white background. Inside this band, a centered content container (matching the main 1100–1200px width) holds the navigation menu, which is horizontally centered on the page and constrained to roughly 60–70% of the container width. The nav items appear in a single row, evenly spaced or left-centered in that row, with generous horizontal padding (16–24px) around each label and ample vertical padding (24–32px) above and below the row, echoing the spacious feel of the mockup’s ‘YOUR SPACE THEATRE’ heading. There is clear vertical separation (about 32–48px) between the bottom of the hero and the top of this band, and another 48–64px gap below it before the mission/history/philosophy section starts, so it reads as a distinct but connected control strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block below the hero, but becomes sticky to the top of the viewport (just under the main site header) once scrolled into place, maintaining 100% width and preserving its centered content container to provide persistent in-page navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section forms the first major content band beneath the section-navigation, on a white background that visually continues the main page canvas. It spans the full browser width but constrains content to the central 1100–1200px container. Within the container, the main text column occupies roughly 8 out of 12 grid columns (about 66% of the container width) and is centered horizontally, leaving generous margins on both sides. Section headings (e.g., ‘Our Mission’, ‘History’, ‘Philosophy’) are stacked vertically, with 24–32px spacing between each heading and its body text, and 40–56px between thematic subsections, keeping the overall feel spacious. The band has about 80–96px of padding at the top (from the bottom of the section-navigation) and a similar amount at the bottom before the policies band begins, creating a calm, clearly delineated slice of content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section participating in the main vertical scroll flow, aligned to the central grid with no special fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy sections appear as the next full-width horizontal band directly beneath the mission/history/philosophy section, separated by a generous vertical gap of about 64–80px. This band uses a very light, neutral or slightly tinted background to subtly distinguish it from the white mission section while maintaining the overall calm tone. Inside the central 1100–1200px container, policies are laid out in a single main column aligned left, again spanning roughly 8 of the 12 grid columns, leaving comfortable left and right margins. Each policy heading is set with clear separation (24–32px from the preceding content), and different policy categories are given 40–56px vertical spacing to avoid crowding. The band includes 80–96px of top and bottom padding so that the text feels like it is floating comfortably within a broad field of color, before transitioning to the contact area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section, full-width band with constrained central content, scrolling naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info panel lives within a dedicated contact band that sits directly below the policies band, with a vertical gap of about 64–80px between them. The contact band spans the full width and may use a very soft contrasting background (e.g., a pale slate or off-white) to signal a functional area. Inside the 1100–1200px container, the layout splits into two columns: the contact-info panel occupies the left column, roughly 4 of 12 grid columns (about one-third of the container width), aligned flush left. The panel itself is a vertical block starting near the top of the contact band, with generous internal padding (24–32px) and 16–24px spacing between lines of contact information (address, phone, email, opening hours), possibly presented in a card-like box with a subtle border or shadow. The right edge of this panel lines up with the gutter between the 4th and 5th grid columns, creating a clear vertical alignment with the accompanying contact form to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static left-column block within the contact band; on smaller screens, it stacks above the contact form, but on desktop it remains a fixed-width column occupying roughly one-third of the container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The general contact form shares the same contact band as the contact-info panel and is positioned to the right of it. Within the central container, the form column occupies approximately 8 of 12 grid columns (about two-thirds of the width), starting at the grid line directly to the right of the contact-info panel. The form’s top aligns vertically with the top of the contact-info panel, with a consistent 24–32px gutter separating the two columns. At the top of this column, just inside the form area, sits the contact status banner (when active), followed by the form fields arranged in a single, vertical stack. Each field spans the full width of the form column, with 16–24px spacing between fields and 24–32px padding above and below the submit button, ensuring comfortable click targets and a relaxed rhythm. The bottom of the form aligns roughly with or slightly below the bottom of the contact-info panel, maintaining a balanced visual block before the related-links band below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static right-column block within the contact band; it stacks below the contact-info panel on smaller screens but remains a wider right-side column on desktop.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-status banner is a horizontal strip that appears inside the right-hand contact column, directly above the first form field of the general contact form. It spans the full width of the form column (approximately 8 of 12 grid columns), with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, giving it a compact but clearly legible presence. There is a small gap (8–16px) between the bottom of this banner and the top of the form fields, so it reads as part of the form stack rather than a separate page section. When inactive, it collapses entirely, allowing the form fields to shift up, while preserving the overall vertical spacing within the contact band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the form column, appearing and disappearing without changing the layout of other sections; it scrolls with the contact form as part of the main page flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-links navigation is the final major band at the bottom of the page, positioned directly beneath the contact band with a vertical gap of about 64–80px. It spans the full width on a white or very light background, visually echoing the simplicity of the header and mission section. Inside the central container, the links are centered horizontally and occupy roughly 60–70% of the container width, either as a row of evenly spaced buttons or a tidy grid of 2–3 items per row, with 24–32px spacing between items. Vertical padding of about 64–80px top and bottom maintains the airy feel, and there is generous space (24–32px) above any heading label that introduces this block. The band ends with extra breathing room at the bottom of the page so the content does not feel abruptly cut off.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section at the end of the scroll flow, providing onward navigation without any sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state controller has no direct visual representation; it conceptually sits behind the main content stack and synchronizes the browser URL and scroll position with the user’s location within the About & Contact page. It maps URL fragments or query parameters to the major sections—mission/history/philosophy, policies, and contact—and ensures that when a user clicks an item in the section-navigation, the page smoothly scrolls to the corresponding band, and the relevant state is reflected in the URL. It also tracks the currently visible section as the user scrolls, informing which item in the section-navigation appears active, all without occupying any physical space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral layer; no rendered box or dimensions. It attaches logically to the scroll container for the main page and the section-navigation component to coordinate state.
